William Alexander Budding, 86, of Mystic, passed away December 15, 2007 at Mary Elizabeth Nursing Home after a brief illness. Bill graduated in 1942 from the US Naval Academy in Annapolis in the wartime accelerated class of 1943.  During World War II he served aboard the submarine, Seahorse, in various roles including Executive Officer.  He received the Silver Star for Gallantry and Intrepidity in Action.  After the war he earned a masters degree in naval engineering from the Massachusetts Institute of Technology.  He retired in 1968 as a Captain in Portsmouth, VA after serving 26 years in the Navy.  During his Naval career he served as Production Control Officer at Norfolk Naval Shipyard in Portsmouth, VA and Shipbuilding and Control Superintendent at Mare Island Naval Shipyard, focusing on the construction and repair of naval vessels primarily submarines.  After his retirement from the navy in 1968, he took a 3-day break and began his second career at Electric Boat in Groton as a naval architect retiring in 1986 as the manager of naval architecture.  Bill's hobbies were golf and sailing.  He raced his ensign, Aries, in southeastern CT from the mid-70's through the early 1980s when he purchased his cruising boat, a Nonsuch named Seahorse.  He enjoyed cruising with different clubs in the local area until 1995.  Bill is a member of Ram Island Yacht Club, Nauyaug Cruising Club and Masons Island Yacht Club. Bill participated at Mystic Seaport and volunteered since 1986 with the Gung Ho squad.  He is also a member of the Mystic Seaport Pilots. Bill is survived by his current wife of 5 years, Molly Griswold Budding.  He was married to Viola Stegman Budding for 57 years.  For the past 5 years, Bill has wintered in Redlands, California with Molly and her family. He is also survived by his son, Al Budding of Forida, grandson, Thomas Budding of Florida, daughter, Anne Budding Orzech of Niantic and their spouses, Jennifer Beach and John Orzech.  His brothers, Claude Budding and Henry Budding, and his sister Norma Mohr and numerous nieces and nephews also survive him.  His extended family of Molly Brenner of California, Kath Chesterman of England, Jimmy Griswold of California, and Gayle Kildebick of California as well as numerous grandchildren and great grandchildren survive him. A Memorial Service will be conducted at the Mystic Congregational Church on Friday at 2pm. Private burial will precede at Elm Grove Cemetery. The Dinoto Funeral Home, 17 Pearl Street, Historic downtown Mystic is assisting the family. Donations in his memory may be made to the Mystic Seaport.  To share a message of remembrance or condolence with his family, please clcik the link below.

WORCESTER___Terese Gedvilas died peacefully on December 14, 2007 at the age of 92. She lived at 10 Dell Avenue in Worcester for 50 years. She is survived by her beloved children, Vito Gedvilas, Jonas Gedvilas, Birute Vazquez who lived with her and Lilia Gedvilas Chavez of Worcester. Her daughter Ruta died as an infant in Germany. Her devoted son-in-law Gabriel Vazquez lives in Worcester.Terese was the daughter of Juozas Lenkas and Ursule Saukaityte and was born in Liubavas, Lithuania. Her mother died shortly after her birth and she was raised by her stepmother Ona. During World War II she was separated from her family and displaced from her homeland. Terese survived her siblings, Ona, Ada, Leonija, Albinas and Petras. The Soviet era prevented her from seeing her family. After Independence her sister Ada came for a yearlong visit in 1988. Terese’s great niece Inga Valaitis accompanied Ada and stayed on to live with the Gedvilas family for several years. Inga and her husband Eric Miller reside in Fairfield, Connecticut.Terese lived in a refugee resettlement camp in Seligendstat, Germany where she met her husband of 55 years John Gedvilas who passed away in 2001. Terese and John came to the USA in 1950 with their two older children and settled down in Worcester where they had two more children. Terese was completely devoted to the care and nurturing of her family. After retiring from a lifelong career as a seamstress she helped to raise her four grandsons who grew up in Worcester and spent every afternoon with their grandparents.Terese leaves five grandsons, Gabriel, Stephen, Michael and Christopher Vazquez and Romas Gedvilas. Gabriel and Christopher live in Worcester; Romas is currently in the U.S. Army at Ft. Hood, Texas. Stephen and his wife Lisa Haney live in New York City as does Michael and his wife Karen Tarbell and their two boys William and Daniel.Terese’s entire world revolved around her family’s well-being and the care of her home and garden. She also spent many summers at the family cottage at Nantasket Beach. Over the past two years Terese expanded her world to include Summit Elder Care. She appreciated the staff that transported her daily and those who provided her healthcare and socialization.Terese was a member of St. Casimir Parish in Worcester.  Her funeral Mass will be celebrated Thursday December 20 at 10:00 a.m. in St. Casimir Churh, 41 Providence Street. Burial will be in Notre Dame Cemetery. Calling hours are Wednesday from 5 to 7 p.m. in HENRY-DIRSA FUNERAL HOME, 33 Ward Street, Worcester. Flowers may be sent or contributions in her memory may be made to St. Casimir Church, 41 Providence Street, Worcester, MA., 01604.

Mrs. Marjorie A. (Richards) Gilbert, 96, formerly of 284 Denmark Street, Berlin, NH passed away on Friday December 14, 2007 in Bedford, NH. Gilbert of Windham, NH; 1 granddaughter Stephanie Braun of Englewood Cliffs, NJ; 1 grandson Stephen Berry of Jacksonville, FL and a large extended family. She was predeceased by her husband Harry E. Gilbert who died on March 28, 1969. Mrs. Gilbert was very active in her community when her children were young and served as President of PTA, 4H and member of the Berlin School Board. She was an avid artist for 30 years and was still active several times a week with her beloved art group prior to her illness. Interment will be in the Russian City Cemetery. Relatives and friends may call at the funeral home on Wednesday morning from 9:00 to 11:00 AM prior to the service.

Kardell, of Plymouth, formerly of East Sandwich, December 13th, Caroline L. (Lewis) 78.  Beloved wife of the late Paul Freeman Kardell.  Mother of Jonathan Kardell of Andover and Jane Linane of Loudon, NH.  Sister of George K. Lewis of Bedford, MA and Richard E. Lewis of Palm Coast FL.  She also leaves 2 granddaughters, Sarah Parker and Tanya Linane.  She was a graduate of Cushing Academy and Lawrence University with a major in history. Caroline was the Historian General for the General Society of Mayflower Descendants for 15 years, during which time she was the driving force behind many initiatives including a major expansion of the Society library.  For many years she was the registrar for the Wayside Inn chapter of the DAR.  She was treasurer of the Wayland Historical Society, president of the Wayland Women’s club and an active chair member of the Bicentennial Celebrations.  Caroline has been a member of the Board of Assistants, Education Chairman, and State Historian of the Massachusetts Mayflower Society.  She was a member and former volunteer at the New England Historic Genealogical Society. She compiled the Sandwich Vital Records for the Sandwich Town Archives which has been published in a three volume set.  She has assisted with the Townsend Vital Records and has authored several genealogical articles for various quarterly publications. Caroline was a Trustee of the Pilgrim Society, a member of the Antiquarian Society, the Duxbury chapter of the DAR, the Plimoth Plantation and the First Parish in Plymouth. She was the Mayflower Society representative on the Plymouth Economic Planning and Development Committee on Town Celebrations where she chaired the Historic Regiments Parade for the 375th Anniversary Celebrations in Plymouth in 1995. A Memorial Service will be held on Wednesday, December 19th at 1 o’clock at the First Parish Church, Town Square, Plymouth.  Burial will be at a later date.   Donations can be made to Unitarian Universalist, First Parish Church, 19 Town Square, Plymouth, MA 02360  508-747-1606.  Arrangements by the Richard Davis Funeral Homes of Plymouth and Manomet.

Reverend Elmer L. “Kim” KimmellThe Reverend Elmer L.“Kim” Kimmell, passed from this life on December 12, 2007, he was 93.  Affectionately known all of his life by family and friends as “Kim,” he passed with his beloved wife Gail (Chase) Kimmell near his side.Kim was born on F Street in Washington D.C. on February 7, 1914.  He attended District elementary and high schools and served notably as a bat boy for the then Washington Senators as a teen, being a particular favorite of All Star Joe Cronin.  When not playing baseball near the Washington Monument, he and his brothers were youthfully known to pal around with Capital Hill Police, once giving them a run for their money racing around the Capital grounds on a motorcycle, complete with side car.  Kim spoke often of his youth and was particularly proud of his father Harry who was an early staff employee of the FBI working directly with Director J. Edgar Hoover and his mother Grace who was the head custodian of the Library of Congress.  He was predeceased by his parents and brothers, Frank and Charlie. Kim was admitted to Dickinson College in Pennsylvania in 1932 graduating with a Bachelors of Arts Degree in 1936.  He also attended Boston University, obtaining both a second Bachelor’s degree in Sacred Theology and a Master’s Degree in Theology in 1939.  His quest for a Doctoral Degree, begun while serving a Methodist Church in Bourne, Massachusetts, was interrupted by church reassignments and the eventual responsibility of rebuilding the Bell’s Methodist Church in Camp Springs, Maryland.  Although he completed all course work and passed all of his doctoral exams, his commitment to his congregation came before his dissertation.  He often joked that his Doctoral Degree was in ‘bricks and mortar.’Over the course of his career as a Methodist Minister he served congregations in Arbutus, Maryland (1939-1943), Bourne, Massachusetts (1946-1949), Bell’s Church (1949-1955) Camp Springs, Maryland Wilson Memorial Church (1955-1966) in Baltimore, Maryland near Johns Hopkins University and lastly at Chevy Chase United Methodist Church in Chevy Chase, Maryland (1966-1980).  One of his more notable parishioners at Chevy Chase was then Vice President Hubert Humprhey.Kim served his country from 1943-1946 in live action as a Navy Chaplin in the Pacific Theater and continued with the Naval Reserve until 1980 retiring with the rank of Commander.Kim and Gail devoted themselves to raising three children, Glenn Alden Kimmell (Lauren) of Fairfax, Virginia, Bradford Lee Kimmell (Dian) of Brinklow, Maryland and Sharon Gail Michalik (James) of Milan, New Hampshire.Married over 66 years, Kim and Gail enjoyed an active lifetime together; prior to and after their retirement, always side by side.  Their retirement included much entertaining and boating at their special ‘Cottage’ at Bay View, South Dartmouth, along with golfing and many sunny winter breaks in Florida.Kim presided over the marriages of all three of their children and he and Gail cherished many warm and happy memories with their children, grandchildren, Kimberly Gail, Jonathan and Jeremy and members of a large and loving family, including The Elliot & Barbara Whitney Family, The Fred & Ann Chase Family, The Curt & Heidi Chase Family, other members of Gail’s family including The family of Helen Potthoff, predeceased.  Kim remained in good humor despite his last illness.  He continued to the end, touching the lives of all in his presence with the same fellowship, warmth and engaging personality with which he had touched many thousands of lives before.  Visiting hours are scheduled at the Dartmouth Funeral Home (Waring-Sullivan) at 230 Russells Mills Road in North Dartmouth, Massachusetts for Sunday, December 17, 2007 from 3pm to 6pm.  A Memorial Service and Internment at St. Aiden’s Chapel on Smithneck Road will be scheduled in the near future. In lieu of flowers the family requests that donations to charity be made in Kim’s honor. Arrangements by the Bryant Funeral Home in Berlin, NH.

Norman Francis Foster affectionately called "Norm" was born on January 9, 1927 in the Bronx, New York but grew up in Providence, Rhode Island.  He moved to Alabama and briefly attended Tuskegee Institute before joining the U.S. Army where he served from July 1945 to October 1952. After military, Norm attended Morgan State University, but graduated from Morris Brown University in Atlanta, Georgia with a Bachelor Degree in English and Literature.  He later received a Master of Science Degree in Personnel Administration and Psychology from Alabama A&M University in Huntsville, Alabama. Norm enjoyed a long and successful 38 year career with the Federal Government where he served in many leadership and management positions to include assignments in Korea and Viet Nam. He removed to Huntsville in 1978 to work for the Department of Army at Redstone Arsenal where he served as the Chief of the Management Employee Relations and Labor Relations Office until his retirement on November 30, 1990.  After his retirement from the government, Norm worked for five years at Alabama A&M University in the Human Resources Office.  Norm was a member of the Alpha Phi Alpha Fraternity for over 50 years.  He loved people and music and he had a great affinity for high technology video, stereo, and TV equipment which were great hobbies for him.  But, Norm cared about his family and friends most of all.  Norm is survived by his wife Betty, two sons, Frank and Danny; nieces, Paula McCullar and her sons, Adam and Joshua, Alice Jamison, Aisha Murray and family, Melita Carney; nephew, Sir Jamison; special friends; Jacqueline Rogers, Dawn Wallace, Ellexus Wallace and son Charles, Walter Pickett, and Jackie Bennett. A memorial service will be held for Mr. Norman Foster at 6:00 p.m. on Monday, December 17, 2007 from the Nelms Memorial Funeral Home Chapel with Reverend Wayne Snodgrass officiating.

George W. Baldwin, 59, of North Easton, formerly of Brockton, died at home, surrounded by his family, on Wednesday December 12, 2007, following a long and valiant battle with melanoma.  Born in Binghamton, New York to the late George J. and Beulah (Ellis) Baldwin, George grew up in the Campello section of Brockton, an area to which he was devoted throughout his life.  He leaves his high school sweetheart and wife of 36 years, Maria (Correnti) Baldwin. A 1967 graduate of Brockton High School, George received degrees from Massasoit Community College and Boston University and earned numerous professional certifications in banking and real estate.  He was the owner and CEO of Corporate Realty Associates for the past 20 years and was a former Vice President and Senior Loan Officer for Peoples Savings Bank where he established a lifelong pattern of community service.  After moving from Brockton to North Easton 22 years ago, George remained a staunch supporter of the City of Champions. He gave his time unselfishly in service to many organizations. He was a member and past president of The Metro South Chamber of Commerce; founder and past president of the Brockton 21st Century Corporation; founder and past director of the Brockton Boys and Girls Club; a founder of the Big Brother Association in Brockton; served on the board of Cardinal Spellman High School and was a past director of the Brockton Regional Economic Development Corporation. In his free time George loved to play golf and was a member of Thorny Lea Golf Club.  He was an avid RV-er and enjoyed traveling with his wife and family. He wintered in the Space Coast area of Florida and spent his summers in Truro, where he loved walking the beach and clamming. But just being with his family gave him the most enjoyment in life. In addition to his wife he is survived by a son and daughter, G. George A. “Bert” Boucher, 80 of Londonderry, NH died December 12, 2007 in Mt. Carmel Nursing Home, Manchester, NH. Bert was born in Dobbs Ferry, NY on February 25, 1927 the son of the late George E. and Elizabeth M. (Landzert) Boucher. He was raised and educated in Hastings-on-Hudson, NY and later graduated from The Ohio State University Class of 1948. He was a life member of Sigma Chi Fraternity. Bert served his country with the US Navy during WWII. He retired from Mergenthaler Lynotype, Brooklyn, NY after a lengthy career and went on to work as a private consultant for the next 30 years. Bert was an active communicant of St. Jude Parish Community in Londonderry. He was a 4th Degree Knight and Past Grand Knight of the Knights of Columbus Council 6949 in Londonderry where he was a founding member. Bert was a life member of the Londonderry Rotary Club and a member of the American Legion Post #27 of Londonderry. He was also active in the planning of the Old Home Day’s celebration for the town of Londonderry for many years.	He is survived by his wife of 60 years, Ginny (Stoner) Boucher of Londonderry, three children, Victoria S. Morley and her husband Rich of Newington, CT, G. Christopher Boucher and his wife Joan of Brier, WA, Kathryn E. Giannelli and her husband Dave of Amityville, NY, and a sister, Betty Kelley and her husband Bill of Mashpee, MA. He also leaves seven grandchildren, Gregory, Michael, Kevin, Jennifer, Derek, Maia, Amanda and one great-grandchild, Hunter.	Calling hours will be held on Sunday, December 16, 2007 from 2 – 6pm in the Peabody Funeral Homes and Crematorium, 290 Mammoth Rd., Londonderry. A mass of Christian burial will be celebrated on Monday at 10:00am in St. Jude Parish, 435 Mammoth Rd., Londonderry with cremation to follow. Burial will be in the Spring in Mt. Hope Cemetery, Hastings-on-Hudson. Memorial contributions may be made to St. Jude Parish Building Fund, 435 Mammoth Rd., Londonderry, NH 03053.

Mr. J.D. Massey, affectionately known by many as "PaPa", was the first child born to the late Robert and Betty (Nobles) Massey on September 7, 1932.  He confessed hope in Christ at an early age and worshiped and served him as a member of the Indian Creek Primitive Baptist Church, Huntsville, AL.  Having completed his earthly journey, J.D. accepted his invitation to eternal life on December 11, 2007. In accordance with Proverbs 18:23, the legacy of J.D. Massey is seeded in his finding of a loving, and faithful wife, Mable Turner Massey.  On December 5, 2007, J.D. and Mable celebrated 48 years of marriage.  God's subsequent favor was multiplied in the blessings of children, grandchildren, and great-grandchildren. J.D.'s journey through life included attending Council Training School, serving in the U.S. Army and meeting many life-long friends as he worked and retired from Oakwood College and the Madison County Board of Education.  Determined to remain active, J.D. found employment with the Volunteers of America organization where he worked until his health deteriorated. J.D.'s wonderful life was tempered with sorrow in the loss of his daughter DeBarbara Massey, granddaughter, Megan Walker, and siblings Robert Jr. and Eddie Lee Massey.  However, his memories and legacy will remain strong through the lives of his beloved wife, Mable, four sons, Duncan (Cynthia) Massey, New Market, AL, Derrick Massey, Huntsville, AL, Toney (Catrina) Massey, Harvest, AL, Gregory Massey, Madison, AL.  Four daughters, Tammy (Vincent) Walker, Harvest, AL, Dianna and Sonya Massey both of Huntsville, AL, and Erica (Larry) Rice, Harvest, AL. Eleven grandchildren, Tangela (Marcus) Beasley, Austin Harris, Tori Massey, Alston and Brooker Walker, Jarred and Alivia Massey, and Brandon and Bradley Massey.  Four great-grandchildren, Amarious and Mason Beasley; Asia Massey and Lauren Rice.  Two brothers Freddie (Barbara) Massey, College Park, GA and Carl Massey, Huntsville, AL. Two sisters, Sadie (Eddie) Jefferson and Bertha (James) Lang all of Huntsville, AL; two brothers-in-law Charles (Annie Ruth) Turner and Cleatus (Ernestine) Turner, one sister-in-law Peggy Turner, Atlanta, GA.  A host of nieces, nephews and other special families and friends.  Funeral Services will be held Saturday, December 15, 2007 at 1:00 p.m. from the Indian Creek Primitive Baptist Church, with Pastor Timothy Rainey Officiating. Interment will be in the Valhalla Memory Gardens. The remains of Mr. Massey will be at the church one hour prior to funeral time.

Sister Rachel Anderson, CSC (Sister Mary David Henry), 70, of Nashua, NH, died December 11, 2007, in St. George Manor.  She was born on June 12, 1937, in North Grosvenordale, CT, and was the daughter of Henry and Josephine (Gagne) Anderson.  Sister Rachel entered the Congregation of the Sisters of Holy Cross in 1958 and made her final profession in 1965. She served the congregation for 49 years. Sister Rachel earned a B.A. from Notre Dame College, Manchester, NH and an M.A. from Fairfield University, Fairfield CT. She taught elementary and junior high schools under the direction of the Sisters of Holy Cross in Westport and Attleboro, MA; St. Albans, VT; Somersworth, and Nashua, NH and Groton CT. She also served as Principal of Sacred Heart School, Groton, CT and as an instructor, secretary, and registrar of Notre Dame College, Manchester, NH for eight years. Sister Rachel was an Administrative Assistant in the Diocesan School Office in Norwich, CT. Since 2004 to the present, Sister Rachel had been the Coordinator of Religious Education at Holy Infant Jesus Parish in Nashua NH. She was a poet and published a book of poetry entitled Gentle Whisperings of the Heart. She also has written another book that is soon to be released. Her vivid pen has touched many persons especially her Sisters in Holy Cross. Sister Rachel was a member of Poets for Integrity. She leaves a sister, Therese Choiniere of Webster, MA; a brother, Richard Anderson of No. Grosvenordale, CT; several nieces and nephews.   A Mass of Christian Burial was celebrated Friday December 14, 2007 in St. George Manor, 357 Island Pond Rd. Manchester, NH. Burial followed in St. Augustin Cemetery, Manchester, NH.In lieu of flowers, memorial donations can be made to Sisters of Holy Cross Development Fund, c/o Sisters of Holy Cross 377 Island Pond Road, Manchester NH 03109. Lambert Funeral Home & Crematory, 1799 Elm St. Manchester, was in charge of the arrangements.

Edward “Ronald” Hildebrand, 62, of Ledyard, passed away Tuesday after a truly heroic battle with lung disease. Ron was born May 7, 1945 to Thurman and Ethel (Maynard) Hildebrand. He was raised in Ledyard and later joined the U.S. Air Force where he served during the Vietnam War.  After receiving his honorable discharge, he lived in Colorado where he worked as an insurance agent. He later learned the trade of goldsmithing and spent many years mastering this trade and was well-known for his work as a master jeweler. Returning to Ledyard to be closer to his family, Ron then realized his lifelong dream of building a log cabin for his family. He will be remembered as a dedicated family man who enjoyed spending time with his family and also loved sailing, firearms, fishing, working on cars, and fishing. He was a member of the Ledyard Fire Dept. In addition to his parents, he is survived by his loving wife of 21 years, Kathy; his daughters Brittany, Heather, and Jodi; son Jeff and his wife Rene’; grandchildren, Haley, Jarett, Riley, Baley, Kiley, and another “on the way”; his brothers, Mark and his wife Marcia, Thurman Arnold, nieces, nephews, aunt Dorothy, uncle Arthur, and his dog “Buddy”. Ron also enjoyed a close relationship with his cousin and friend, Raymond Maynard. Ron’s family will greet relatives and friends at The Dinoto Funeral Home, 17 Pearl Street, Historic downtown Mystic on Wednesday from 6-8pm. His family will gather privately for a service with military honors at Avery-Stoddard Cemetery, Ledyard.  In lieu of flowers, contributions may be made to American Lung Assoc., 45 Ash St., E. FRANKLIN - Charles E. Feyler, 74, of Franklin died Monday, Dec. 10, 2007, at Milford Regional Medical Center, shortly after his arrival there. He was the husband of Kathleen M. (Gagnon) Feyler for 48 years. Born in Boston, a son of the late Wilbur and Margaret Feyler, he was raised and educated in Boston and was a Franklin resident since 1960. Until his retirement, Mr. Feyler worked for the Massachusetts Bay Transportation Authority for more than 30 years. He also worked in the home building trade with the late George Iverson, who built many homes in Franklin in the 1960s and 1970s. He was a member of the Dedham Moose Lodge, Franklin Lodge of Elks, Franklin Senior Citizens and American Association of Retired Persons and was a communicant of St. Mary Church. His hobbies and interests included his grandchildren, neighborhood children, the Boston Red Sox and New England Patriots and volunteering with the Franklin Elks and delivering food baskets to the needy and homeless. He also enjoyed wintering in Sarasota, Fla., where he and his wife spent the last 15 winters. Besides his wife, he leaves a son, Robert P. Gaskell of Phoenix, Ariz.; a daughter, Karen A. Kelleher of Mashpee; three brothers, Michael Feyler of Dedham, Richard Feyler of Stoughton and Robert Feyler of Pennsylvania; three sisters, Pauline Young of Wellesley, Brenda O'Brien of Hyde Park and Carol Clifford of Readville; four grandchildren; and two great-grandchildren. He was also the brother of the late Wilbur and Paul Feyler. The funeral will be held with military honors Friday, Dec. 14, at 8:15 a.m., from the Charles F. Oteri & Son Franklin Funeral Home (franklinfuneral.com), 33 Cottage St., with a funeral Mass in St. Mary Church, 1 Church Square, at 9 a.m. Burial will follow in St. Mary's Cemetery. Calling hours are Thursday, Dec. 13, from 4 to 8 p.m. Memorial donations may be sent to American Heart Association, 20 Speen St., Framingham, MA 01701.

George E. MacNair, a resident of Newton Centre for over 64 years, died Monday Dec. 10, 2007 at his home. He was 92.Born in Houlton, ME, he was a graduate of Colby College. Visiting hours will be in the Eaton funeral Home 1351 Highland Ave Needham on Thurs from 2-4 & 6-8 PM.In lieu of flowers memorials in George’s name may be made to Trinity Episcopal Church 11 Homer St. Newton MA 02459 or the Alzheimer’s Assoc. 311 Arsenal St. Watertown MA 02472.

Peirce.     Born in Seekonk, MA on February 28, 1031 she was a daughter of the late Ernest and Elsie (Lincoln) White, Sr.    She grew up in Norton, was educated in Norton public schools and was a 1949 graduate of Norton High School.    A retired Jewelry Inspector, Mrs. Peirce had worked in the general findings division at Leach & Garner in Attleboro for 19 years prior to retiring.    Bette had lived in Norton for most of her life and enjoyed collecting glass figurines.     She is survived by her devoted children; Jeffrey Peirce and his wife Barbara of Sturbridge and Cindy Astrofsky and her husband Daniel of Attleboro, she was the sister of Ernest White, Jr. of Attleboro, Sandra Richard of Vale, North Carolina and the late Shirley Cronin, also survived by her grandchildren; Kerri Peirce, Jeffrey Peirce, Niccole Astrofsky, Alexandria Astrofsky, Alicia Peirce and Kevin Peirce as well as many nieces, nephews and grand nieces and nephews.     Funeral services, to which relatives and friends are cordially invited to attend, will be held on Friday December 14th at 12:00 P.M. at the Norton Memorial Funeral Home 19 Clapp St. (Off Route 140, Taunton Ave.) Norton. Burial will follow in the Timothy Plain Cemetery, Norton.     Visiting hours are omitted. In lieu of flowers those wishing may remember Bette with a donation in her memory to the charity of ones choice.     For additional information please visit www.nortonmemorial.com

Julia M. (Scutellaro) LaMarca, of Medford, died Monday at Wilmington Health Care Nursing Home. She was 96. She was born and raised in Boston’s North End the daughter of the late Luigi and Adelina Scutellaro. She met her husband Charles in the North End. The couple lived in Quincy briefly and settled in Medford. In her early years she helped operate her family’s business, LaMarca Baking Co. Julia lived in Medford for over 70 years. A wonderful wife, mother and grandmother, Julia loved the company of her family and friends. She loved to cook for family and also enjoyed many visits to Foxwoods.   She was the beloved wife of the late Charles A. LaMarca, Sr. She was a devoted mother to Louis A. LaMarca and his wife Teddi of Tewksbury, Charles A. LaMarca, Jr. and his wife Phyllis of Burlington, the late Carmen LaMarca. She was a dear mother-in law to Dolores LaMarca of Billerica. She is lovingly survived by 14 grandchildren, 31 great-grandchildren, two great-great-grandchildren. She was a dear sister to Jennie Scalfani of Medford, the late Nancy Ragusa, Mary Sciaraffa, Thomas, James, Armando, Frederick, Joseph and Frank Scutellaro. She was a devoted sister-in-law to the late Josephine and Phillip LaMarca. Her funeral will be from the Dello Russo Funeral Home, 306 Main St., Medford Thursday at 8 AM followed by a funeral mass celebrated in St. Clement Church, 71 Warner Street, Medford, at 9 AM. Relatives and friends are respectfully invited to attend and may visit with the family, Wednesday 4 thru 8. Services will conclude with burial at Oak Grove Cemetery, Medford.

WEST BOYLSTONRobert “Bob” P. Plucinski, 80, of West Boylston died Mon., Dec. 10 at home.He leaves his beloved wife of 57 years, Nancy E. (Emmett) Plucinski and their three children; Susan E. Galvin and her husband Walt of Springfield, VA, Ellen M. DeSanti and her husband Raymond of West Boylston, and Stephen F. Plucinski of Holden, and  six grandchildren; Christopher, Sharon, Marya, Jaime, Nathan and Sarah; and one great-grand-daughter Jessica. He also leaves his brother Walter Plucinski of Worcester and several nieces and nephews, including his fishing buddy Paul Plucinski. He was a devoted brother to Sophie Keith, Irene Pion, and George Plucinski, who predeceased him.He was born in Worcester, son of Benjamin and Agnes (Warchal) Plucinski.  He graduated from Commerce High and Clark University. He served in the Army during the Police Action at the end of the Korean War.Bob was a foreman at Norton Company for many years, retiring in 1985. He loved the outdoors, hunting, fishing, walking, and puttering around his vegetable garden and yard. He also took pleasure in cooking and enjoyed playing cards with his family and friends.The funeral services are private. In lieu of flowers, memorial donations may be made to VNA Hospice, 120 Thomas St., Worcester, 01608 or to the American Cancer Society, 30 Speen St., Framingham, 01701. Fay Brothers Funeral Home, 1 W. Salem:  Peter J. Doyle age 77, retired Salem educator, died unexpectedly, Monday, December 10, 2007 at the NSMC / Salem Hospital.  He was the husband of Joan (Laborde) Doyle with whom he shared over 50 years of marriage.Born and raised in Salem, he was the son of the late Joseph and Mary Alice (Hayes) Doyle.  A graduate of St. Mary’s School and Salem High he received his Master’s Degree in Education from Salem State College.   Peter began his career in education teaching at the Bowditch School, the Pickering School and later the Saltonstall School.  He subsequently became principal of the Saltonstall School, Horace Mann South and the Bates Elementary School.  He retired from the Bates School in 1995.Peter was an avid basketball, tennis and golf fan.  He began playing semi-pro basketball for Winslow Chip Co.  At Salem State he was inducted into the Hall of Fame and was later joined by his son Tom, the first father and son to be so honored. For 12 years he was the doubles champion in the City of Salem Tennis competition held at the Willows.  Golf was his other passion and for years he was active at the Salem Muni later to become Olde Salem Greens.  In 1976 & 1978 his twosome won the Salem Country Club 4 Ball Championship.   Peter was also a staunch supporter of Salem High basketball and golf as well as researching the history of athletics for Salem High, Salem State and the Salem Muni.  He was a member of the Salem State Alumni Committee.In addition to his wife Joan, he is survived by a daughter, Kim M. Erps and her husband Ralph of Salem, 4 sons:  John P. Doyle of Salem, David Doyle and Louanne Gray of Worcester, Patrick J. Doyle of Salem, Thomas R. Doyle and his wife Jessica of Salem, 3 brothers: James Doyle of San Diego, CA, David Doyle of Salem, Louis Doyle of NY, NY, 3 sisters:  Mary Doyle of Salem, Ann Kiley of Peabody, Ellen Tassinari of Beverly.  Peter is also survived by 3 grandchildren: Timothy P. Erps, Ethan T. Doyle and Jack P. Doyle and many nieces and nephews.  He was predeceased by 5 brothers: Joseph, Jr., Jack, Richard, Philip and Thomas Doyle.Relatives and friends are invited to his funeral on Friday, December 14, 2007 at 8 am from the Levesque Funeral Home, 163 Lafayette St., [Rt. 114 / 1-A] followed by his funeral mass in the Immaculate Conception Church, Salem at 9 am.  Visiting hours:  Thursday 2 to 4 & 7 to 9 pm.  Burial will be in St. Mary’s Cemetery, Salem.  In lieu of flowers memorial contributions may be made to the Peter J. Doyle Memorial Fund,c/o Salem High School, Attn: Carol Amodeo, 77 Wilson St., Salem MA 01970.  For guest book and additional information, please visit www.levesquefunerals.com

Carolyn Y. (Yukna) Babcock, nee Carolyn Ann Yukna, 62, of Marion died December 10, 2007 at St. Luke’s Hospital after a period of failing health.  She was the wife of Bruce R. Babcock with whom she celebrated her 29th anniversary in May. Born in Brockton, the daughter of the late Daniel A. and Stacy (Stripinis) Yukna, she had lived in Stanton, New Jersey, and Charlotte, North Carolina, as well as Mattapoisett and Marion. She attended the Brockton public schools and graduated from The University of Massachusetts, Amherst with a BA in English. She began her employment as a programmer at Honeywell Corporation and eventually retired from AT&T after a lengthy management career during which she held significant responsibilities in several outside vendor and billing facilities.  Subsequent to retirement she explored new career interests as both a personnel shopper and chef.   She was a lover of animals, particularly cats and dogs which she enjoyed as house pets. She also had an avid interest in sports and was fond of playing and watching golf.  Additionally, she enjoyed traveling and loved the beaches of St. Johns in the Virgin Islands.  Being of Lithuanian heritage, she traveled to the source of her ancestry in 2004 and immersed herself in the culture of Lithuania for ten days. Her greatest interest and passion focused upon food and cooking.  She derived many hours of pleasure watching The Food Network and amassed a vast library of cook books and recipes which she had researched and collected.  She was happiest when working in the kitchen preparing to entertain family or friends.   Survivors include her devoted husband, who will miss her forever; her twin sister Kathryn A. and husband Leo of Hubbardston, Massachusetts; two brothers, Dr. Raymond A. Yukna and his wife Charlotte of Denver, Colorado; and Paul D. Yukna and his wife Marie of Milford, Connecticut.  Additionally, she leaves two nephews, Ryan R. Sullivan and his wife Carrie and daughters Shea, Kelsey and Elyse and Brendan T. Sullivan and his wife Kate. Donations may be made to the American Heart Association, National Center, 7272 Greenville Avenue, Dallas, Texas 75231 Private arrangements are with the Saunders-Dwyer Mattapoisett Home for Funerals, 50 County Rd. (Rt. 6) Mattapoisett.  For on-line guestbook, please visit www.saundersdwyer.com.

Jean H. Poulin, 74, of Goffstown, died December 9, 2007 at Catholic Medical Center, following a brief illness.  He was born in Manchester, October 19, 1933 and was the son of Henry and Julienne (Dupont) Poulin. He was a graduate of St Marie's High School in Manchester.  He served his country with the US Army Military Police from 1955 to 1957 and was stationed in Europe.   While stationed overseas, Jean traveled extensively throughout Italy, Austria, Germany and France.  Although he found Paris to be the most beautiful city, the highlight of his travels was the blessing by the Pope in Rome.  Jean was a very kind and gentle man who loved music, the arts and classic movies.  He especially enjoyed time spent with family.  He was a gracious, loving man who will be missed by all who had the pleasure of knowing him. A former factory worker, Jean found his true calling helping the residents of Mt. Ten years later she married Nathaniel Hunt of Boston, who predeceased her in 1992. An active member of the Unitarian Universalist Society of Fairhaven, she served for many years on the Board of Directors of the former Bradford Russell Home in Fairhaven. In 1992, Edith retired to Thirwood Place in South Yarmouth and in 2000 moved to Our Lady's Haven, where she died on December 8, 2007. Survived by two nephews, Charles McKim Mitchell of Fairhaven and Douglas J. Mitchell. A celebration of Life service will be held at the Unitarian Memorial Church in Fairhaven on Saturday, December 22, at 11 A.M. In lieu of flowers, donations may be made in her name to Oglala Lakota College, Piya Wiconi, P.O. Box 490, Kyle, SD 57752. Arrangements are with the Wilson Chapel, 479 County St., New Bedford.

Jerry L. Mennitto, a retired Deputy Sheriff for Middlesex County, died Saturday at the Lahey Clinic, Burlington. He was 80.  Mr. Mennitto was born in Harrisburg, PA, the son of the late John and the late Anita (Preciosi) Mennitto. He was raised in the Bronx, NY where he developed a love for the New York Yankees. After his service in the Navy during World War II, Jerry eventually settled in Somerville and married his wife of 60 years, Evelyn McCoy. A natural leader, he was an active member in the American Legion, Post 19 and served as Commander. A talented speaker, Jerry was active in local political campaigns and always could be counted on to warm-up a crowd for the candidates and to help win a precinct or two. Alice B. Donovan...of Tewksbury; age 78TEWKSBURY - Alice B. (Collins) Donovan, age 78, a resident of Tewksbury since 1959, died unexpectedly on Saturday, Dec. 8, at Beth Israel Deaconess Hospital in Boston. Alice was the loving wife, for over 35 years, of Albert J. Donovan, who died in 1988.  She was born January 23, 1929, a daughter of the late James and Mary (Hart) Collins.  She lived in Waltham before moving to Tewksbury.For more than 30 years, until her retirement, she was employed by Raytheon Company, South Lowell.Mrs. Donovan was a communicant of St. William's Church.Alice was described by her family as having enjoyed Ballroom Dancing, going to the beach, camping, and traveling with family and friends.  She dedicated her life to helping others. She "had a big heart,"  and always thought of everyone else first.  Her most important activities were spending time with her family and spoiling her grandchildren.She is survived by four children,  James Donovan of Tewksbury, Diane Donovan of Dracut,  Paul Donovan of Dracut,   Linda (Donovan) Stumpf and her husband Robert  of  Townsend.  Another family member the family would like to acknowledge is Mirta Cruz  of Dracut who Alice viewed as a daughter.   Alice is also survived by a brother, James Collins and his wife Jenny of Virginia; a sister, Mary Tyman of Connecticut; brother-in-law, William Donovan and his wife Ellie of Quincy;  twelve grandchildren; one great granddaughter; several Nieces, Nephews, Cousins, as well as many close friends especially her dear friend, Mary McManis of Florida and Lorraine of Lowell.Alice was also the mother of the late Stephen P. Donovan and Timothy Donovan; and sister of the late Dolly Snell. Her Funeral Mass was celebrated Friday, Dec. 14, in St. William's Church, Tewksbury.  Burial followed at St. Mary Cemetery, No. Tewksbury.

Thomas F. Beals, Sr. 73 of Whitman passed away quietly Saturday December 8th in the Brockton Hospital after a lengthy illness. During the Cold War he was entrusted with the highest level of security conferred upon non-military personnel.   After retiring in1989, he became well know for his restoration of early American furniture and clocks.  He also enjoyed landscaping and gardening. He was a long time member of St. Paul’s Episcopal Church in Brockton, the Church of Christ in Easton and the Immanuel Baptist Church in Brockton where he was a frequent soloist. In addition to his loving wife he is survived by his children, Thomas F. By request of the family all other services will be private.

Felicia A. McDonald, 86, of The Woodlands, TX died Friday December 7, 2007, in The Woodlands where she lived with her family Corey and Katie Collins and their son Liam.  Mrs. McDonald was born in Quincy, MA on March 2, 1921 a daughter of Felix and Nellie (Pawlizyn) Romanowsky. She was a 1940 graduate of the Woodward School for Girls. Felicia moved from Boston, MA to Derry, NH in 1966 and then to The Woodlands, TX to live with her family in 2005. She retired in 1986 from Sanders Associates in Nashua, NH after 20 years. She was known for her selflessness and extreme generosity. A true fan of New England Sports, she never missed a football or baseball game no matter where she was. She recently became a jetsetter, flying between New Hampshire and Texas with her first flight when she moved in 2005. An avid storyteller, she also loved crossword puzzles and the television shows Jeopardy, Wheel of Fortune and Survivor.       She is survived by her sister, Genevieve Coughlin of Carver, MA, her adopted family, Corey Collins, his wife Katie, and their son Liam of The Woodlands, TX, many nieces, nephews and friends. She was predeceased by her husband, Hugh L. McDonald in 2000 and her sister, Helen Kieslowski.       There will be no calling hours. A graveside service will be held Thursday December 20th at 10:00am at the Forest Hill Cemetery, Hampstead Rd., E. Derry, NH. Felicia was an avid and proud blood donor when she was able. In lieu of flowers, the family asks that all able persons take time out of their lives to donate blood to help give the “gift of life” so others may live. The Peabody Funeral Homes and Crematorium, 15 Birch St., Derry is handling the arrangements.

WORCESTER__ Joseph A. Letiecq, 92, of Worcester, a survivor of the attack on Pearl Harbor died in Saint Francis Home, 101 Plantation Street, on Friday, December 7, 2007, the 66th anniversary of the attack. He leaves a sister, Cecile Lucas of Worcester; a brother, Albert A. Letiecq and his wife, Lynn of Sunny Isle, Florida, a sister-in-law, Irene Letiecq of Worcester and several nieces and nephews. He was predeceased by a brother, Achilles Letiecq and a sister, Alexina Roy. Joseph was born in Worcester, a son of Gustave and Alexina (Pageau) Letiecq and lived here all his life. Mr. Letiecq was a drill hand operator for Crompton & Knowles Company retiring in 1980. He was a U.S. Navy veteran of World War II serving on the USS Regal in Pearl Harbor as a Machinist mate 1st class. During his ten years in the U.S. Navy he also served on the Landing Ship LSD No.29 and in 1946 on the Aircraft carrier USS Shangri-La. During that time, he played baseball on the U.S. Navy traveling team. He attended Naval School in Bainbridge, MD. Joseph was a witness to the first atomic bomb test in the Pacific.  He received the Asiatic Pacific Ribbon with five stars and the Phillipine Liberation Medal. Mr. Letiecq graduated from Worcester Boys’ Trade High School. He was an lifelong sportsman, having played semi-pro baseball and was an avid golfer and bowler throughout his life. He was a member of St. Joseph Church and was also a member of the Greendale Retired Men’s Club.The funeral will be held on Tuesday, December 11 from DIRSA-MORIN FUNERAL HOME, 298 Grafton Street, with a Mass at 11:00 a.m. in St. Joseph Church, 35 Hamilton Street. Burial will be in Notre Dame Cemetery. A calling hour will be held in the funeral home  Tuesday December 11 from 9:30 to 10:30 a.m. In lieu of flowers, memorial contributions may be made to Saint Francis Home, 101 Plantation Street, Worcester, MA 01604.

FRED B. COLE, of Hingham, died on Thursday, Dec. 6th at his home.  Born in 1915, Fred was a Hingham native for 92 years.  He graduated from Thayer Academy and the Leland Powers School of Broadcasting.       Fred was a well known Boston Broadcaster working for stations WNAC, WBZ and WHDH.  His career took him to Hollywood and New York City where he worked for both the ABC and NBC Networks.  He was best known as the voice of the Big Bands, announcing for the Orchestras of  Glenn Miller, Artie Shaw, Tommy Dorsey, Charlie Barnet, Chick Webb as well as vocalists Frank Sinatra and Vaughan Monroe among others.       While working for WHDH from 1946 to 1967 he continued playing the sounds of the Big Bands.  In the 60's he traveled New England in the first A& P sponsored Mobile Studio, allowing his listeners to view his show live.  He created a huge fan base by connecting with many traveling salesmen who had become members of the Fred B. Cole Huckster's Club.  As a tribute to his career, Fred was inducted into the Massachusetts Broadcasters Hall of Fame in May of 2007.       In 1984 Fred's smallest audience gave him his biggest thrill.  For several years he was a rookie civilian dispatcher for his much admired and respected Hingham Police Department.       He gave of his time to the town he so loved by serving on the Conservation Commission for many years.  He was also a member of Old Colony Lodge, A.F. & A.M. of Hingham and was a longtime member of the Hingham Yacht Club.  In addition, he served as a director of the Industrial Credit Union in Boston.        He is survived by his wife of 65 years, the love of his life, Betsey (Balch) Cole.  Devoted father of Cheryl Sherwin and her husband Leo, and Daphne Keeler and her husband Dan, all of Hingham.  Four loving grandchildren; Scott McLaughlin of San Diego, Stacey Paretchan of Hingham, Dana Keeler of Boston and the late Dean Keeler.  In addition, four great-grandchildren; Alex, Tyler, Danielle and Cole.         At the request of his family, funeral services will be private.  In lieu of flowers, donations in his memory may be made to the Hingham Land Conservation Trust, P.O. Box 10, Hingham, MA 02043.

Boylston – Elsie I. (Gustavson) Sjoblad passed away Thursday, December 6, 2007 at the age of 85 in UMass Memorial Medical Center. She leaves her husband of 62 years, Roy Sjoblad; her children Roy D. Sjoblad and his wife Beverly of Hyattsville, MD, Nancy S. Gill and her husband Dennis of Wenham, MA, Jonathan Sjoblad and his wife Stephanie of Raleigh, NC, and Donna O’Driscoll of Northborough, MA; and grandchildren Nathan Sjoblad, Allison Green, Johanna Soyars, Andrew J. Gill, Katelyn, Michael, Griffin and Molly O’Driscoll, and Krister and Karsten Sjoblad plus two great grandchildren, Braylin and Jaxson.  She also leaves a dear sister, Norma, wife of Kenneth Ahlberg and also several loving nieces and nephews.  Elsie was predeceased by a sister, Vivian Johnson. Born to Irenes and Helen Gustavson in Worcester, June 27, 1922, she was a graduate of the former Belmont Preparatory School and the North High School. Elsie then attended the Memorial Hospital School of Nursing, graduating in 1945. She served in the Nursing Arts Section and the hospital wards. Elsie resumed her nursing profession at Worcester City Hospital in 1969. Retiring in 1986, she was active for many years at Salem Covenant Church in Worcester, on the North High Reunion Committee and also received the Thomas Jefferson Award for her work as a volunteer teacher’s aide at Clark Street School. Elsie was an avid seamstress and enjoyed all her grandchildren. All through her life she exhibited a great concern for those physically ill in hospital or anyone in need or less fortunate. She will be missed by all her family and those who came in contact with her love and concern. Funeral services will be private. Burial will be in Boylston Pine Grove Cemetery. Memorial donations may be made in her name to Salem Covenant Church, 215 E. Mountain St., Worcester, MA 01606. Mary Frances Rice was born on July 28, 1934 to the late Mr. Issac Jacobs and Mrs. Beatrice Stone in Madison County, Alabama. She gracefully and quietly transitioned into eternal life on Thursday Morning, December 6, 2007. Mary Frances Rice showed her love for God by caring for others; her family, friends, and anyone that spent time in her presence. Mary Frances spent her childhood in Madison County, where she received the training and directions that guided her life. As a young lady, she moved to Toledo, where she met and married her soul mate, Mr. James F. Rice.  When inquiries were made about the length of their marriage, they would response in unison, “50 plus years.” Mary Frances Rice was employed by Hunt Wesson Food (Conagra) as an assembly worker. She took special care in the nurturing of her family, cooking and maintaining her home. She also had a love for shopping. After relocating to Huntsville, you could find her in the company of Matilda or Mary, her chaperones, on the rare occasions when her number one driver, James, was unavailable.  Following their move to Huntsville the family knew that there lives were not complete without the covering and care of a church family, following the will of God, they united with First Missionary Baptist Church. Mary Frances was preceded in death by her daughter, Linda Ewing and her two granddaughters. Always a mother, following the death of her sister, she begin to love and nurture a new daughter, Sharon Stone. She leaves loving and cherish memories to her devoted husband, James Floyd Rice;  daughter, Sharon Stone; three brothers: Percy Stone, Aundre (Allison) Jacobs, both of Huntsville, Alabama and Darrell (Monaca) Jacobs of Detroit, Michigan; stepson, Harold (Shirley) Davis of Huntsville, Alabama; four sisters-in-law: Lorna Rice, Kathleen Rice, Iradell Rice, all of Madison, Alabama and Katie Janey of Philadelphia, Pennsylvania; one brother-in-law, C. L. (Berda) Rice of Chattanooga, Tennessee, special friends: Bessie Boston of Mobile, Alabama, Ida Williams of Florida, and Elizabeth Gray of Toledo, Ohio, who visits and phone calls she truly enjoyed, and a host of nieces, nephews, other relatives and friends. Funeral Services for Mrs. Mary Frances Rice will be held Thursday, December 13, 2007 at 11:00 from the First Missioanry Baptist Church with Pastor Julius R. Scruggs officiating. Interment will be in the Valhalla Memory Gardens.

BROCKTON, MA - George R. Papas, one of the most vociferous and energetic local politicians and liberal activists in Brockton's recent history, died tragically as a result of injuries sustained in a fire at his home on December 5th. He was 74 years of age. A teacher of art in the Stoughton Middle School for 30 years, George combined the sensibilities of an artist and a love of literature with the uncompromising brashness of a man who devoted his life to righting wrongs and fighting for what he considered to be the highest principals of democracy and human rights. If there was a war to be protested or a strike to be supported, you could find George on the picket line. He was a man who was determined to "make a difference," and he succeeded. Moving to Brockton in 1962, George threw himself immediately into the political life of the city. He ran the local headquarters for the presidential campaign of Eugene McCarthy in 1968, served as Brockton coordinator for the gubernatorial campaign of Kevin White in 1970, served as local campaign director for presidential candidate George McGovern in 1972, and went to the Democratic National Convention in Miami that year as a McGovern delegate. Determined to hold political office himself, he made two unsuccessful runs for School Committee starting in the 1960's, but returned undaunted to secure a seat on Brockton's City Council in the 1970's as a member of a controversial group of young reformers labeled by their opponents "The Sinister Seven". Later, after a hiatus from public office for more than a decade, he returned to the Council for two terms in the 1990's to help rescue the city from a fiscal crisis, and served as Council President in 1992. As an office holder as well as in his personal life, George was respected by friends and political opponents alike for always "telling it like it is." Although he had the mind of a social activist George had the soul of a poet and the hands of an artist. He painted whenever he could find the time, bold abstracts, often filled with political and social symbolism. Studying Italian Civilization, in Italy under a Fulbright-Hays Scholarship, he created a number of colorful oil pastel sketches to add to his portfolio. He even organized a juried art show at Brockton High School that with the help of his many friends, mushroomed into a three-day cultural festival called Summerfest that attracted thousands of people for several years in the early '70's.Confined to a hospital from the age of two to twelve because of an infection that settled in his hip, George turned to reading as an escape from his confinement. Later, as an adult polymath, books continued to be an open doorway through which he explored an ever-widening circle of interests. At the time of his childhood illness he was told he would never walk again, but with courage and determination he regained his mobility, although with a noticeable limp, and even played softball on one of Brockton's City League teams. George was loved by his family and admired by a loyal cadre of friends, as a strong willed, exceptionally intelligent, and exceedingly well-informed man for whom life had a clear purpose: to make the world a better place. George Papas was born in Boston in 1933, son of the late John and Beatrice (Trudell) Papas, and brother of the late Nicholas Papas. He attended Boston schools, and graduated from Boston State College with a BA in Education. He studied art at the School of the Museum of Fine Arts, Boston and at Massachusetts College of Art, Boston. He studied poetry at Harvard University. George received National Endowment for the Humanities grants, one to study Theatre of the Holocaust at the University of Wisconsin and another to study Literature of the Holocaust at Hollins University in Virginia. Among his many affiliations, he was a member of the Fulbright Association, the Stoughton Teachers Association, the Massachusetts Teachers Association, the National Education Association, and was past president of the D.W. Field's Park Association. George was also a long time member of the Brockton Democratic City Committee and served as chair of the Ward One committee for many years. He is survived by his wife of 49 years, Carole (Bumpus) Papas, their daughter, Sandra Papas-Summers, his son-in-law, David Summers, all of Brockton, and a son Myles Papas of Alabama. He is also survived by a granddaughter, Sandy Papas, and numerous nieces and nephews.Visiting hours will be Saturday January 5th from 1-3 PM in the Conley Funeral Home, 138 Belmont Street (Rte. 123) Brockton, followed by a memorial tribute from 3–4 PM. Donations in George's name may be made to The Jimmy Fund/Dana-Farber Cancer Institute 10 Brookline Place West 6th Floor Brookline, MA 02445, for online donations click here

LTC Charles (Chuck) McCloskey, Jr. (USAF-Ret) of Hingham, formerly of Canton and Venice, FL, passed away December 5, 2007 at the age of 84. Devoted husband to the late Doria (Lanza) McCloskey.  Revered father to Craig M. McCloskey of Stoughton, Carla A. Barlow of Hingham, and Lisa M. McCloskey of Wrentham.  Beloved grandfather of Brad P. McCloskey, Lisa M. Barlow, Grace L. Barlow and Jack S. Barlow.  One of seven siblings, he is survived by Imogene M. Bruenig of Punta Gorda, FL. Chuck devoted his life to serving his family, country and God.  He grew up in Stow, Ohio and entered the  military on January 10, 1942.  In 1944, he was commissioned through the Air Force Aviation Cadet Program and became a fighter pilot.  A highly decorated veteran, his military service included numerous combat missions in the European Theatre of Operation in World War II, flying bomber escort missions with a Mustang fighter squadron earning the Distinguished Flying Cross Medal.  He went on to fly combat missions in the Korean Conflict while planning the missions in Vietnam where his many achievements included the Bronze Star Medal at Tan Son Nhut.  After faithfully serving his country for more than 30 years, Chuck, an avid golfer, pursued many interests in civilian life including obtaining a master's degree, a real estate license, teaching, sales, and touring.  Chuck is remembered as a dedicated and devoted husband, father and grandfather and his Irish wit and charm will be missed by many including his friends and neighbors at Linden Ponds in Hingham. Relatives and friends are respectfully invited to attend the visitation at the Pyne Keohane Funeral Home, 21 Emerald St., HINGHAM, Tuesday commencing at 9 AM. A Funeral Mass will follow at Saint Paul's Church, Hingham at 10:00 AM. Burial will be private at the National Veterans Cemetery in Bourne. In lieu of flowers, donations in memory of LTC Charles L. McCloskey, Jr. may be made to Massachusetts Veterans, Inc. 69 Grove Street Worcester, MA 01605, www.massveterans.org a private, non-profit organization Chuck admired which is dedicated to the care and rehabilitation of veterans in need.

Brian MacMahon MD, PhD, a Needham resident and renowned epidemiologist died on Wednesday, December 5th at Beth Israel Deaconess medical center in Boston. He was 87 years old. Brian MacMahon was born on August 12, 1923 in Sheffield, England.  After education in public schools he entered the University of Birmingham where he qualified in medicine in 1946.  After serving for two years as a Ship Surgeon, he returned to the University of Birmingham, receiving the degrees of DrPH (Public Health) in 1949, PhD (Social Medicine) in 1952 and MD (with Honors) in 1955. He taught in the Department of Social Medicine at the University of Birmingham for four years before moving to the United States.  After a year as a student in the Department of Epidemiology at the Harvard School of Public Health, he taught in the Department of Environmental Medicine and Community Health of the State University of New York for four years where he was appointed Professor in 1957.  In 1958 he was appointed Professor and Chairman of the Department of Epidemiology at the Harvard School of Public Health where he remained until retirement in 1988. He served on many advisory and supervisory committees of the National Institutes of Health, the National Center for Heath Statistics, the American Cancer Society, the Canadian Cancer Society, the World Health Organization and the International Agency for Research on Cancer, as well as several industry-wide medical advisory committees. His research was in the fields of breast cancer, leukemia, Hodgkin’s disease and the congenital malformations.  He published five books or monographs and over 200 papers in the medical literature. He received the Edwards Memorial Medal of the University of Wales, The Donald Reid Medal of the London School of Hygiene and Tropical Medicine, the Lucy Wortham James Clinical Research Award of the Society for Surgical Oncology, the Thomas Francis Medal of the University of Michigan, the Mott Prize of the General Motors Cancer Research Foundation, the Prix Antoine Lacassagne of the French National League Against Cancer and Medals of Honor from the American Cancer Society and the International Agency for Research on Cancer.  He also received honorary doctoral degrees from the University of Athens, the State University of New York and the University of Birmingham. Brian married Heidi Graber on August 28, 1948.  They had four children: Michael, Kevin, Kathleen and Mary.  Michael and wife Marcy live in  New Hampshire, Kevin and wife Debie  in Daytona, Florida, Kathleen and husband Jason Barkeloo in Ohio and Mary in Massachusetts.  Brian has 3 grandchildren and 4 great grandchildren.Funeral services will be private. In lieu of flowers, donations in Brian’s name may be made to the Brian and Heidi MacMahon Epidemiology Education Fund, c/o Harvard School of Public Health, Office for Resource Development, Attn. Gift Processing, 401 Park Drive East, Atrium 3, Boston, MA, 02215. Arrangements by the Eaton Funeral Home 1351 Highland Avenue, Needham MA.

James A. Smith of Raymond, NH passed away this past Wednesday morning at his daughter's residence in Hampstead NH, surrounded by his loving family. James was born January 29, 1944 in Lawrence, Massachusetts, the son of the late Lawrence P. and Marguerita Smith.   Jimmy was educated at St. Augustine Grammar School in Lawrence, MA and Haverhill Trade School, where he studied to be an electrician. He was a licensed Master Electrician and was employed by the Essex County Sheriffs Department for over 30 years.  He was a lifelong active member of St. Augustine Parish, Lawrence, MA. Jimmy raised his family with his former wife and good friend Jacqueline Smith.  Family time with his three girls was his life.  He spent many long hours working to support his family, but always found the time to spend with each one of them.  He was a loving and caring father who will be greatly missed by his daughters.   During his retirement years Jimmy found great joy in strolling through antique stores and refinishing furniture.  He was a frequent participant and well known  at antique auctions in the area.  It was, however, in 1995 that his true joy in life came to being - his grandchildren.  Jimmy was best known as "Poppi", the loving and doting grandfather to his six grandchildren: Connor, Julia, Gabrielle, Maxwell, Jillian and Adam.  At times Poppi could be more of a child than the children.  He played with each child for endless hours, going to every sporting and dance event, showing interest in their lives and always expressing his love for each and every one of them.  If his life's accomplishments were measured in love, his life would be immeasurable!  He is an example of a true believer and we will forever believe in him and his love for all of his family and friends. Jimmy was a fighter for life and lived each moment to the fullest.  In 2005, he survived a long, difficult battle with cancer.  He is a true example of the power of faith, courage and determination.  We will forever be inspired by his fight and his will to live.        Jimmy is survived by his three daughters:  Suzanne M. and her husband David R. Casa of Wakefield, MA; Maureen A. and her husband Mark J. Donahue of Hampstead, NH; Laurie F. and her husband Christopher D. Palermo of Franklin, MA; six grandchildren: Connor F. and Julia E. Lauderdale, FL; Patrick J. Smith and his wife Joan of Rockville, MD; Kathleen A. and her husband David Burke of Methuen, MA; Barbara J. and her husband William Casey of Mashpee, MA; Cornelius J. Smith and his wife Annemarie of Methuen, MA; Mary-Ellen and her husband Richard Beshara of Methuen, MA; Richard J. Smith and his wife Maureen of Atkinson, NH; Francis A. Carrie Lou Branam, 90, of Irwin County, died Wednesday, December 5, 2007 at her residence. Funeral services will be held 3:00 P.M., Friday, December 7, at Abba Baptist Church with Rev. Frances M. (Wray) Beeley,  88, of Salem, NH, died Tuesday at Holy Family Hospital in Methuen, MA. She was born and educated in Lawrence, MA, graduating from Lawrence High School. She was a resident of Salem for over 60 years, formerly of Methuen. Frances was a retired director of the lunch programs for the Salem and Atkinson, NH Schools and had worked in the school food service program for over 29 years. She was State Legislative Chairman and State Treasurer for the NH School Food Service Association. Frances was a member of the First Congregational Church in Salem, where she was a former Sunday school teacher, youth group advisor and a former deaconess. She was a member of the Philathia class. Frances volunteered for the American Red Cross and the March of Dimes. She enjoyed cooking and was famous at Salem High School for her chocolate fudge cake. She was predeceased by her husband of 63 years Peter Beeley. Survived by her Son: Peter Beeley Jr. & wife Nancy of Salem, NH Daughter: Carol Southwick of Salem, NH Grandchildren: Stacey Beeley, Peter Joseph Beeley & Erin Clancy, Amanda Beeley and Deanna & her husband Donald Johnson Great Grandsons: Donald Jr., Tyler, & Nicholas Johnson and Peter Daniel Beeley Many Nieces & Nephews Funeral services will be held Friday, Dec. 7th at 10:00 AM at Douglas & Johnson Funeral Home, 214 Main St, Salem, NH. Jean P. Pelletier, a Veteran of both WW II and Korea passed away, after a long illness, at the Cedar Glen Nursing Facility in Danvers on Monday morning, December 3, 2007.  He was 89 years old.  Jean was born, raised, and educated in Salem. He was the son of the late Odias and Delia Pelletier.  In the beginning of WW II he supported the war effort by working at Bethlehem Steel Company in Quincy as a Marine Machinist.  He was very proud to have worked on the construction of the Battleship USS Massachusetts.  He joined the Army as a member of Company A of the 49th Armored Infantry Battalion spending 13 months in Germany, Holland and France.  He returned to Salem and opened North Shore Market with his brother-in-law.  He was called back to serve in the Army during the Korean War.  He was the recipient of the Good Conduct Medal, American Campaign Medal, WW II Victory Medal, National Defense Medal, and the Korean War Medal.  He was very proud of his service to his country.     Jean owned North Shore Dredging from 1958 to 1978.  He had extensive knowledge of the waters surrounding Salem.  Dredging, hauling cargo, building piers were just some of the types of projects he worked on.  Jean was a member of the Salem Knights of Columbus and was extremely active in Salem politics.  He was a strong supporter of the Shaughnessy Hospital and served as the Chairman of the Board of Trustees.     Family was a very important part of Jean’s life. John C. Cucinotta...formerly of Tewksbury, retired from Hanscom A.F.B.; age 87 Virginia Beach, VA - John C. Cucinotta, age 87, a former resident of Tewksbury, died Monday, December 3, at the home of his daughter in Virginia Beach, after a lengthy battle with heart disease. He was the husband of the late Philomena "Fran" (Mascetta) Cucinotta, who died in November, 1996.He was born in Messina, Italy, on September 28, 1920, a son of the late Carmelo and Antonina (Trimarchi) Cucinotta. He emigrated to the U.S. in his infancy, and his family settled in Chelsea, where he graduated from Chelsea High School. He also attended classes at Boston University.During WW2, he served in the U.S. Army, in the European Theater, including the Battle of the Bulge, and was honorably discharged as a Staff Sergeant.He moved to South Amos Street in Tewksbury in 1956, where he lived until moving to Virginia Beach in 2005.He retired from Hanscom Air Force Base in 1978, after a distinguished civil service career in the logistics field and was later employed by the Boeing Company of Wichita, KS from 1980 to 1983.Mr. Cucinotta was a former communicant of St. William's Church in Tewksbury, where he was a member of the St. William's Men’s Club, and a former member of the Wilmington Son’s of Italy.He is survived by a daughter and son-in-law, Ann Marie Shannon and her husband John of Virginia Beach; one son, Carmen Cucinotta of Tewksbury; a sister, Marguerite Frassica of Reading, MA; a sister-in-law, Mary Cucinotta of Lady Lakes, FL; two grandsons, Brian Shannon and his wife Beth of Alexandria, VA, and Christopher Cucinotta of CA; a great granddaughter, Rebecca Ann Shannon of Alexandria, VA; and many nieces and nephews.He was also the brother of the late Anthony Cucinotta.The family would like to thank Dr. Barbara Parks for her dedicated and compassionate care of our Dad since the first day we met on our flight from Boston. She has been a vital part of his excellent medical care here in Virginia Beach. Our sincere gratitude to Sentara Hospice and their team of nurses, aides, chaplain and social workers. A special thank you to the health aides from Elder Care who cared for Dad with love and respect and supported us during the most difficult of times.His Funeral Mass was celebrated, Monday, December 10, in St. William's Church, Tewksbury.  Burial with Army honors followed at St. Mary's Cemetery, No. Tewksbury.  Memorials to the St. Jude Children's Research Hospital, P.O. Box 50, Memphis, TN  38101 will be appreciated.

Arthur F. Fonseca, 99, a resident of Winchester for over 50 years, passed away at Life Care of Stoneham on Dec. 2.  Born and raised in Cambridge, he was the son of the late Jessie and Rose (Bernade) Fonseca.  Arthur was employed as a newspaper printer with the Hearst Corp. for over 47 years with the Record American newspaper, retiring in 1973.  He was active in the Jenks Senior Center in Winchester, where he enjoyed membership on the Recreation Committee.  Arthur was always interested in art, and during retirement, he pursued this interest further, participating in art classes at the Jenks Center for over 10 years.  He also took art classes at the DeCordova Museum and in the Adult Education program at Winchester High.  He converted a room in his home into an art studio, where he spent many hours painting oil portraits, landscapes and seascapes.  He also enjoyed gardening, and sailing.  He always loved the water and got his first boat at the age of 16.  Arthur was predeceased by his wife, Helen (Palladino) and daughter Rosemary Fonseca.  He was also the brother of the late Joe, Jack, Manny, Tony, Jesse, Annie, Mary, Ollie and Dorothy.  Several nieces and nephews survive Arthur. In lieu of flowers donations in his memory may be made to the Jenks Senior Center, 109 Skillings Rd., Winchester, MA 01890.

William Chester Ahlman, died Sunday at the Catholic Medical Center, Manchester, NH. He was born November 2, 1946 at Clover Hill Hospital in Lawrence. He was the first son born to Edith E. Butman and Richard W. Ahlman. He resided in North Salem his entire life.  Billy was a Marine Corp Vietnam Vet who fought on the front lines of the war. He was shot three times eventually being sent home with an honorable discharge and two Purple Hearts. He came home a different man with shrapnel lodged near his spine and a different outlook on life. The memories of Vietnam took a toll on him as he was a quiet man and didn't like to talk about it. He worked as a heavy equipment operator for Brox Construction for several years and eventually retired.  He married Lorraine Keating, having three children with her: Glen, Richard and Darlene. He and Lorraine eventually divorced but he always remained in NorthSalem in the same house they shared. His children came and went to live with him as did anyone else who might have hit hard times. Bill would always welcome them. Billy's passion was race cars. He was an avid NASCAR fan and even co-managed a super-modified race car with his brother Pete. Even as a young boy he and his brothers and friends made a "figure 8" racetrack on a part of their parents' many acres of land and raced junk cars around for hours. They tinkered with them and learned cars inside out. Billy was that rare person who trusted everyone and welcomed them into his home. Anyone who was down on his luck and needed a place to stay; Billy's door was always open. Bill was predeceased by his brothers Richard, David, and sister Nancy He will be truly missed by all his family and friends. He is survived by his Daughter Darlene & her Husband David Coleman of Hillsboro, NH, Sons: Glenn Ahlman of Salem, NH and Richard Ahlman of Hillsboro, NH.  sister Arlene Knope and her husband Kenny,  a brother Peter Ahlman and his wife Jayne, a sister Barbara Marcotte and her husband James, a sister Martha Ahlman, and sister Shirley Contarino and her husband Michael. He leaves several nieces, nephews, cousins, and many many friends, including his brothers and sisters from the Derry-Salem Elks Lodge #2226. Funeral services will be held Thursday, Dec. 6 at 10 AM at Douglas & Johnson Funeral Home, 214 Main St, Salem, NH. Burial will follow in Mt. Pleasant Cemetery,  No. Salem, NH. Calling hours will be held Wednesday, Dec. 5 from 4-8 PM at the funeral home. To send a message of condolence to the family, please view the obituary at www.douglasandjohnson.com

Funeral services for Gladys Gardner Wood, 90, of 591 North Mattie Ave., Sycamore will be held Wednesday, December 5, 2007 at 11:00 A.M. at the Bethel Baptist Church with burial to follow in the Bethel Cemetery.  Mrs. Wood died Sunday at her residence following an extended illness. Married for almost 61 years until the death of her beloved husband Howell Wood in 1999, she was born on a farm in Thomas County, Georgia, near Coolidge as the youngest of 11 children and was the daughter of the late Zollie Coffer & Lizzie Louvenia Braddy Garnder.She loved her home and the life on the farm and was a major contributor to the success of the family farm that she and her husband farmed near Sycamore, Georgia, since 1949. She was a faithful member of Bethel Baptist Church for most of her married life and was a member of the Lydia Sunday School Class. Her children attribute much of their drive and determination that have made them successful in their professional lives to the lessons learned from their mother. She worked in the fields and in the home in order to make her contributions to the family and to the success of the farm business. Her home was the gathering point for members of her family as nieces and nephews stopped by to visit and for members of the community as they came by to check on her and to stop for a visit as well. She became “Aunt Gladys” to sons and daughters of friends, then to grandsons and granddaughters of the same friends and then to their children. It was often remarked that it was amazing how many nieces and nephews she had but they were the ones who had “adopted” her as their “Aunt Gladys”.   Mrs. Wood is survived by her children, Bill Wood, Portsmouth, Virginia, Louvenia Graham, Hahira, Ga., Helen Copelan, Smyrna, Ga.. and Kaye Lunsford, Jacksonville, Florida.  She is also survived by six grandchildren, six great grandchildren, and one sister, Louise Sinclair, Pavo, Ga. In lieu of flowers, for those who wish please make donations to Hospice of Tiftarea, Bethel Baptist Church, or the American Cancer Society.  The family will receive friends Tuesday from 5:00-7:00 P.M. at Perry Funeral Chapel.

Dr. John K. “Jack” Boyle, 77, of Smithfield, RI passed away on December 1, 2007 at the Philip Hulitar Inpatient Center in Providence, RI after a courageous battle with Parkinson’s disease.  He was the devoted husband of Kathryn A. (Riedl) Boyle to whom he was married for 55 years.  He was the son of the late Thomas E. Boyle and Florence G. (Carberry) Boyle of Worcester, Mass. His father was a high school physics and radio teacher and laboratory assistant at Clark University to Dr. Robert Goddard, United States rocket researcher. Dr. Boyle leaves five children and twelve grandchildren:  Kevin Boyle and his wife Dorothy of Concord, MA and their children Joseph, Patrick and Bartholomew Boyle; Thomas E. “Tim” Boyle, II of Cranston, RI, and his children Brittany and Scott Boyle; Michael J. Boyle and his wife Mary of Smithfield, RI and their children Michael, John, and Nicole Boyle; Elizabeth Boyle Eckel and her husband Jonathan of Westerly, RI and their son Jonathan Eckel, Jr.; and Susan E. Wild and her husband Robert of North Providence, RI and their children Brett, Cody, and Kasey Wild. A native of Worcester, Mass, Dr. Boyle was a four-star athlete at North High School earning that school’s top athletic award upon graduation in 1949. Dr. Boyle graduated from Dartmouth College in 1953, majoring in history and economics and was a member of the football and basketball teams.  At Dartmouth, he was a member of Phi Gamma Delta fraternity and the Sphinx Senior Honor Society.  He earned a Master’s degree from Harvard University in 1956 and a certificate of advance graduate studies from Harvard in 1960.  Dr. Boyle earned a doctorate degree in education from Nova University in 1980.  A life-long educator, Dr. Boyle began his career as a substitute teacher in Newton, MA, and later served as a history teacher, as well as football and track coach at Concord (MA) High School. He served as Assistant Superintendent of Schools in Stoneham, MA for three years, before moving to Smithfield, RI in 1963 to become Superintendent of Smithfield Public Schools, a position he held for 25 years.  During his tenure in Smithfield, he supervised the construction and renovation of six schools, including Smithfield’s first high school and new middle school.  Upon his retirement in 1988, the town named the Boyle Athletic Complex on the Smithfield High School campus in his honor. He was inducted into the Town of Smithfield Heritage Hall of Fame in 2001. He served as President of the Rhode Island and New England Superintendent’s Association, and was named Rhode Island Superintendent of the Year in 1988.  He was a member of the American Association of School Superintendent’s and the IDMADT organization. He was also a member and held office in the Rhode Island Business Officials Association.  He was instrumental in establishing the Northern Rhode Island Collaborative consisting of nine communities and the beginning phase of the Age of Technology in Schools.  Dr. Boyle served in Korea as a Second Lieutenant and platoon leader with the First Marine Division of the United States Marine Corps receiving the National Defense Service Medal, the Korean Service Medal and the United Nations Service Medal. An avid sports fan, Dr. Boyle enjoyed coaching youth sports and was a Providence College hockey enthusiast.  He enjoyed reading, gardening, cooking, and playing cribbage. Most of all, he enjoyed spending time with his children and grandchildren.  He’ll be forever remembered for telling great stories and jokes, reading books and playing ball with his family, and camping and building sandcastles at the Cape Cod.   In lieu of flowers, friends and family may make donations to the Dr. John K. Boyle Scholarship Fund c/o Citizen’s Bank, 639 Putnam Pike, Smithfield, RI  02828. Funeral Wednesday at8:30am from the Anderson-Winfield Funeral Home, Route 44 at Greenville Common, Greenville. Mass of Christian Burial at 9:30am  in St. Philip Church, 622 Putnam Pike, Smithfield. Visitation Tuesday 4-7pm. If you need a ride to the funeral or the visitation please call Anderson-Winfield Chauffeured Services at 401 949 5557
