A. Seymour Parker, M.D. 	A. Seymour Parker of Needham, formerly of Wellesley Hills, died on November 20 at North Hill. 	Born in Buffalo, New York on March 20, 1912, he was raised in Washington, D. C.  He graduated from Gonzaga High School in 1930, from Georgetown University with a B.A. degree in 1934 and from Georgetown University Medical School in 1938.  After an internship and residency in medicine, pediatrics and obstetrics at St. Elizabeth's Hospital in Youngstown, Ohio, he completed a fellowship at the Lahey Clinic in Boston. 	Dr. Parker served on the staff of the Lahey Clinic for thirty-seven years in the Department of Internal Medicine, sub-specializing in endocrinology.During this time he was also a staff member in internal medicine at the New England Baptist, New England Deaconess, Corey Hill and Brooks hospitals. 	He was a Board Member of the Department of Internal Medicine, The American College of Physicians, The American Diabetic Association, The New England Allergy Association, The American Clinical Research and The New England Allergy Society. 	Following his retirement from the Lahey Clinic in 1977, Dr. Parker established a part-time practice in internal medicine, allergy and dermatology in Peabody.  He retired in October, 1988. 	Dr. Parker spent twelve years as a coach and manager in the Little League and Babe Ruth leagues in Wellesley and was active in the Wellesley football leagues. 	He is survived by his wife, Margaret Lovering; one daughter, Anne P.Stommes and her husband, Leon, of Framingham; three sons, Stephen L. Parker and his wife, Linda of Sudbury; David R. Parker and his wife, Karen, of Claremont, NH; Dr. James Parker and his wife, Leah, of Sudbury; five grandchildren, Benjamin and his wife Tracey, Stacey, Andrew, Madeline and Katharine; and two great grandchildren, Logan and Mason.  He was the brother of the late Mildred P. Dougherty. A funeral Mass will be held on Saturday, November 24 at 12 noon at St. Paul's Church in Wellesley. Relatives and friends kindly invited. Interment in Woodlawn Cemetery, Wellesley. 	In lieu of flowers, memorial contributions may be made to The Lahey Clinic,41 Mall Road, Burlington, MA 01805-0105 or to the Cystic Fibrosis Foundation, 220 North Main Street, Suite 104, Natick, MA 01760.

Sister Eliane Lemieux, PM, (Sr. Louise de Ste Marie) 92, of the Sisters of the Presentation of Mary, St. Joseph Residence, Manchester, died at her residence on November 20th, 2007.	Sister Eliane was born on February 10, 1915, at St-Edouard-des Mechins, PQ, Canada.  She was the daughter of the late Philippe and Marie-Louise (Crousset-Harrison) Lemieux.  She entered the Presentation of Mary Congregation in St. Hyacinthe, PQ, in 1934.  Sister Eliane made her first Profession in 1936 and her final Profession in 1941.  Two years later, 1943, she became an American citizen.	She received her bachelor’s degree in education from Rivier College in Nashua, NH.  	Sister Eliane was an educator serving as a teacher in the New Hampshire Catholic schools.  She taught many years in the North Country in Berlin and Gorham, also in Concord, Hudson, and Manchester.  She was transferred to Rhode Island and taught in Artic and Phenix.  She taught the CCD program in both the Berlin and Manchester schools.  She semi-retired in 1981 to undertake podiatry and served the community as podiatrist until her retirement in 1996.	Family members include her brother, Roland Lemieux of Berlin, CT; nieces, nephews, and cousins. 	A Mass of Christian burial was celebrated on Saturday November 24, 2007 in the Resurrection Chapel.  Burial followed in Presentation of Mary Cemetery, Hudson.

Delaney Elias Casey, 72 succumbed to cancer on Tuesday morning, November 20, 2007 at his home in Huntsville.  "Ben" as he was fondly known by family and friends, was born February 4, 1935 in Asheville, North Carolina to Estus and DeRosette Casey.  He was raised in Cullowhee and graduated from the all-black public school, Central Consolidated High in Sylva. He attended Johnson C. Smith University in Charlotte, NC for two years, before being drafted into the US Army.  During the Vietnam Conflict, he was a reporter for THE STARS AND STRIPES newspaper in Germany and the Armed Forces Network in South Korea. In 1985, Ben retired from the military after 26 years with the rank of sergeant major.  After retiring from the Army, Ben worked for radio stations WAHR as News Director and WEUP (AM) as host to "Community Focus". On December 5, 1970, he was united in Holy Matrimony to the former Ezella Smith and became the proud father of Joseph Nobles (deceased) and Yolanda Nobles-McCoy.  Ben was an elder at First Seventh-day Adventist Church in Huntsville, where he served as a deacon for several years.  He also headed a prison ministry.  After losing his eye sight to glaucoma, he became an activist for those with disabilities.  He served as president of the local chapter of the National Federation of the Blind; a member of the Board of Directors of Technology Assistance for Special Consumers and the Children's Advocacy Center Advisory Council. Delaney "Ben" Casey's memory will live on in the hearts of his beloved and devoted wife, Ezella; a daughter, Yolanda McCoy; one granddaughter, Dianna Lynn Nobles-Stewart (Rico); one great-grandson, DeMario Nobles, all of Huntsville, Alabama; one brother, Horace Casey of Greenville, NC; five sisters: Geraldyne Casey of Ashville, NC, Lorinda Casey, Cullowhee, NC, Victoria Casey McDonald of Sylva, NC, Floretta Graham of Greenville, NC and Irma Casey of Kindton, NC; and a host of other relatives and friends.  Funeral services for Mr. Delaney "Ben" Casey will be held at 11:00 a.m. on Monday, November 26, 2007 from the First Seventh-day Adventist Church with Pastor Benjamin Jones officiating. Interment will be in Valley View Memorial Gardens.

John E. “Jack” Slack, Jr., 76, of Salem, passed away on Monday after an extended battle with cancer. He was the husband of Virginia Cable (Lee) Slack. Jack was the son of the late John E. Slack, Sr., and the late Mildred F. (McInis) Slack. He was born in Ancon in the Canal Zone while his father was working in Panama. Upon returning to Massachusetts during the Depression, he lived in the Boston area in Milton and Brighton and was a graduate of Boston Latin High School. After graduating, Jack enlisted in the United States Air Force and served honorably in Seoul during the Korean War. After returning from war, he got an Associates Degree in Engineering from Boston University. He subsequently worked on the Jupiter missile program in Turkey and Italy. Later in life, Jack changed professions and entered the world of finance. He became a stockbroker and a business journalist. He wrote for the Boston Business Journal and the Boston Herald, and was a radio personality on WBZ and WEEI. He interviewed important figures of the day including President Ronald Reagan. In the 1980s, he hosted a Sunday morning talk show as WEEI’s Business Editor prior to the station’s change to an all-sports format. After retiring from the media, Jack worked as the accounts manager for the Learning Tree Store in Danvers and Stoneham. He was also very active in the Catholic Church. He became a Eucharistic minister and volunteered at Shaughnessy-Kaplan Rehabilitation Hospital in Salem. He also served as associate director of the North Shore Christian Network, helped run Alpha courses on the North Shore, and supported the efforts of St. Joseph Food Pantry in Salem. Jack is survived by his daughters Ann Gold of Acton; Paula Slack of Marblehead; Maura Madden of Newton; Shawna Slack-Watkins, who currently lives in Switzerland; and son Eric Slack of Beverly. He also leaves behind five grandchildren and a large extended family. Friends and families are invited to a celebration of his life with a Memorial Mass at Immaculate Conception, 15 Hawthorne Blvd. in Salem, on Friday, November 23 at 10 a.m. In lieu of flowers, donations in his memory may be made to either the St. Joseph Food Pantry, 15 Hawthorne Blvd. in Salem, or Hospice of the North Shore, 10 Elm St. in Danvers.

AUBURN-Margaret Anne (Beatty) Carberry passed away on Monday, November 19th at her home in Auburn.  Earlier this month she happily and heartily celebrated her 87th birthday.   Born in Worcester on November 5th, 1920, Peg was the only surviving child of H. Ward Beatty and Marion Beatty, and she was truly beloved by them.  After graduating from Becker College and working for a time with her father in his Dental Laboratory, she married Donald Edward Carberry, an officer in the U.S. Air Force, just after the Second World War and gave birth to two children, Don and Peggy.  For more than forty years she made her home in West Tatnuck, and she worked at Memorial Hospital for some twenty years as an Emergency Ward clerk, before retiring to care for her mother.  Peg was a truly unique individual.  Right up until the day she died, she had a quick and clever wit, a tremendous sense of humor, and a deeply devoted love for her family.  As a grandmother, she didn’t knit; she didn’t bake cookies—in truth, she would have been the first to tell you that she couldn’t cook—but she was a die-hard Boston sports fanatic, a lover of action movies, and reveled in her Irish heritage.  Receiving the gift of an up-right piano at the age of 7, she kept it with her always and continued to play it year after year.  Her favorite song to play was “Danny Boy.”  In her youth, Margaret loved to swim in Brooks Pond in Spencer, and just this past summer, she donned a new bathing suit and swam in the pool at her home.  When she lowered herself into the water, she said “Glory be to God!”  Peg – or “Gram”, as she was affectionately known for more than 37 years – will be greatly missed by her son Donald and daughter Peggy, as well as by her daughter-in-law Gail, her cousin and best friend Ruth Knott, her grandchildren Donny, Colin, Catherine Erin, Alison, Laurel, Galen and Nolan, her many great-grandchildren, and especially her first great-grandson, Brendan, who very much enjoyed living with her in this, her last year. The funeral is Friday, November 23rd, from O’CONNOR BROTHERS FUNERAL HOME, 592 Park Avenue, Worcester, with a Mass at 11:00 A.M. in St. Peter’s Church, 931 Main Street, Worcester.  Burial will be in St. John’s Cemetery, Worcester.  A visiting hour is from 9:30 until 10:30 A.M. Friday in the funeral home.  In lieu of flowers, contributions may be made to the Amy H. Carberry Scholarship Fund, c/o Quinsigamond Community College, 670 West Boylston Street, Worcester, MA 01606.

Thurston Fuller Ackerman of Medford, beloved husband of Margaret (Albo) Ackerman, died at the Lawrence Memorial Hospital in Medford on November 18, 2007. He was 87 years old.He was born on March 22, 1920, in Boston, Massachusetts and was a beloved son of the late Thurston Fuller, Sr. and Margaret (Myers) Ackerman. Mr. Ackerman was a graduate of Medford High School, class of 1939 and a graduate of Hillyer College in Hartford, Connecticut. Mr. Ackerman entered the U. S. Army on July 20, 1942, at Ft. Devens, Massachusetts, and served as Tech. Sgt. During World War II in the Aleutian Islands and in New Guinea. While serving in the Army, Mr. Ackerman worked as a Radio Repaiman and was a qualified Sharpshooter. After receiving his honorable discharge on December 2, 1945, from Fort Devens, he was awarded the Good Conduct Medal, Victory Medal, Asiatic Pacific Theater Campaign Ribbon and the American Campaign Ribbon. Mr. Ackerman then worked for GE for more than 34 years.He was a lifelong member of the North Street Union Congregational Church of Medford and served as teacher and superintendent of the Sunday School and was a long time member of the Diaconate Board, Clerk and served as Moderator and a member of many other committees of the church that is now known as the North Prospect Union United Church of Christ. He was also the former assistant of Scoutmaster Troop 6 in Medford and Scoutmaster of Troop 1 in Springfield. Mr. Ackerman was Past Master of the Brookline Masonic Lodge, a member of the Aleppo Shriners Temple and member of the Board of Directors of AARP Chapter #1132 of Medford. He was also a licensed amateur radio operator and computer enthusiast as well as a member of the Arlington Seniors Computer Club.Besides his beloved wife, Margaret, of more than 65 years, he will be sorely missed by his loving children; Robert A. Ackerman of Annapolis, MD., Karl B. Ackerman of Medford and Susan L. Ackerman of New York City, cherished grandchildren; Jared E. Ackerman of Medford, Rebecca A. Ackerman of Boston and Michael D. Ackerman of Cambridge, and devoted brother; Robert Arthur Ackerman and dear nephews Eric Ackerman and Konrad Ackerman of Washington, DC.His funeral service was celebrated in the North Prospect Union United Church of Christ, 144 North Street, Medford, Monday, November 26, 2007, at 11 AM. Visitation was Monday, prior to the service, in the Church, from 10 to 11 AM. Interment was private. In lieu of flowers, donations in Thurston’s memory may be made to the above named church, or to the charity of your choice. For additional information, please visit www.magliozzifuneralhome.com.

Charles W. Horton, Jr. .....well known local contractor, age 56TEWKSBURY- Charles W. Horton, Jr., 56, a well known local contractor, passed away onSunday, Nov. 18th after a long courageous battle with cancer,  surrounded by his loving family. 	He was the beloved husband of Ruth (Cox) Horton of Lowell formerly of Tewksbury, towhom he was married for 36 years this past Feb. 19th. 	He was born in Arlington on August 16, 1951, a son of the late Charles and Rose(Bradley) Horton formerly of Tewksbury.  	He was educated in Tewksbury schools and started his career as a cement form worker.He then went on to frame houses and was the proud owner of three businesses during his career. In time he physically built three homes in Tewksbury for his family.  He also built an addition tohis home so that he could take care of his in laws, the late Ruth and Thomas Cox, formerly ofLowell.	Because he was known for his strong work ethics and his pride in his work, he was thepreferred contractor to several area home builders. It was Charlie they called when building theirown residences.   	In is spare time, he loved to be out on the ocean,  fishing,  or spending time with hisfamily.  He had recently purchased a vacation home in Florida and was looking forward towintering there with his wife and dear friends.	His fondest memories were his winter vacations with his wife and sister-in-law andbrother-in-law, Sharon and Michael Decato from York, Maine.	In addition to his wife, he leaves two daughters and a son.  Laurie Higgins of Lowell,Sherri Horton and Marcos Sodre of Pepperell, and Charles T. Horton of Tewksbury.  He alsoleaves his three grandchildren who were his pride and joy. Billy, Ryan and Katie.  He had twosisters and two brothers-in-law.  Donna and Gary Large of Florida, Charlean Horton of Pelhamand Thomas Cox of Dracut.  He is also survived by several aunts, uncles, cousins, nieces,nephews and two grandnieces.  He will be sadly missed by his family and friends.  	His Funeral Service was held Monday, Nov. 26, in the Funeral Home.  Burial  followed  at Lowell Cemetery.  It was his wish that donations in his name be sent to the“Jimmy Fund” c/o Dana Farber Development Office 10 Brookline Place West 6Th Floor BrooklineMA 02445, or the Mass General Hospital Development Office, Pediatric Cancer Research Fund,  165 Cambridge St. Suite 600 Boston MA 02114.

She was also honored this yearby the Commonwealth of Massachusetts with the “Good Neighbor” award.She worked tirelessly in her job, and was also a valuable volunteer for many organizations andcharities including the North Shore Village Association, N.E.A.R., for whom she was a memberof the Board of Directors,( R.C.S.) Powers a longtime Realtor, Construction /Developer of Whitman .   Born in Bellows Falls, VT, in 1927, he was schooled and educated in Alstead, NH, attended University of New Hampshire, graduated from the School of Accounting and Finance in Manchester, NH.Joined the U.S Navy in 1944, discharged in 1945, moved to Cambridge in 1948.He was Chief Accountant for Cambridge Electric Light & Steam Corporation form 1948-1959.In 1959, went with the State Convention as Business Administrator for the American Baptist Churches of Massachusetts, 21 years and for those number of years, was instrumental in the purchasing and development of “Grotonwood” Camp & Conference Center, in Groton, MA. and the  merger of Tabcomb /City Society, Oceanwood Conference Center in Maine, also the developing of “Evanswood” in Kingston. Mr. Rhoades was a two time President of the Boston Social Union, treasurer of Lord’s Day League , comptroller/treasurer of State Convention, Boston, MA., Active in State wide work of his boards and committees, from the state level to local. He has been a mason of the Puritan Lodge since 1955, a lifetime active member of the First Baptist Church of Whitman, member of Lord’s Day league for Civic Welfare, the Republican National Task Force, Congressional & Senatorial of Washington, D. C. In addition to his wife, he is survived by his two daughters, Dianne and Donna Rhoades both of Edgartown, Martha’s Vineyard.  Friends are kindly invited to his funeral services on Friday December 7th at 12:00 P. M. in the Blanchard Funeral Chapel, Inc, Plymouth Street (Rt 58 at the Rotary Circle), Whitman, MA. Interment will follow in the Mt. Zion Cemetery, Whitman. Visiting hours will be held two hours prior to service, Friday December 7th 10:00 A.M. – 12:00 P.M.

Mother Minnie Briggs Davis was born June 30, 1922 to the late Freddie Briggs and Emma Gertrude Harris Briggs in Madison County, Alabama.  She made her transition from this life on Saturday, November 17, 2007 at the Huntsville Hospital surrounded by her loved ones. Affectionately known as “Sistah”, Mother Davis was preceded in death by her brothers (Freddie Jr., Sam, Otha, and Eugene Briggs) and her sisters (Lillie Mae Briggs, Violet Edwards, Paralee Briggs and Everlou Turner). Her oldest daughter, Bessie Mae Readus, also preceded her in death.  She was married to the late Marion Davis.   Mother Davis had a very full life. She was a domestic for many years and took joy and pride in all of her work endeavors. One of her greatest loves was her church and church family. She has been a member of St. Mark Church for more than forty years under pastors Reverend W. A. Donaldson and Reverend John Clay. Mother Davis served on the Mothers Board and previously served as president and treasurer. She was also a member of the Sisters Aid Society, the Senior Citizens Group, Day Care Center Volunteer, and a very devoted and dedicated Sunday School scholar. One of her proudest moments was receiving perfect attendance for the year in Sunday School in 2001. Mother Davis was also a member of Maple Chapter #88 of the Order of Eastern Star. A loving caretaker who assisted in the care of her parents, in-laws, children and siblings, Mother Davis enjoyed cooking and feeding people. We will always have memories of those wonderful sweet potato pies that no one has been able to duplicate or those magical butter rolls. With food as in life, she had a way of taking very little and making it very special.  Mother Minnie Briggs Davis leaves to cherish her memory a devoted daughter, Juanita (Howard) Townsend of Huntsville, AL; devoted sons: J. D. (Geraldine) Briggs of Huntsville, AL, Larry Donnell Davis of Washington, DC, and James Earl Davis of Philadelphia, PA; grandchildren: Charlie Connell (Paula) Davis, Alcinda (David) Cox, Alphonso (Alice) Readus, Howard Ray (Bonnie) Townsend, Bobby (Shirley) Readus, Sharon Harris, Yvonne (Keith) Taylor, Frederick Craig Readus, Tiffany High, and Da’nate (Demetrius) Townsend; twenty great grandchildren; three great-great grandchildren; a brother, Thomas Briggs, Sr.; a sister-in-law, Omega Davis Jordan of Chattanooga, TN; and a host of nieces, nephews, cousins and friends.  Funeral services for Mrs. Minnie Davis will be held at 1200 Noon, Friday November 23, 2007 from the St. Mark Baptist Church with Rev. John Clay officiating.  Interment will be in Valhalla Memory Gardens.  The remains of Mrs. Davis will be in the church one hour prior to funeral time.

Frederick A. Bauman Jr., 84 of Ipswich died November 17 in his son’s home in Medway. Mother Elizabeth Hayes was born April 13, 1908, in Huntsville, Alabama, to the late Add White and Lizzie Pruitt White.   She resided in Huntsville until 1997.  Due to illness, she relocated to Pontiac, Michigan. There she resided nine years until her prayer was answered in March 2007, to return home to her beloved city, family, and place of worship. She proudly boasted of her membership to St. James Primitive Baptist Church, to which she joined at 15 years of age.  She faithfully served on the Mothers’ Board, and Sisters’ Aide Society. She possessed “undeniable” gifts of servitude, cooking, and sewing.  She became a Mother figure to many A&M college students - providing room & board, words of wisdom, hot cooked meals, and her infamous pound cakes. She is lovingly remembered by her family for her overwhelming love and affection.  She was like a gentle breeze on a warm summer’s eve.  She will be greatly missed. Mother Hayes leaves to cherish her memory one daughter, Francida Moore (Michigan); one son, Add Davis (Louisiana); one sister, Tommy Babb (Nashville, TN); six grandchildren: Brenda Moore, Calvin Moore, Janice Moore, Larry Moore, Denise Moore, and Phillip Moore (Michigan); 13 Great-Grandchildren; 16 Great-Great Grandchildren; special friends:  Herbert Gray and Reaver Brown, numerous cousins, nieces, nephews, and a host of loving friends.   Funeral services for Ms. Elizabeth Hayes will be held at 1200 Noon, Saturday, November 24, 2007 from St. James Primitive Baptist Church with Rev. Billy Jones officiating.  Interment will be in Valhalla Memory Gardens. The remains of Ms. Hayes will be in the church one hour prior to funeral time.

Leeman, 77 of Millis and formerly of Franklin died November 16 in Caritas Norwood Hospital.Born in Worcester, March 4, 1930 the son of the late Frederick and Ruth (Martin) Leeman, Mr. Leeman was raised in Worcester and had lived for many years in Franklin before moving to Millis 15 years ago.Mr. Leeman had been a meat cutter for Star Market in Norwood and prior to that in Roxbury.Mr. Leeman served in the Air Force during the Korean War attaining the rank of Airman First Class.He is survived by his son, Wayne Leeman of Harrisville, Rhode Island, his daughters, Karen Woods of Franklin and Deborah Thomsen of Papillion, Nebraska.He is also survived by his grandchildren, Leslie, Michelle, Christopher, Christina and Connor and his great-grandchildren, Eric and Brittany.His funeral service will be held on Wednesday, November 21 in the Ginley Funeral Home of Franklin, 131 Main St., Franklin at 10:00 a.m.Burial will in the Massachusetts National Cemetery in Bourne.Calling hours will be on Tuesday from 7-9 p.m.

Frederick D. Barnett, 74, of Ledyard, died November 16, 2007 of cancer.  Fred, known as Phil to family and friends, was born March 30, 1933 in New Haven, Connecticut to Charles R. and Helen Dudley Barnett. He met his wife Janet Holdridge while attending the University of Connecticut.  They were married on June 19, 1955.  His adventurous spirit and love of the outdoors became dominant themes as he and Janet raised their family.  He loved camping, backpacking, canoeing and kayaking, and all of his children and grandchildren learned to share those passions with him.  Among his many accomplishments, he hiked the entire Long Trail in Vermont and much of the Appalachian Trail, and he kayaked the entire Connecticut coastline.  Always searching for adventure, he traveled to 49 states and over 10 countries, making new friends wherever he went. Phil selflessly helped family, friends, and community throughout his life with his unending energy and talents.  As a teenager he earned the rank of Eagle Scout and later served as a Scout leader for many years.  He served his country as a U.S. Marine Corps Lieutenant.  For 27 years he was a Landscape Architect for the State of Connecticut Department of Transportation, leaving his creative mark on miles of state highways.   Above all else he was a devoted and loyal family man, proud of his children and grandchildren.  He will be missed by Janet, his wife of 52 years; their children, David and his wife Eileen of Boxborough, MA, Catherine of East Lyme, Lawrence and Robert of Ledyard, and Mark and his wife Nichole of Harrisburg, NC; his nine grandchildren, Sara, Daniel and Mari Nazzaro, and Jake, Marie, Michelle, Jenny, Molly and Mason Barnett; his twin brother Franklin “Jerry” Barnett of Westerville, OH, and his sister Lorraine Perry Barnett Bashor of Fayetteville, Arizona.  He was predeceased by his brother Charles of Westhampton Beach, NY, and his son Jack.    His family will greet relatives and friends at The Dinoto Funeral Home, 17 Pearl Street, Historic downtown Mystic on Friday from 5-7pm.  Funeral service will be held at the Ledyard Congregational Church on Saturday at 11 am, followed by a celebration of his life with the family in the church fellowship hall.   In lieu of flowers, please remember Phil by taking a walk in the woods or lending a helping hand to a friend. To share a message of remembrance/condolence with his family, please click the link below.

He also leaves behind many, many friends and co-workers that will miss him terribly. Robert touched our lives in ways he could have never imagined and he will be missed deeply. Friends may call at the DRACUT FUNERAL HOME, 2159 Lakeview Avenue, DRACUT on Monday from 5 until 8 PM. Mr. Joseph “DeDe” Sardone, 86 of Medford, formerly of Boston’s North End died on Thursday in Mass. General Hospital following a brief illness. He is the husband of Evelyn Sardone with whom he shared more than 58 years of marriage.Born in Worcester, son of the late Joseph and Carmella (Rango) Sardone. He was raised in Boston’s North End and attended local schools, in addition he went on to a college of culinary arts.A Veteran of WWII, he served in the U.S. Navy. While in the Navy, he began amateur boxing. During that time he became a Golden Gloves Boxing Champion in 1936.  Among his many friends in the fight game were boxing champions Gene Tunney and Jack Dempsey.DeDe was a chef by trade, working in many of the finer establishments throughout greater Boston. He was a member of the D.A.V. Medford post #21 and also was a member of the H.E.P.A. club in Florida. He loved cooking and was an avid Red Sox fan.In addition to his wife, he leaves  a son John Sardone and his wife Barbara of Medford. Loving grandfather of Cara Sardone of Medford.  Brother of Mary Bibo of Braintree, Delores Luongo of Medford and the late Samuel, Francis and Leo Sardone. Funeral from Dello Russo Funeral Home, 306 Main St., MEDFORD Tuesday at 8 am.  followed by a funeral mass celebrated in Immaculate Conception Church, 600 Pleasant Street, Malden, at 9 am. Relatives and friends are respectfully invited to attend. Visiting hours Monday 4 –8 pm. Services will conclude with burial at Oak Grove Cemetery, Medford.

Methuen, Ma. – Patrick J. Perrotta, 94, passed away on November 15, 2007 at the Lawrence General Hospital after a brief illness.Born and educated in Methuen,  Patrick was the son of Joseph and Olympia Perrotta. Patrick was the widower of his beloved wife Ann (Sarcione) Perrotta.After attending Methuen schools, he worked as a welder at the Boston Navy Yard. Later he worked for the United Shoe Machinery Corporation as a machinist. Patrick was honored  as being the longest working employee at that time, having given more than 35 years of service. As a young man, Patrick played a banjo in a local band at nearby clubs. He was an avid sports enthusiast and was a member of an area softball team. Upon Patrick’s retirement he found a late in life passion for golf which he enjoyed with his brothers. He held a membership at Crystal Springs Golf Club.  Patrick’s pride and joy was his wife of 60 years with whom he spent his later years traveling.  Mr. Perrotta was a communicant of Saint Monica’s Church in Methuen where he regularly attended the Saturday 4 o’clock Mass. Patrick is survived by two children; Dennis P. & wife Carol Perrotta of Pelham, NH, and Patricia A. Del Signore, wife of Vincent of Methuen, and brother Albert & wife Rose of Methuen; five grandchildren Debra & husband Jay Setterlund, Julie-Ann & Michael Savoie, Christine & Christopher DeSimone, Cheryl & Brian Haidaichuk, and Steven & wife Melinda Perrotta, and three greatgrandchildren, Jonathan, Christopher, and Owen and several nieces and nephews. Survivors also include three sisters-in-law, Emily Perrotta of California, Ida Perrotta of Salem, NH, and Michelina Perrotta of Windham, NH. A funeral mass will take place on Tuesday morning at 10:30 A.M. in Saint Monica’s Church in Methuen with burial to follow in Bellevue Cemetery of Lawrence. Friends may call on Monday from 3-7 P.M. at the Kenneth H. Pollard Funeral Home, Inc. 233 Lawrence Street in Methuen. Memorial contributions may be made to Saint Monica’s Church Building Fund, 212 Lawrence Street Methuen, Ma. 01844.

Mr. Frederick A. Spires, 70, of Hampstead, New Hampshire, passed away peacefully at his home, Thursday morning, November 15th, 2007.      He was born on December 29th, 1937 in Lawrence, Massachusetts, to the late Frederick F. and Teresa M. (DiLonardo) Spires.  Fred graduated from Central Catholic in Lawrence, Massachusetts,  Class of 53. Fred served in the United States Air Force for over 23 years, including a tour in Vietnam and several European posts. Mr. Spires worked for the United States Postal Service in Lawrence, Massachusetts. He had several hobbies; he enjoyed and was looking forward to pursuing, one of which was golf.  He also wanted to go on a cruise and to Hawaii with his bride.  Unfortunately, because of ill health, that never materialized.  However, he turned to his love of reading and really enjoyed the computer where he routinely kept in touch with his many friends.  He maintained his sense of humor throughout his long battle with cancer and his courage and strength was reinforced by the love and support of his family and loyal friends for which he was so very grateful. He is survived by his wife of 49 years, Jean (Hawthorn) Spires.  He is survived by his children, Mark and wife Susan of Bow, N.H., Kim Borchers and husband Lenny of Windham, NH, Lisa Lombardi of Haverhill, MA. and Lesley J. LaPerle and husband Reginald of Bow, N.H.; brothers:  Kenneth of Seville, OH, and Wayne of Haverhill, MA, sister:   Deborah A. Stanisewski, and nine grandchildren. His funeral mass will be celebrated on Tuesday, November 20th, 2007 at 12:00 noon in Saint Anne's Church in Hampstead, New Hampshire. Burial will take place at a later date. Family and Friends may call on Monday, November 19th, 2007 from 2 to 4 and 6 to 8 at the John Breen Memorial Funeral Home, Inc., 122 Amesbury Street, Lawrence, MA.  To send online condolences and for directions, please go to www.breenfuneralhome.com. Memorial Contributions may be made to the New Hampshire Air Force Memorial Fund, P.O. Box 422, Dover, NH  03821.

SALEM - LCDR Dorothy H. Callahan, US Navy, Retired, age 80, died Thursday evening, November 15, 2007 at the Kaplan Family Hospice House, Danvers following a lengthy illness. Born in Salem, she was the daughter of the late John & Annie (Gallagher) Callahan. Other than when she was in the Navy, she was a lifelong resident of Salem, and parishioner of the St. Thomas The Apostle Church. She attended St. Margaret Hospital School of Nursing in Dorchester and worked as a registered nurse before entering the Navy in August of 1954.  She served as a Nursing Service Supervisor for over twenty years, retiring as a Lt. Commander on November 1, 1974. During her military career, in addition to various stateside assignments, she served in Japan, the Philippines and Italy. Known for her quick wit and fun loving personality, she had a love for travel that she especially enjoyed with her sister Margaret.  In her younger years, she enjoyed golf and won tournaments at the Wenham Country Club.  A devoted fan of the Red Sox and Patriots, she also followed professional golf. Dorothy is survived by 1 Brother, Arthur Callahan, Sr. & his wife Alice of Topsfield, nieces and nephews, Patricia Devoe, James Callahan Jr., Maureen Amenta, Debra Callahan, Donald Callahan, Linda Callahan, Arthur “Chuck” Callahan, Margaret Yonis, several cousins in the US and Ireland and several grand nieces and nephews.  She was the sister of the late James Callahan, Sr., John Callahan, Margaret Callahan, Joseph Callahan and Thomas Callahan. Her funeral service will be held Tuesday, November 20, 2007 at 10 am from the Full-Spychalski Funeral Home, 84 Washington Square East, by Salem Common, Salem, followed by a funeral Mass at 11 AM at the St. Thomas The Apostle Church, Salem.  Visiting hours are Monday, 4 to 7 PM.  Relatives and friends respectfully invited.  Burial is in St. Mary Cemetery, Salem. In lieu of flowers, donations may be made to either the Salvation Army, 93 North St., Salem, MA 01970 or to the Haven From Hunger, 77 Wallis St., Peabody, MA 01960. For on-line guestbook and other information, visit www.SalemFuneral.com.

WORCESTER–Martha Louise (O'Hagan) O'Sullivan, a longtime school teacher, died Wednesday, November 14th, at Tatnuck Park. Her husband, Thomas V. O’Sullivan, Sr. died in 1969. She leaves three daughters,  Dr. Kathleen wife of Dr. Colin Mailer of Victoria, British Columbia, Eileen Stewart and her spouse Sarah Scott of Williamsburg, MA and Margaret O'Sullivan and her husband Dan Carney of Beaufort, S.C; four sons, Dr. Thomas V. O’Sullivan, Jr. of Louisville, KY, John M. and his wife, Mary (Cahill) O’Sullivan of Cambridge and Kevin and his wife, Linda (Turco) O'Sullivan and Michael and his wife, Mary (Moylan) O’Sullivan all of Worcester; five grandchildren, several nephews and two nieces. Her daughter, Maureen, died in 1997, an infant son, Terrence, died in 1944. Her sister, Mary Barbara McCurdy, died in 2005.  Mrs. O'Sullivan was born October 27, 1912, in Boston the daughter of John and Martha (McGrath) O'Hagan and attended Notre Dame Academy in the Fenway and Boston University, where she was president of her senior class. She received her master’s degree in education from Worcester State College. Mrs. O'Sullivan was the typing teacher for many years at the former Providence Street Junior High School in Worcester, retiring in 1980. She began her teaching career in Hardwick, MA and later taught in Simsbury, CT. After marrying, she and her family lived in Boston, Columbus, OH, Chappaqua, NY and Norwalk, CT before settling in Worcester in 1958.  After her retirement, Mrs. O'Sullivan volunteered for many years at Memorial Hospital. A devout Catholic, Mrs. O'Sullivan was a member of Blessed Sacrament parish in Worcester, where she attended Mass daily and once served on the Parish Council. In Worcester, she joined and later served as president of the Worcester Children’s Players; and was a member and past president of the Neried Swim Club at the YWCA. As a teacher, Mrs. O’Sullivan regularly put on student and teacher plays, and once managed a joint production in which her students performed and toured with the Boston Opera Company. She was a great fan of the theater, and often performed in community theater.  She loved to read, being particularly fond of mysteries. She took an active interest in the affairs of the day, and remained interested in politics and current events until shortly before her death. The funeral is Monday, November 19th from O’CONNOR BROTHERS FUNERAL HOME, 592 Park Avenue with a Mass at 11:00 AM in Blessed Sacrament Church, 555 Pleasant Street.  Burial will be in St. John’s Cemetery.  Calling hours are Sunday, November 18th from 4:00 until 7:00 PM in the funeral home.  In lieu of flowers memorial contributions may be made to the E.A.W. Scholarship Fund, 397 Grove Street, Worcester, MA 01605 or Blessed Sacrament Church, 551 Pleasant Street, Worcester, MA  01602.

Emanuel “Muscles” Tsipopoulos, 78, of Manchester, died November 13, 2007, at his home surrounded by family from complications of Parkinson’s Disease. He was born in Manchester, June 19, 1929, to George and Helen (Blagmenos) Tsipopoulos. He grew up in Manchester graduating from Central High School, class of 1947, and later graduated from the Culinary Institute of America in New Haven, CT.  He served in the US Army in Germany during the Korean Conflict. Mr. Tsipopoulos worked in the food service industry in various capacities most of his life. He worked in several restaurants in the New England area including the Wauwinet House on Nantucket Island. Upon returning to Manchester, he was a co-owner of Merrimack Fruit on Elm Street for many years. He retired from Southern NH University where he worked as a chef. During his retirement he ran a private catering business. Manny was an excellent athlete and an avid sports fan throughout his life. He played basketball at Central High School and later coached the youth basketball team at St. George Greek Orthodox Cathedral. He was also an avid golfer and was a member of the Derryfield Country Club. He was dedicated to his family and friends and was loved by many for his upbeat personality, his wonderful sense of humor and his generous heart. Family members include his wife of 50 years, Anastasia (Kourtis) Tsipopoulos of Manchester; two daughters, Helen Minkle and her husband, David, of Manchester and Dorothea Tsipopoulos and her husband, Thomas Kennedy, of Honolulu Hawaii; a sister, Elpeneke (Picky) Bobotas of Manchester; seven nieces and nephews; six godchildren and many grand nieces and grand nephews      The funeral was held Saturday November 17, 2007 at St. George Greek Orthodox Cathedral. Burial followed in Pine Grove Cemetery. Memorial donations may be made to the Visiting Nurse Association, 33 So. Commercial St., Suite 401, Manchester, NH 03101, or to Easter Seals, 555 Auburn St., Manchester, NH 03103.

Gaston R. Vaillancourt, 84, formerly of Whitefield, NH, passed peacefully from this life on Tuesday, November 13, 2007 after a long and very courageous battle with Parkinson’s Disease. Gaston was born in Coaticook, Quebec, on March 15, 1923, son of Emile and Ernestine (Lefebvre) Vaillancourt. He was educated in the Coaticook school system and served in the Royal Canadian Army during WWII.  Following his marriage to Theresa Roy, they moved to the United States in 1948. Settling in Whitefield, he opened Vail’s Jewelry Store. He later opened a second location in Lancaster, splitting his time between the two until closing his Whitefield business. In the evenings, he repaired televisions in customers’ homes then repaired watches into the late hours of the night. He retired in 1998 after 50 years as a successful, respected and very well-liked business man.  Loyal customers will always remember him for “giving them a good deal”. He was known for selling jewelry to children for the change they had in their pockets, especially when they were buying for their mothers. To family and friends, he was known for his wonderful good humor, and his limitless patience and kindness.  He was especially adored for being the perfect grandfather. Members of his family include his wife, Theresa (Roy) Vaillancourt of Florida; two daughters, Susan Vaillancourt and Sylvia Langer, both of Manchester; one son, Michel Vaillancourt of Chapel Hill, NC; two granddaughters, Jessica Langer and Samantha Vaillancourt; two sisters, Theresa McGlathery of Sarasota, FL and Cécile Allard of Coaticook; two brothers, Léon Vaillancourt of Magog and Marcel Vaillancourt of Sherbrooke; nephews, nieces, and cousins. He was predeceased by his son, Jean Paul Vaillancourt and one brother, Jean-Louis Vaillancourt.   A memorial Mass of Christian burial was celebrated in St. Matthew Church, Whitefield, on Saturday November 17, 2007. Urn burial followed in Park Street Cemetery, Whitefield.  In lieu of flowers, donations may be made to the American Parkinson’s Disease Association, Inc. (APDA, Inc.), 135 Parkinson Avenue, Staten Island, NY, 10305-1425.

Marilyn Ruth (Miller) Marsh, 75, died peacefully after a long illness on Monday, November 12, 2007 at her home, surrounded by her loving family.  She is survived by her three children and their partners, David Marsh and Darren Dobrowsky of Willington, CT, Margaret Robinson and William Potts of South Onondaga, NY and Andrew and Heather Marsh of Needham, MA, as well as her six grandchildren, Emily Jane Robinson, Oren Rigel Robinson, Miles Blackwood Robinson, Ceile Rose Marsh, Olivia Anne Marsh, and Marion Elizabeth Marsh.  She is also survived by her sister Gertrude Baulch of Lake Worth, FL and her two brothers, John Miller of Shelton, CT and Steven Miller of Beaumaris, Victoria, Australia.  She was predeceased by her beloved husband Donald James Marsh in May of 1999. Born in Bronx, NY, she graduated from Evander Childs High School where she developed an early talent for art.  Later, along with her husband and family, she enjoyed traveling the world, and lived for several years in San Juan, Puerto Rico and Madrid, Spain.  Upon returning to the U.S, she studied at and graduated from Sacred Heart University in Bridgeport, CT, earning an associate’s degree in interior design.  In succeeding years, she owned her own design business.  On retirement to Dennis, MA, she devoted herself to her many other hobbies, including quilting, gardening and cooking, and to spending time with her large family.  Following the death of her husband, she relocated to The Pinehills in Plymouth where she soon made many wonderful friends.  She was an active member of The Pinehills Singles on the Go and was both the founder and president of The Pinehills quilters group. Calling hours will be on Saturday, November 17, 2007 from 4:00 to 6:00 PM, at the Richard Davis Funeral Home located at 619 State Rd (Route 3A) in Plymouth (Manomet), MA.  A reception will follow at the Overlook Clubhouse at Great Island from 6:30 to 8:00 PM.  Memorial services will be private.  In lieu of flowers, donations in her memory may be made to the Jordan Hospital Cancer Center, 275 Sandwich St, Plymouth, MA 02360 or to Cranberry Hospice, 36 Cordage Park Cr, Suite 326, Plymouth, MA 02360.

West Bridgewater - Eugene Kostecki, 70, died suddenly, but peacefully, at his daughter's home in Chatham, N.Y., on Monday, Nov. 12, 2007.He was the husband of the late Sylvia Hashem Kostecki; and father to Lynn Kostecki Brown of Chatham, N.Y., and Anita Kostecki of Worcester.Born in the Ukraine in 1937, the seventh son of Andrew and Catherine Kostecki, Eugene emigrated to the United States as an orphan in 1948, in the aftermath of World War II.He lived with extended family in East Bridgewater and graduated from East Bridgewater High School in 1955.He became a U.S. citizen on Nov. 15, 1957.He was married to Sylvia on Nov. 17, 1957. This Saturday would have been Gene and Sylvia's 50th wedding anniversary.Mr. Kostecki served in the Brockton unit of the Massachusetts National Guard in the late 1950s.He completed barber school and opened his own barbershop in West Bridgewater in 1962. "Gene the Barber" was still continuing to work at the time of his death.He was an active member of the West Bridgewater Lions Club during the 1970s.He served for 34 years as the West Bridgewater representative on the Southeastern Regional Vocational School Board, spending many of those years as chairman of the board and retired in 2006.Gene seemed to enjoy life most when he was helping others.In addition to his daughters Lynn and Anita, Gene is survived by his sons-in-law, Stephen Brown and Michael Ennis; and five grandchildren, Christopher, Alexander and Adam Brown and Jackson and Hannah Ennis. His children and grandchildren were the joy of his life.Calling hours will be from 4-8 p.m. on Friday, Nov. 16, 2007, at Prophett Funeral Home, 98 Bedford St., Bridgewater. The funeral service will be Saturday, Nov. 17, 2007, at 10 a.m. from the Prophett Funeral Home, followed by burial at Pine Hill Cemetery in West Bridgewater.Donations may be made to Southeastern Regional High School, 250 Foundry St., South Easton, MA 02375, designated to the Eugene Kostecki Scholarship Fund.

Clifford F. “Cliff” Shearing, Jr. 61 years of Bridgewater died Monday evening at his home under the care of hospice. He was the husband for 18 years of Marylou E. (O’Brien) Shearing. For many years Cliff worked part time at Hassan Pharmacy in Brockton. Cliff was an accomplished golfer who once scored a double eagle. He was a member of the Brockton Country Club and had won the club championship in 2000. In his younger years Cliff played on the Juniper Tap Slow Pitch Softball Team and won the championship 11 out of 12 years that he played. He also enjoyed vacations at East Sandwich Beach, Marco Island and Newport, RI and loved to read. Cliff will be remembered as humorous and forthright but also very sentimental and with a large heart.In addition to his to his wife he is survived by a sister Terry Gaquin and her husband Jack of Bridgewater and a brother Lawrence Shearing and his partner Philip Garcia of Brockton. He also leaves an uncle Gerald Albares of Baton Rouge, Louisiana, a nephew John Gaquin and many nieces, nephews and in-laws in the O’Brien family.A funeral will be held from the Conley Funeral Home 138 Belmont St. (Rte 123) Brockton Friday at 8 am. Burial followed at Tewksbury Cemetery.  Memorials to the AmericanCancer Society, 30 Speen St. Framingham, MA 01701, will be apprecieted.

Mavis Twomey Cox, a lifelong resident of Shawsheen Village in Andover, MA and Great Boar's Head in Hampton Beach, NH, passed away on Sunday, November 11 at home surrounded by her loving family. She was born on October 8, 1928 in Andover, MA to Jeremiah J. and Hilda (North) Twomey. Mavis was educated in the Andover public schools, graduated from Abbott Academy in 1946, and received a secretarial degree from the Katherine Gibbs School. She was an active parishioner of St. Augustine's Church in Andover and a member of the Catholic Daughters. Mavis was employed for 40 years as a secretary in the Andover Public School System. For the first 25 years she worked for the Assistant Superintendent and in the Personnel office. After retiring in 1993, Mavis worked for  15 years for the principal of South School, Dr. Eileen Woods, and enjoyed close friendships with co-workers and being surrounded by children. In 1997 she was recognized by the Boston Globe as an "Unsung Community Hero" for her commitment to education and her enthusiasm and dedication in helping children. Family was the center of Mavis' life. She was actively involved in the education and extracurricular activities of her children and grandchildren and was very proud of their accomplishments. All of her children received college degrees. Mavis spent many happy summers relaxing at Hampton Beach, and her hobbies included gardening, reading mystery novels, and knitting sweaters for her grandchildren. She enjoyed traveling and visiting with family members in Texas, Alaska, Colorado, New Mexico, and England. Mavis is predeceased by her beloved husband of 49 years, George Cox, Jr., parents, and brother Gerard Twomey. She is survived by her sister Madge and her husband G. Robert Keepin, Jr. of Los Alamos, NM; cousin Daphne Higham of Southampton, England; her six children, including daughter Susan and her husband Gordon Whicher of Andover, MA and Houston, TX; son George (Joe) III and his wife Mary of Louisville, CO; daughter Lisa of Andover, MA; son Gerald and his wife Cynthia Lemere of Needham, MA; daughter Sara and her husband Jonathan Faulkner of Homer, Alaska; son Michael and his wife Lakshmi of Arlington, MA; 11 grandchildren and 1 great-granddaughter; many nieces and nephews and their families; and her faithful collie, Molly. A Funeral mass will be celebrated on Friday, November 16, 2007 at 1:00 P.M. in St. Augustine's Church in Andover, MA. Worcester—Irja Rachel (Ryssy) Hofschire, 96, died Sunday, November 11, 2007.  Her husband, John Hofschire, died in 1973.  She leaves a son, Charles E. Hofschire of Worcester and his significant other, Sheila Rader, a niece, Carole (Petterson) Lundquist and her husband Donald of MN, a nephew Pete Petterson and his wife Shirley of Sterling, and several grandnieces, grandnephews, great-grandnieces and great-grandnephews.  She was predeceased by her daughter Jane Davis, granddaughter Geneva Davis, brother RADM  J.W. Ryssy, and sister Dagmar Petterson. Irja was born in Worcester, daughter of Matti G. and Anna (Kivisto) Ryssy. Irja graduated from Sergeant College, Boston in 1931 and was a physical educational teacher in the Baltimore, MD public schools for 12 years.  She was the girl’s summer camp director, 7 years, for the Maryland League of physically handicapped children, at Camp Green Top, MD founded by President Franklin D. Roosevelt.  She attended the University of Wisconsin Medical School for Physical Therapy in 1943 and then served as a physical therapist in the U.S. Army Medical Corps for the duration of World War II, and through the end of 1946.  1LT Irja Ryssy treated many severely wounded American service men and German prisoners of war.  She treated General Dwight D. Eisenhower for a severe case of pneumonia and exhaustion during the European liberation campaign and General Jonathan Wainwright following his release from 3½ years as a Japanese prisoner of war.  1LT Ryssy married SSGT John Hofschire, U.S. Army Airborne Infantry in Fort Benning, GA, resigned her commission, and returned to Worcester in December 1946.  She continued on as a physical therapist in civilian life and treated many young people afflicted with polio in Worcester County from 1947 and throughout the 1950’s.  She received a master’s degree from Clark University and returned to physical education teaching at Burncoat Sr. High School, retiring in 1983.  She was a member of Trinity Lutheran Church, a charter member of the Women in Military Service of America, a life member of the American Physical Therapy Assn., and a member of the Mass. Retired Teachers Assn.    A private graveside funeral service was held in Worcester County Memorial Park, Paxton.  In lieu of flowers, contributions may be made to the UMass Memorial Foundation Hospice Care, PO Box 2795, Worcester MA 01613-9938.  Nordgren Memorial Chapel, 300 Lincoln Street, Worcester directed arrangements.   For on-line condolences, please click on link below.

Edward A. Butler, a 55 year resident of Melrose, died on Sunday November 11, 2007, "Veterans Day", at Sawtelle Hospice House in Reading following an extended illness. He was 95 years old. Ed was born in Wakefield on September 21, 1912. He grew up in Wakefield where he attended Wakefield Public Schools and graduated from Wakefield High School in 1931. He began working for The Federal Reserve Bank of Boston as a cashier in 1933. He worked in several capacities for the bank recieving several promotions while also attending The American Institute of Banking. He also attended the Staley School of A Spoken Word which specializes in public speaking. He enlisted into the United States Army on July 25, 1942. He served during World War 2 and was a member of the 8th Army Air Corps while he flew with the B-24 Liberators over Normandy, Germany and several other locations in Europe. He recieved his Good Conduct Medal, European African Middle Eastern Theater Service Medal (with 7 Bronze Stars.) He was discharged on October 19, 1945, with the rank of Staff Sergeant. Ed was credited with sending back information and stories to The Wakefield Item Newspaper about the war with his secret name of "Joe Hip Boots". Following his discharge, Ed returned to The Federal Bank of Boston and met his then future wife Anne Doherty and worked there for a total of 44 years. He retired as Assistant to the Vice President in 1977. He was Past President of The Federal Reserve Bank Society, Past President of The Federal Reserve Bank Quarter Century Club, member of The Federal Reserve Bank Retirees Club. Ed was an original founder and 75 year charter member of The West Side Social Club of Wakefield. He was Past Exhaulted Ruler and Life Member of B.P.O.E. Lodge #1276 of Wakefield. He was a member of Harold O. Young V.F.W. Post # 2394 of Melrose. He was a member of The Most Blessed Sacrament Church Holy Name Society and was a co-ordinator of My Brothers Table. He was an avid golfer who also enjoyed playing bridge and scrabble.  Ed is the beloved husband of 55 years to Anne T. (Doherty) Butler. He is the devoted father of Stephen E. Butler of Wayland, Thomas M. Butler of Simsbury, Ct., Karen A. Kelly of Foxborough, Ellen M. Vaitkunas of Milford and Susan J. Butler of Holliston. He is the loving grandfather of Meghan, Sarah and Stephen Butler, Mark and Kevin Butler, Michael, Timothy and Caroline Kelly. Arthur E. Batson, Jr., age 73 years, formerly of Brockton, died on November 10, 2007, at the V.A. Hospital in Long Beach, CA after a courageous battle with cancer.  He was the loving father of Michael A. Batson and his wife Laura of Lusby, Maryland, and Jilleen M. Erickson and her husband Kraig of Indian Rocks Beach, Florida and cherished grandfather of Lindsay, Daniel, Ashley, David, Brandon, Blake and Logan. He is also survived by several nieces and nephews and his former wife, Marylou (Sproul) Hansen of New Bern, NC, with whom he shared a long friendship.   He was predeceased by his parents, Arthur E. Batson, Sr., and Marietta (Lombardi) Batson. Arthur graduated from Brockton High School in 1953.  After graduation he served four years in the U.S. Coast Guard and is a Korean War Veteran. He began his career with the Shipbuilders Union in 1959 as an electrician at General Dynamics in Quincy. He became Shop Steward and was elected Local Union #5 President in 1970.Two contentious strikes ensued while he was the Local Union President. He led the shipyard workers through their toughest years and also championed worker safety in the shipyard. He negotiated the merger agreement between the Shipbuilders Union and The International Association of Machinist (I.A.M.). The Shipbuilders Union continues their proud legacy as District 4 of the I.A.M.  In 1975 he was appointed Vice President/Secretary-Treasurer, of the National Union in Washington, D.C. and moved to Maryland with his family.  He was re-elected to this position until 1982 when he was elected President, IUMSWA, and held that position until 1994 when he retired and moved to California. Visiting hours will be held 3-7 pm on Sunday, November 18th, at the Conley Funeral Home, 138 Belmont Street (Rte 123) Brockton, MA.  Funeral from the Conley Funeral Home Monday, November 19th, at 8 AM followed by a Funeral Mass at Christ the King Parish/St. Colman Church , 42 Wendell Avenue, Brockton, MA at 9 AM. Burial with military honors will be in Calvary Cemetery, N. Cary Street, Brockton, MA.

Lynne Kelley-Lewicki, 54, of Stonington and Canton, spouse of Raymond Lewicki, passed away unexpectedly at their shoreline home on Saturday, November 10, 2007.   She was born in Detroit, MI and spent her childhood years in Manchester, CT, the daughter of Robert and the late Thora June Kelley.  Lynne met her husband Ray during college, and the two were married in 1976.  Sharing their passion for traveling over the years, they particularly enjoyed getting away from it all on sailing trips.   As an officer at the Stonington Harbor Yacht Club, Lynne nurtured their love for sailing.   A long and successful career in consulting also took Lynne to many places here and abroad, most recently as the Regional Strategies Director for Effective Workplace Practice for The Nelson Group.  Lynne approached her career with the same zest that she lived life.  Her ability to effect positive change in all that she did was truly remarkable.  She displayed this energy as a tireless supporter of the Fidelco Guide Dog Foundation, where she spent countless hours volunteering.   Lynne will be dearly missed by her family and many close friends, who will always reflect fondly on her energy for living life to the fullest and appreciating every moment of it. Besides her husband, Lynne leaves her father Robert of Roanoke, VA; sister Nancy Estey and her husband Elmer of Troutville, VA; and three nieces and four nephews, Kelley Denny, Ashley and Briana Demusz, Christopher Estey, Adam Lewicki, and Kai and Noah Demusz; and two great nieces, Vivian and Lauren Denny.  She also leaves her German shepherd, Jake, and cats Chloe, Grace and Stella.   A memorial service honoring Lynne will be held at the Calvary Church, 27 Church Street in the Borough of Stonington, CT on Friday, Nov. 16 at 10:00am.   In lieu of flowers, donations in the memory of Lynne may be made to the Juvenile Diabetes Research Foundation at JDRF, 20 Batterson Park Road, Farmington CT  06032.  To share a note of remembrance/condolence with Lynne's family, please click the link below.

Karen M. Ring-Connelly, formerly of Woburn, MA died on November 10th, 2007 after a long illness. Karen was the daughter of the late Albert F. Ring of Woburn, MA. She is survived by her mother, Phyllis I. Ring of Plymouth, MA and her sons Edward, Sean, and Daniel Connelly. Karen was the mother of the late Laura Anne Connelly. Karen is also survived by her sister, Pamela Ring-Dorman of Olympia, WA and her brothers, Albert F. Ring Jr. of Christmas, FL and Dennis Ring of Plymouth, MA. Karen was the sister of the late Maryellen Ring of Woburn, MA. Karen was born on July 26th 1953, in Woburn. She graduated from Woburn Public High School in 1973. She then went on to obtain a bachelor’s degree and a teaching certificate from Salem State College. Karen loved to cook and she was quite an accomplished caterer in addition to her teaching credentials. Karen enjoyed shopping, yard sales, a “good bargain” and clipping coupons. She also enjoyed traveling and being with her family. Karen valiantly fought Multiple Sclerosis and the debilitating effects of this disease. She spent the last several years in the Boston Home, a world renowned convalescence home for individuals stricken with this devastating disease. Karen was an individual who would never capitulate to the insidious effects of the disease and her fighting spirit was inspirational to all who knew her. Karen never complained about her disease and she fought tenaciously against the disease until her death this past week.   Karen is also survived by many aunts, uncles, nieces, nephews, and cousins. In lieu of flowers, donations in her memory may be made to The Boston Home, 2049 Dorchester Avenue, Boston, MA, 02124. There will not be any visiting hours and all services will be private.

DARTMOUTH — Richard "Dick" Davenport, 76, of South Dartmouth, died peacefully at his home on Saturday, Nov. 10, after a lengthy illness, with his wife and family at his side. He is survived by his wife of 54 years, Shirley Veale Davenport, and was the son of the late Henry and Florence Brenneke Davenport. A native of New Bedford, he was a graduate of New Bedford High School, where he played soccer and earned all-State honors. He continued his soccer career at Springfield College, graduating with a degree in education. Dick was the waterfront director at Camp Clark in Sandwich, which was affiliated with the New Bedford YMCA. He later joined the Air Force as an air traffic controller, and, achieved the rank of captain before leaving the service. He was well known in the food retailing business in southeastern Massachusetts. Dick was the sales manager, and later, Vice President of sales at Brenneke's Pies before the company was sold in 1971 to Table Talk Pies. He later opened Davenport Distributing in 1973, and became Cain's Potato Chips' second largest distributor in New England. In 1981, he joined Charles Chips as a distributor, and built the business to the eighth largest in the country, while serving on the Board of Directors of the Charles Chips Dealers Association. Dick continued his sports associations by officiating high school basketball for 16 years, and high school and college soccer for over 25 years, including a semi-final NCAA game. He was also very active in the Dartmouth Youth Athletic Association as a coach and supporter. An avid and accomplished golfer, Dick was a member of the New Bedford County Club for 48 years, serving on numerous committees and boards. He was also a member of the Congregational Church of South Dartmouth. Survivors include his wife and four children: Doug Davenport and his wife Ilze, of Medfield; Nancy Kearney and her husband Jim, of Needham; Steven Davenport and his wife Kathy, of South Dartmouth; and, Janice Davenport Russell and her husband David, of Westwood. Dick also had eight wonderful grandchildren, and, he took great pride in their various athletic and academic successes. He was happiest when spending time with his family and his many close friends. Donations in lieu of flowers may be made in his name to Southcoast Homecare Hospice and Palliative Care, 200 Mill Road, Fairhaven, MA 02719. His funeral will take place on Wednesday, November 14th at 11 A.M. in the Wilson Chapel, 479 County St., New Bedford, with visitation at the funeral home on Tuesday from 4-8 P.M. Burial in South Dartmouth Cemetery.

Daniel O. Mahoney, 78, of Mattapoisett died Friday, November 9, 2007,  from complications due to Alzheimer's disease. Born in New Bedford, the son of the late Robert and Virginia (O'Connell) Mahoney, he was raised in Mattapoisett.  He lived in Marblehead for many years, where he and his late wife, Nancy (Miller) Mahoney, raised their family. He then returned with Nancy to Mattapoisett in the 1980s. Mr. Mahoney was a senior litigation partner at the law firm of Palmer & Dodge in Boston until his retirement. A prominent member of the Boston trial bar, he had a distinguished career, both in his private practice and in public service. He was a Fellow of the American College of Trial Lawyers and served as president of the Massachusetts Bar Association. In the late 1970s, he was a  member of the Ward Commission, which investigated corruption in the awarding of state and county building contracts in Massachusetts and brought about far-reaching reforms, including the first-in-the-nation state inspector general's office.    He served on the board of directors of Social Justice for Women and as president of the boards of trustees of the Tower School in Marblehead and Concord Academy.  In Mattapoisett, Mr. Mahoney was a member of  the Zoning Board of Appeals for many years and served two terms as a selectman.   During the Korean War, he served as a captain in the U.S. Army Judge Advocate General's Corps. He was a graduate of Center School, Fairhaven High School, Williams College, and Harvard Law School. He is survived by three children, Daniel O. Mahoney II of Marion, Timothy M. Mahoney of Folsom, CA, and Hannah Mahoney of Cambridge; a brother, Robert Mahoney of Springfield, MO; three grandchildren, Daniel and Meaghan Mahoney and Emily Larabee; and several nieces and nephews. He was the father of the late Matthew B. Mr. Charles F. Nyhan, Sr. 92, a life-long resident of Lawrence, Massachusetts, passed away on Friday, November 9th, 2007 at the Caritas Holy Family Hospital in Methuen, Massachusetts, surrounded by his loving family. He was born in Lawrence, MA., to the late Mr. and Mrs. John J. and Mary (Manning) Nyhan.  Mr. Nyhan was educated in Saint Mary's School and was a graduate of Lawrence High School in 1932.  He was a parishioner of Saint Patrick's Church in Lawrence, MA.,  for 67 years, where he was an Usher for many of those years.  Mr. Nyhan served in the United States Army during WWII. He worked for H.P. Hood and Sons as a Foreman for some 25 plus years.  He later became Assistant City Clerk for the City of Lawrence in 1959 and then became City Clerk for the City of Lawrence for some 30 plus years, his last day was March 31st, 1989 and his retirement took place on April 20th, 1989.  He was a National Life member of the Ancient Order of the Hibernians and was The Irishman of the year in 1981; he was a member of the Knights of Columbus, 4th degree in Lawrence, MA.; a member of the Men of Saint Patrick's; a member of the American Legion, Veteran's of Foreign Wars; a member of the Lawrence Zoning Board, a member of the Caritas Holy Family Hospital Men's Guild; he was a life-long member of the Arlington Club.  Mr. Nyhan enjoyed his children, grandchildren and great grandchildren.  He loved to travel to Ireland, enjoyed video taping his family and friends and most of all he Loved Flag Poles and the Flags they flew.  He even won an award, "Proudly We Hail" from the Lawrence Exchange Club.    Mr. Nyhan made sure each of his children had a flag pole and a flag to fly on it, even if you were not Irish. His loving wife of 67 years Mrs. Mary C. Ms. Patricia "Pat" Green Carter was born May 20, 1947 to Mrs. Geneva Kirby Harris and the late, Floyd Green Sr. in Madison County, Alabama. Pat was the apple of her mother's eye and as the first grandchild, a delight for her grandparents, the late William and Mary Kirby. As a youngster, Patricia accepted Christ as her personal Savior and united with the Lakeside United Methodist Church. Patricia was a graduate of Council High School, where she reigned as Homecoming Queen.  She attended the Spelman College of Atlanta, the School of Medical Technician Washington, DC, Alabama A&M University and the North Alabama College of Commerce. Pat was married to Lincoln Carter and their union was blessed with a glorious gift, daughter, Sixx Leah-Patrice. God equipped Patricia with a beautiful, vibrant, and melodious voice that she loved to share.  Pat's life's dream was to sing and she began publicly performing doing her Hight School and College Years.  Pat was one of the first performers to participate in the Atlanta Jazz Festival and one of the first African American performers to grace the stage of the Huntsville Hilton.  Her love for music and performing is a legacy that she leaves for her daughter, whose career she played an active and vital roll. Patricia spent many years not only singing but working in Washington, DC for the U.S. Congress as an Executive Secretary.  After relocating home to Huntsville, she worked as an administrative assistant for various companies. Following her heart and her desire to be near her daughter, during the Summer of 2007 Pat relocated to Los Angeles, California, where on Thursday, November 8, 2007 in the loving arms of God, she found rest, peace, and a choir that welcomes her harmonizing voice with outstretched arms. Wonderful and loving memories of Pat will forever remain in the hearts of her entire family, but especially her daughter, Sixx Leah-Patrice Carter of Los Angeles, CA; mother, Geneva Kirby Harris of Huntsville, AL; siblings: Vernita McNeil (Marshall) of Franklin, TN, Floyd Jr. and Ronnie both of Huntsville, AL; aunt, Mrs. Odell Jefferson of Chattanooga, TN and uncle, Mr. Roy (Christine) Kirby of Huntsville, AL, many cousins, other relatives and friends.

Funeral services for James Cecil Adair will be held Tuesday, November 13, 2007 at 11:00 A.M. at Perry Funeral Chapel with burial to follow in the Rose Hill Cemetery in Ashburn.  Mr. Adair died Thursday at the Ashburn Health Care following a lengthy illness.   Mr. Adair was born February 15, 1912 in Carnesville, Franklin County, Georgia.  He was the son of the late James Pascal & Lina Jolly Adair. Mr. Adair graduated from the University of North Carolina where he played baseball, football, and basketball.  He came to Ashburn, Ga. in the early 1930’s as a member of the Ashburn Baseball Club, semi-pro baseball team and played third base.  He was a Army veteran of World War II.  He was a member of the Ashburn First United Methodist Church for over 70 years.  He was a retired US Postal Service Rural Letter Carrier. He was a member of the Georgia Rural Letter Carrier Association, Sycamore Masonic Lodge #210 and the Hasan Shrine Temple. He was preceded in death by his parents, his sisters, Frances Pittman and Mary Harden and his brother Doyle Adair.  He was the widower of the late Dorothy Rogers Adair to whom he was married for 67 years. Survivors include one sister Mildred Wiley, Estanolle, Ga., Dianne & Ed Bacon who took an active part in his care.  Also surviving are his nieces, Mary Adair Mullin, Merritt Island, FL., Dorothy Ray, Gulfport, MS, Janice Cooke, Jacksonville, FL; his nephews, James Ray, Gulfport, MS., Tim Pittman, Commerce, GA, and Bud Wiley, Watkinsville, GA.

Mr. Lawrence Drake, Sr., was born to the late Mrs. Lela Mae Drake Brandon and Mr. Lawson Charlie Ross on Apri1 4, 1948, in Huntsville, Alabama. On Thursday, November 8, 2007, at the Huntsville Hospital CCU, he transitioned from life to eternity. He gave his life to Christ at an early age and joined the Union Chapel Missionary Baptist Church under the pastoral leadership of the late Reverend L. F. Lacey. He later joined Zion Progress Missionary Baptist Church under the pastoral leadership of the Reverend John Baker. Lawrence was a strong and wise servant of God. He knew the Lord and daily walked according to his Word. He constantly offered words of wisdom and encouragement to those with whom he came in contact. Prior to his hospitalization, his faith was strong and he was continuously expressing love and giving praise and thanks to God for his many rich blessings. He was a football enthusiast who loved to travel, attend motorcycle events, and watch any western movie that came on television. Lawrence's formal education was in the Huntsville City School System. He graduated from William Hooper Councill High School in Huntsville, Alabama in 1966. He was employed by the General Electric Company in Decatur, Alabama for nineteen years until his health began to decline. He leaves treasured memories with his loving and devoted wife of eighteen years, Sharon Patton Drake; seven children: Lawrence, Jr., (Vonda); Fonda (James) Rice, Jacquelynn (Terrance) Elliott, Anthony Drake, and Kelley Drake, all of Huntsville, Alabama; Steven Drake of Tanner, Alabama and Tiara Yvette Drake of Tanner, Alabama; brothers: Earl (Ninell) Drake, Franklin Drake, Cornell (Sarah) Drake, Darrick Brandon and Robert Drake all of Huntsville, Alabama; sisters: Ida Mae Drake, Lizzie Brandon, and Debbie(Anthony) Preyer all of Huntsville, Alabama; goddaughter, LaShonda Humphrey; his mother in-law, Mrs. Cora Belle Farris; two sisters-in-law: Mary (Dewayne) Kelley, Shelia Jones; two brothers-in-law: George Cleatus (Gwen) Patton, Willie C. (Renae) Patton; thirteen grandchildren; two great-grandchildren; and a host of nieces, nephews, cousins, and friends. Funeral services for Mr. Lawrence (Head) Drake Sr., will be held at 1200 noon on Monday, November 12, 2007 from First Missionary Baptist Church with Elder Hernandez Ford, Sr. officiating. Mr. Drake's remains will be in church one hour prior to the services.  Interment is in Blutcher's Ford Cemetery.

Leon Pierre Trahan, 94, of Hampton, NH, died Nov. 8, 2007, surrounded by family in his home.  He was born in Manchester on Sept 10, 1913 to David and Emma (Hamel) Trahan and was the youngest of five children. He spent most of his life in Manchester and lived the past 10 years in Hampton NH.  He was a member of the 172nd Battalion of the US Army National Guard in Manchester. Their unit and others were responsible for the liberation of the first town in France, Mere St. Eglise, during WWII after landing on Utah Beach, in Normandy France. He was a fourth degree Knight of Columbus and a communicant of St Theresa Church in Rye NH.  He had a devotion towards St. Anthony and St Benedict who was the protector of men in battle. He and his wife Grace were the former co-owners of the Myrtle Convalescent Home in Manchester and Seacoast Health Center and the Partridge House in Hampton. Their lives were dedicated to the caring of the elderly.  He was predeceased by his wife of 63 years, Grace E. (Francoeur) Trahan who died May 4, 2007.  He leaves three children, Eileen M Piet, of Hampton, Daniel P. Trahan, of Epsom, and David A. Trahan, of Deerfield;. seven grandchildren, Christopher Gagnon, DeAnne West, Craig Trahan, Keith Trahan, Sarah Trahan, Hannah Trahan and Emily Trahan; three great grandchildren, Lucas L Gagnon, Christopher E. Gagnon and Samantha G. West. Leon was a dedicated family man who loved having his children and grandchildren surrounding him. He loved to cook, going out for breakfast at Hoaty’s with his friend Tom and building doll houses.   A Mass of Christian Burial was celebrated Monday November 12, 2007 at Parish of the Transfiguration, Kelley St., Manchester. Committal prayers followed in Mt. Calvary Mausoleum, 474 Goffstown Rd. Burial was in Mount Calvary Cemetery. Memorial donations may be made to the Great Bay Chapter of the American Red Cross Nursing Assistant Fund for education materials in Newington NH.

Francis “Frank” A. Bilodeau, 78, of Manchester, died November 8, 2007, at the Elliot Hospital, after a courageous battle with cancer.He was born in Manchester, on October 1, 1929 and was the son of Amedee, and Florence (Pineo) Bilodeau. He was a member of St. Pius X church.Frank was a graduate of Manchester High School West class of 1948. Later he joined the Army, where he was a chef. After an honorable discharge he entered the Dunwoody School of Culinary Arts, as a pastry chef. Upon completion he worked as a trouble shooter for Federal Bake Shops, and at Regis College. Later he completed his studies in Electronics at the Boston Institute of Technology. He began a career that lasted over 36 years at Vishay Sprague Electric, in Concord. Following retirement he did work for  Limited Seasons, equipment for fly fishing.He enjoyed all sports in particular the Red Sox, and Patriots, as well as fly-fishing. He and his wife enjoyed traveling together, especially going to fairs. Frank was a member of Trout Unlimited.IN HIS LIFE: Family members include his wife of 48 years, Claire (Gaumont) Bilodeau, of Manchester; a brother, Benjamin Bilodeau, of Epping; several nieces and nephews, Devon Rains, Kay Bilodeau, and partner Terry, and Neill, Jeannie Sciarappa, and her husband Anthony, Karl Bilodeau, and his wife Kim, Joseph Bilodeau, and his wife Laura, Daniel Bilodeau, and his wife Carole, David Bilodeau, and his wife Laura; several grand nieces and nephews and cousins; a former sister in-law, Elizabeth Bilodeau, of ME. A Mass of Christian Burial was celebrated Monday November 12, 2007 in St. Pius X Church, Candia Rd.  Manchester.Entombment took place in the Mausoleum at Mt. Calvary Cemetery.Memorial donations may be made to the Jimmy Fund, c/o Dana-Farber Cancer Institute, 10 Brookline Place West, 6th floor,Brookline, MA 02445-7226 ATTN: Contribution Services.

John B. Cullen, Sr., 86, a retired corporate executive and Hingham resident for 55 years, died November 7, 2007 at his home. Husband of the late Hilda (Feldman) Cullen, his beloved wife of 48 years, Mr. Cullen is survived by his four children, Kathleen Cullen of Annapolis, MD, John B. Cullen, Jr. and his wife Judith of Nantasket, MA, Barbara Cullen Goodman and her husband Russell of Hingham, MA and Carol Cullen of Brockton, MA; four grandchildren - Jessie Lee Cullen, Devin Cullen Janey, Luke Cullen Goodman, Keir Cullen Janey; a sister, Mary Cullen Yuhas Anger of East Lyme, CT; five nephews and a niece - Sgt. Laurence E. Yuhas, Jr., USMC, Anita Yuhas, Gregory Yuhas, Timothy Yuhas, Joseph Cullen, Jr. and Steven Cullen. Born in New London, CT on September 16, 1921, Mr. Cullen was the son of the late Julia (Cray) Cullen and Bernard  Valentine Cullen and the brother of the late Joseph Cray Cullen.  He attended St. Mary's School in New London, CT and graduated from Bulkeley High School for Boys in 1940. Enlisting in the Army Air Force in 1942 as an Aviation Cadet, he served as a cryptographer in the European Theatre of Operations as a member of the 311th Signal Aviation Company attached to the Supreme Headquarters Allied Expeditionary Forces, attended Oxford University's cryptography school to learn British code and cyphers systems, and served at various Command Headquarters as a cryptographer until 1945 when he was honorably discharged after receiving multiple service decorations including a Presidential Unit citation. Mr. Cullen received a B.S. in Business Administration from Boston University in 1952 and was member of Theta Kappa Phi fraternity and the Accounting Society of Boston University.  He then embarked on a 50 year corporate career most of which was spent with Brigham's Inc. and Boston Filter Company as Vice President and Controller.  After retiring from Boston Filter he then, while in semi-retirement, was Controller of Nickerson Machinery Company in Norwell and later went with Web Engineering Associates in Hingham as their Chief Financial Officer. An avid sportsman and lifelong fan of Notre Dame football, Mr. Cullen donated sports memorabilia to Notre Dame University's Athletic Department in South Bend Indiana, the International Boxing Hall of Fame in Canastota, New York and counted 1936 Heisman Trophy winner Larry Kelly of Yale among his circle of friends. A member of the Notre Dame Club of Boston, a Charter Member of the Hingham V.F.W. Post #6053, Mr. Cullen was also a lifelong member and past president of the Hingham Lions Club, coached Little League baseball and was a member of the 350th Hingham Anniversary Committee.    Relatives and friends are respectfully invited to attend the funeral from the Pyne Keohane Funeral Home, 21 Emerald St. (off Central St.), HINGHAM, Saturday at 9 AM. Funeral Mass in Saint Paul's Church, Hingham at 10 AM.  Visiting hours Friday 6-8 PM.  Burial in Saint Paul's Cemetery, Hingham.  Donations in memory of Mr. Cullen may be made to Hingham Elder Services, 224 Central St., Hingham, MA 02043.
