DRACUT - Army Spc. Christine M. Ndururi, 21, a resident of Dracut, died unexpectedly Tuesday November 6th in Camp Arifjan, Kuwait.   She was born in Kenya on December 6, 1985, and was a daughter of Wilson N. Wachira and Mary W. Mwaniki, of Dracut.  She emigrated with her family to the United States and moved to Dracut in February of 2003 when she was sixteen years old.  She was a graduate of Dracut High School, class of 2005.   Christine enlisted in the Army reserves while a senior at Dracut High School.  In March of 2006 she entered the active Army and in September of that year she was assigned to the 4th Squadron, 3rd Armored Cavalry Regiment in Fort Hood, Texas, as an automated logistical specialist.  She earned the National Defense Service Medal, the Army Service Ribbon, the Global War on Terrorism Service Medal and the Iraq Campaign Medal. Prior to entering the active Army she was employed as a certified nursing assistant by Tewksbury Hospital.  Christine was a devout Christian, and was an active member of the Kenyan Community Presbyterian Church [Ushindi] in Lowell. She enjoyed singing and was a member of her church's choir.  She also enjoyed playing and watching sports and watching movies. In addition to her parents, she is survived by three brothers George Ndururi of Dracut, Simon Ndururi of California, and Ambrose Ndururi of Dracut; a sister Faith Ndururi of Dracut; her grandparents Simon and Millcent Mwaniki and Peninah Kiriamburi, all of Kenya; two nieces Maureen Wanjiku and Beatrice Waithiegeni, both of Kenya, and many aunts, uncles, cousins, and friends.   She was also the granddaughter of the late John Kiriamburi. Friends may call at the DRACUT FUNERAL HOME on Thursday, November 29th from 4 until 8 PM.  Her Funeral Service will be held at 11 AM on Friday, November 30th in the First Church of the Nazarene, 1195 Varnum Ave. in Lowell.  Interment in Oakland Cemetery in Dracut.  Memorial contributions may be made in her name to the Christine M. Ndururi Memorial Fund, c/o Bank of America, 501 Bridge St., Lowell, MA 01850.

Joan Biggers Born Barbara Joan Nevil Cobbold in Blackheath, London on December 21st, 1924 to Cicely Middleton Cobbold and Geoffrey W.N. Cobbold.  Joan was educated at Sherborne School for Girls in Dorset and later graduated with a First Class Honors Degree in Physiology from London University.  She then married, moved to Sydney, Australia, and began raising a family before returning to England seven years later. In 1959 she emigrated to the United States, living first near Philadelphia and later in Baltimore where she worked for the City as a social worker starting in 1966.  In 1972, she moved north to Massachusetts, residing during the 1970’s in Marblehead, then in 1979 moving to North Weymouth, where she lived for over twenty years, prior to moving to Dedham.   In 1979, she developed and directed the Department of Patient Representatives at the Boston’s Peter Bent Brigham Hospital [before its merger].  She was the founding president of the Massachusetts Patient Representatives Association.  Shortly afterwards, in what she considered her most important life achievement, she founded the Hospitality Program in association with the Episcopal Archdiocese in Boston.  In the over 25 years since it’s founding, the Hospitality Program (now called Hospitality Homes) has hosted thousands of families visiting their loved ones - patients at Boston area hospitals, by connecting them with volunteer host families. The program has achieved national acclaim including a story by Peter Jennings of ABC News in 1997.  Joan received the South Shore News Woman of the Year award in 1991 two years after she retired.   She was a loving mother, grandmother and dear friend to many.    In lieu of flowers, please send donations to Hospitality Homes, P.O. Box 15265, Boston, MA 02115.  Joan is survived by Jennifer Biggers Wasserman and Peter Wasserman of Hopkinton, NH, Philippa Biggers and Robert Salzman of Wellesley, and David Biggers and Kathleen O’Connell of Canton; by nine grandchildren ages 10 to 18, and by her beloved sister Margaret Morgan of Ringwood, England.  Services to be held on Wednesday, November 14th at the St. John the Evangelist, 172 Main St, in Hingham at 11:00 am with reception to follow.

Roy Rogie Jackson, Sr., passed away on November 6, 2007 in his home surrounded by his family and loved ones.  Funeral services will be held at 2:00 p.m. on Friday, November 9, 2007, at Trinity United Methodist Church.  Rev. Chris Ramsey will officiate with burial to follow in Oak Ridge Cemetery with full Masonic rites.  Masons will serve as pallbearers.  Honorary pallbearers will be Mason, members of the Ferry Lake Fishing Club, employees of Tifton Machine Works, Inc., and employees of Georgia Controls and Power Transmission, Inc.  The family will receive friends on Thursday, November 8, 2007, from 6:00 to 8:00 p.m. in the Chapel of Bowen-Donaldson Home for Funerals. Mr. Jackson was born in Telfair County on January 18, 1919 and was the son of Thomas Hardy Jackson and Alberta Glynn Jackson, both deceased.  He was also preceded in death by one brother, Hardy Jackson, and two sisters, Ruby Rush and Ethel Martin.  He served in the United States Navy in 1946.  Following his naval service, he moved to Tifton, where he founded Tifton Machine Works, Inc.  He was devoted to his family and community throughout his life.  He was a member of Trinity United Methodist Church, where he served as member and chairman of the finance committee, administrative board and, in 1988, Trinity United Methodist Church honored him with a “Roy Jackson Day”.  In 1990, he received the prestigious George A. Wright Memorial Reward.  Mr. Jackson was a member of the President’s Club of Abraham Baldwin Agricultural College, and a past director of the Tifton Bank and Trust Company.  For over 20 years, he was President of the Ferry Lake Fishing Club.  Mr. Jackson was also awarded Honorary Alumni at Abraham Baldwin Agricultural College. In spite of his busy schedule of work and home, Mr. Jackson found time to participate and devote his life to the works of the Masonic Order and other philanthropic organizations.  As a Mason, he received his first apron in May of 1948, where he began his illustrious career.  He was soon raised to the sublime degree of a Master Mason in June of 1948 in the Tifton Lodge No. 47 of which he was a Past Master.  He lived by the standards of the Masons until his death.  It is extraordinary for a Mason to accomplish all that he has done within the Masonic Order. His Masonic history is as follows:  Past High Priest of Tifton Chapter No. 47, Royal Arch Masons, Past Most Illustrious Master, Tifton Council No. 58, Royal and Select Masters, where he was Past Illustrious Grand Master of R&S Masters of Georgia, and Past Commander of Coastal Plains Commandery No. 42, Knights Templar.  He was a member of Georgia Priory No. 39, Knights of the York Cross of Honour, and a member of Luther G. Palmer York Rite College No. 113.  Brother Jackson was a charter member of charter governor of Wiregrass College No. 203, Georgia Forest No. 181, Tall Cedars of Lebanon, where he was then a charter member of Tall Pines Forest No. 203, Charter G.T.C., Good Shepherd Conclave, Red Cross of Constantine and a life member of Knights Masons of South Carolina Council No. 20.  He was also a charter member of Carl F. Lester J. Council of Knights Masons of Georgia.  He was a past Master of the Wire Grass Convention, Past Grand Royal Patron of Georgia, and Order of the Amaranth, Past Royal Patron and life member of Tifton Court No. 25, Order of the Amaranth.  At the time of his death, he was Deputy Supreme Royal Patron, International Council, and Order of the Amaranth and a member of Blanche Chapter No. 8, Order of the Eastern Star.  Brother Jackson was past Chairman of the District Deputies in the Second Masonic District, a Royal Arch District Deputy, an Ambassador at Large, Hasan Shrine Temple, and past Worshipful Master of the Second Masonic District Convention. A 33 Degree Scottish Rite Mason, Valley of Albany, he served on the Masonic Advisory Committee of the Children’s’ Health Care of Atlanta at Scottish Rite.  He also served as the chairman of the Second District Trial Commission.  One of the outstanding Masonic endeavors in which Brother Jackson has participated, was serving as Chairman of the Building Committee for restoration of the old Abba Lodge Building at the Agrirama. Brother Jackson was a member of the Upper Room Tabernacle XLV, Holy Royal Arch Knight Templar Priests.  He was also Knighted as a Knight of York cross of honor.  He was a past Prior of Georgia Priory, KYCH No. 39. Through the words of the Masonic ritual:  He labored long and hard in the quarries of masonry.  And now that the working tools of life has slipped from his nerveless hands, I assure you that he has received from he who sitteth Judge Supreme those welcome words, well done thou good and faithful servant.  You have been faithful over a few things, I will now make you ruler over many.  He hast now entered into the glory of the Lord. Mr. Jackson is survived by his wife of 65 years, Jimmie Lenora White Jackson of Tifton; one son and daughter-in-law, Roy R. Jackson, Jr. and his wife, Diane, of Tifton; three daughters and two sons-in-law, Mary Glynn Hendricks and her husband, Larry, of Tifton, Rogie Susan Jackson of Statesboro and Kathryn Jackson Hardy and her husband, Ron, of Panama City, Florida; seven sisters, Milly Deshazo of Waycross, Francis Fishcette of Jacksonville, Florida, Martha Andres of Jacksonville, Florida, Sarah Davis of Tifton, Betty Webb of Valdosta, Ellen Hearn of Macon and Joanne Preston of Greensboro, North Carolina; seven grandchildren, Jason and Cynthia Hendricks, Jayme and Chuck Powell, Jennifer Nunn, Jeremy Nunn, Rollins and Deidra Jackson, Caron and Kevin Reid, and Neal Hardy.; and one great grandson, Joshua Thompson Grimsley. In lieu of flowers, the family requests donations be made to the Roy and Jimmie Jackson Educational Foundation, P. O. Box 22, Albany, GA 31702 or any other Masonic charity.

MOLLIE MORRISSEY- DiGIOVANNI...Tewksbury resident since 1950TEWKSBURY, MA -   Malvena “Mollie” (Jasilewicz) (Morrissey) DiGiovanni, whose role asMatriarch of extended Polish and Italian families ended on November 5, 2007, a month shy of her87th birthday,  when she passed away in her sleep at the Sawtelle House in Reading.         On December 5,1920, in Boston, Mollie became the firstborn child of Boleslaw “Benny”and Josephine (Lubin)  Jasilewicz.  She was her Mother’s Helper raising the family in the Polishtradition while she attended Boston High and Latin.  As a teenager, she modeled ballroom gownsand fur coats for prominent Boston boutiques.  At the age of 21 she worked for Charlie Adams inhis Cambridge garage, the beginning of Raytheon.           Mollie married Tom Morrissey from Dorchester on September 5,1943 and they settled inWatertown, where he worked for the Watertown Arsenal.  They and family members builtMollie’s new home on County Road in Tewksbury in 1950.  While her three children were inschool, Mollie was on staff at Vogue Dolls in Waltham and later Tennis Creations in Brookline,where she sewed and on occasion modeled at Longwood Cricket Club and Abercrombie and Fitchin New York City.  At night she attended Fitchburg State enabling her to teach tailoring andadvanced sewing at Tewksbury and Wilmington schools.  She felt fortunate to be working fulltime at Millipore in Bedford, while shortly later her beloved first husband, Tom,  passed away onNovember 8,1977.	Mollie was among the first members of St. Dorothy’s Church, and the St. Dorothy’sSodality, was a member of the Red Hat Society with her dear friend,  Helen Hair, was a memberof the Tewksbury Golden Age Club, and a daily visitor to the Tewksbury Senior Center.           In June of 1981, Mollie married Freddie DiGiovanni from Tewksbury, whom she hadknown for 25 years, and they wonderfully combined their two families with love, music and Italianfeasts.  They traveled often to California and the east coast, until Freddie died on May 23, 1997.	She is survived by her children with Tom:  Thomas Morrissey, Jr., and Terry Morrissey,both of Tewksbury, Linda Neeley and her husband Cecil of Alton Bay, NH; her children fromFreddie’s side: Joyce Trinchera of Wilmington, and Dennis DiGiovanni and his wife Nancy ofSandwich, Cape Cod; six grandchildren, Robert Mott of Laconia, NH, Marc Trinchera and RyanTrinchera of Wilmington, David DiGiovanni of Sandwich, MA, Amy DiGiovanni and PaulDiGiovanni of Milford, NH; three sisters, Stella Goulet and her husband Roger of Attleboro, LucySosnowski of Andover, and Marion Sanborn and her husband Chuck of Spring Hill, FL; a sister-in-law, Martha Jasilewicz of Tyngsboro; and many nieces and nephews.She was also the sister of the late Helen Domurat and Benny Jasilewicz.        Mollie’s Funeral Mass was  celebrated  on Friday, Nov. 9, at St. Dorothy’sChurch,  Wilmington.  Burial will followed  at St. Bernard’s Cemetery in Concord,MA.  Memorials tothe Tewksbury Friends of Elderly, Building Fund, c/o Linda Brabant, 460 East St.  Tewksbury,MA 01876, will be appreciated.

Sheila A. McDermott, Principal of Lee Associates, a human resources consulting firm and Trustee Emerita of tufts University, died suddenly at her home in Medford on November 4. She was 71. A resident of Medford throughout her life and a product of the Medford public schools. Sheila graduated from Tufts University in 1957 with a B.A. in Modern Languages. She became a Personnel Assistant for Riverside Press in Cambridge, the manufacturing subsidiary of Houghton Mifflin, and moved up to Personnel manager. When the plant closed in 1971, she decided to take a chance at entrepreneurship, joining forces with a co-worker and founding Lee Associates, a consulting practice based in Lexington and Medford. For the past 36 years Lee Associates has worked with banks, insurance firms, school and municipal systems in the areas of employer relations, compensation planning, performance evaluation, OSHA, compliance issues, and training. Sheila maintained a lifelong connection with Tufts University. She was an Alumni Trustee from 1991 to 2000, chairing the Audit Committee and serving on the Academic Affairs, Administration and Finance, and Honorary Degree Committees. She was elected Trustee Emerita in 2001. A former President of the Tufts University Alumni Association, she held a host of leadership positions within that body and received its Distinguished Service Award. She was a member of two Boards of Overseers, Arts and Sciences and Student Life, as well as the Friends of the University Libraries. She interviewed prospective students as a member of the Tufts Alumni Admissions Program and actively volunteered for all of her call reunions. She was also an active member of the Boston Tufts Alliance, the Association of Tufts Alumnae and the Tufts Jumbo Club. In her home community of Medford, Sheila was a member of St. Joseph parish, where she is dearly remembered for sharing her time and expertise on the Finance and Liturgy committees. She also took great joy in participating in the annual Medford Jingle Bell Festival, a holiday charity event that raises funds to support Alzheimer’s patients. She was also a member of the Fortnightly Club, a Medford charitable organization. Sheila leaves her godchild, Michelle Gallipeau of Ipswich, a godchild and cousin Barbara Roos of Arlington, Virginia. Her cousins Marion Gallipeau, Peter Gallipeau, Richard Gallipeau, Jr., James Gallipeau, Jacquelyn Cousens of Middletown, RI, Craig Roos of Nantucket, Elin Roos of New York City, Carol Graber of Parsippany, NJ, William Roos of Mountain Lakes, NJ, and her cousins’ many children and grandchildren. As an expression of sympathy memorial contributions may be sent in Sheila’s name to the Little Sisters of the Poor, 186 Highland Ave., Somerville, MA 02144 or The Ronald Brinn Scholarship Fund at Tufts University, c/o Brigette Bryant, 80 George St., Medford, MA 02155. Funeral from the Dello Russo Funeral Home, 306 Main St., Medford Friday at 10 AM followed by a funeral mass celebrated in St. Joseph Church, 118 High St., Medford at 11 AM.  Relatives and friends are respectfully invited to attend. Visiting hours Thursday 3-8. Services will conclude with burial at Oak Grove Cemetery, Medford.

Barry, John E., of Hingham, beloved husband of Marjorie A. "Marge" Barry, died peacefully November 4, 2007 in the house where he had lived for 84 years. He was also the dear father of Jane and her husband Davis Hartwell of Portland, Maine. Cherished grandfather of Thomas and Benjamin Hartwell. Devoted uncle of John Mahar of Marshfield and the Philippines.  Mr. Barry was a lifelong resident of Hingham.  He was born on November 3, 1921, and since 1923 has lived in the house built on Main Street by his parents. He graduated from Hingham High School in 1938 and from M.I.T in 1942. He was married for 57 years to his wife, whose maiden name was also Barry.  John served in the U.S Army during WWII and retired as a Reserve Colonel.  He served for over 55 years in volunteer or elected positions with the Town of Hingham, including the Board of Health (15 years) and the South Shore Country Club Development and Management Committees.  He was a member of the Hingham Tennis Club since 1946.   Although aged 86, John was tenacious at staying active. He died in his home after a day of gardening. He had large flower and vegetable gardens and even managed to dig up the dahlia tubers and bed the gardens before his passing.  Relatives and friends are respectfully invited to attend the funeral from the Pyne Keohane Funeral Home, 21 Emerald St., (off Central St.), HINGHAM, Monday at 9 AM. Funeral Mass in Saint Paul's Church, Hingham at 10 AM.  Visiting hours Sunday 2-6 PM.  Burial in Saint Paul's Cemetery, Hingham.

Comerford, Joseph F. "Joe" of Hingham.  He died November 4, 2007 at South Shore Hospital after a brief illness with his family by his side.  Beloved husband of Jane (Sharp) Comerford of Hingham.  Loving father of Kathleen & Molly; both of Hingham and Patrick and his wife Erin of NY.  Brother of Marianne and her husband Robert Fahey of NY, William of FL and the late Michael, Thomas, Daniel, Nelson, Vincent, John and Kathleen.  Brother in law to Alice (Holland) of NY, Maureen Comerford of NY, Bob Sharp of NY, Dan McKenzie of NY and the late Betty, Margaret, Mary and Kay (Leitten) Sharp.  Also survived by 35 nieces and nephews and dozens of great nieces and nephews.   Joe was born in Buffalo, New York on August 14, 1928.  One of ten children to Dan and Kate Comerford, Joe was the youngest boy.  Joe graduated with honors from St. Joseph’s Collegiate Institute in 1948 and from Canisius College in 1952. He was a member of the Di Gamma Honor Society.  Joe joined the United States Army after graduation.   Fighting the Korean War by way of Fort Dix, New Jersey, Joe rose through the ranks as a recruiter for potential re-enlisters just returning from the war.  It was at Fort Dix that Joe received Last Rites for the first time.  He would go on to set the Comerford record of receiving Last Rites eight times, the first in 1953, and the last on Saturday night. Joe married Jane Sharp in Buffalo, New York on July 11, 1959.  As a young couple, they lived in Buffalo as Joe began his nearly forty year career in the beauty and fragrance industry. Joe worked as a Sales and Management Executive in the New England, Northeast and Mid-West regions, for companies including Revlon, Coty, Lancome, Vitabath, Smith Kline Glaxo Beecham, Parfums Givenchy, and Louis Vuitton Moet Hennessy.  Joe also served as President for the New England Cosmetics Association for many years. After living in New York, Ohio, and Kansas, Joe and Jane finally settled in Hingham, Massachusetts in 1972.  Joe and Jane made their home in Hingham for 36 years.  Jane taught 4th grade at St. Paul’s School in Hingham for over 25 years.  Joe served as an Usher at Resurrection Church in Hingham for over a decade.  Joe also started and served as the first President of “Friends of Notre Dame Academy”, the weekly Bingo Fundraiser. When not putting in the long hours of travel his career demanded, Joe was wholly devoted to his family.  No matter the cost of the tuitions for Notre Dame Academy, BC High, Boston College, Regis College, Holy Cross College, and Fordham Law School, the annual family summer trip to Nantucket and a pie from Bartlett’s Farm, always happened.  Joe will be forever remembered for his faith in God and people, keeping life fun and never losing his youthful sense of humor.   Relatives and friends are respectfully invited to attend the funeral from the Pyne Keohane Funeral Home, 21 Emerald St., HINGHAM, Friday at 9 AM.  Funeral Mass in Saint Paul's Church, Hingham at 10 AM.  Visiting hours Thursday 4-8 PM.  Burial in Saint Paul's Cemetery, Hingham.   Donations in memory of Mr. Comerford may be made to the Joseph F. Comerford Scholarship Fund, St. Joseph's Collegiate Institute, 845 Kenmore Avenue, Buffalo, NY 14223 or contact their Director of Endowment Gifts, Mr. Scott Burns at (716) 270-4115.

Brownlow Jackson Crawford, a business owner and entrepreneur whose philanthropic spirit has forever changed the heartbeat of Water Valley, MS as well as other surrounding communities, has died at age 77. Brownie, as he was affectionately called, was born in Sugar Creek, GA on February 18, 1930, to Carl Clifford and Nona Margaret Cobb Crawford, and was the eldest of nine children. He passed away on Sunday, November 4, 2007 at Baptist Memorial Hospital in Oxford, MS.Though not formally educated beyond a high school diploma, Brownie was a self-educated man who read voraciously, listened intently, and had a keen sense of business trends and marketing. Brownie, along with his wife Dorris, sent his younger brothers and sisters to college during the fledgling years of their poultry business, fueling his love for giving and helping others as he and Dorris grew more successful in their business ventures.Despite his efforts to mask his generosity, the term “anonymous donor” became synonymous with “I bet that’s Brownie.” His innumerable contributions have touched the lives of Yalobusha and Lafayette residents and beyond, notably in the areas affecting youth and children, local and foreign medical missions, sports, education, and those less fortunate who need a helping hand. Brownie enjoyed recounting the unusual and humorous details of his salvation experience with Christ Jesus, and never hesitated to give God the glory for the many blessings he enjoyed sharing with others.In addition to his reputation as a generous giver, Brownie was widely reputed as a practical joker with hilarious tales to share.  His infectious laugh was magnetic, and his tales grew with each retelling. Brownie was an avid walker, often up and walking in the wee hours of the morning before the rush of the business day compelled him to spend many hours in his Crawford and Associates office near his home.Brownie was a member of the First Baptist Church of Water Valley, Past President of the MS Baptist Children's Village, Director of Reedy Acres Foundation, Past President of the Water Valley Chamber of Commerce, Director Mechanics Bank, Three Rivers Development Council, Water Valley Ambassadors, Director of Case Foods, and he was an Eagle Scout.In an effort to capture Brownie’s life in a few paragraphs, his daughter, Lorri, was asked to offer input. Her modest answer sums up everyone’s incapability to pen his life. “Daddy’s life was bigger than words; his life was action.”  Brownie will be tremendously missed by his family, his church, and his countless friends.A visitation for Brownie Crawford is planned for Tuesday evening, November 6, 2007 from 6 until 8 p.m. at Seven Oaks Funeral Home in Water Valley. The service will take place at First Baptist Church of Water Valley at 11 a.m. on Wednesday, November 7, 2007 with Dr. Randy Bain and Rev. Raymond Aven officiating. The family will hold a private graveside service at Pinecrest Memorial Gardens in Yalobusha County, MS.Pallbearers will be Robby Parsons, Richard Shelly, Cam Tyler, Tom Fitts, Sean Carothers, and Wayne Harris.  Honorary pallbearers will be Bill Williamson, Snooky Williams, Bob Tyler, Eddie Ray, Arnold Carothers, Eric Weeks, Dr. Joe Walker, Dr. Paul Odom, Charles Sharp, Perry Crawford, Charles Walker, Robert Weaver, Lowell Edwards, Floyd McGehee, Dee Young, Richard and Lisa Howorth, and The Mechanics Bank Directors.Brownie Crawford is survived by his wife, Dorris Hemphill Crawford of Water Valley; two sons, Len Jackson Crawford of Blue Ridge, GA and Darrell Brownlow Crawford of New York, NY; and one daughter, Lorri C. Williamson of Water Valley, MS; two sisters, Johnnie C. Watkins of McCaysville, GA and Dorris C. Olnick of Blue Ridge, GA; five brothers, Pete Crawford of Mount Juliet, TN, Bob Crawford of Douglas, GA, Gene Crawford of Morganton, GA, Jim Crawford of Tucker GA, and Joe Crawford of Toccoa, GA; five grandchildren, Joeli Williamson of Oxford, MS, Cory Williamson of Water Valley, MS, Emily Crawford of Bentonville, AR, and Caroline and Abigail Crawford, both of Blue Ridge, GA. In addition to his parents, he was preceded in death by one brother, Jack Crawford of Water Valley, MS.Memorials may be made to Compassion Ministries, c/o Carroll Crenshaw, 70 CR 386, Water Valley, MS 38965 or to the Northwest Community College Brownlow Jackson Crawford Scholarship Fund, P.O. Box 7015, Senatobia, MS 38668.

Mrs. Laura Sue Eggleston was born July 23, 1927 in Huntsville, Alabama to the late Earl Moore and Muzetta Fisher Moore. After the passing of her mother, while still in infancy, was raised by her father and grandmother. She attended Councill High School and later as a young lady, during WWII, worked at the Huntsville Arsenal, what is now known as Redstone Arsenal. At an early age, Laura confessed hope in Christ and joined Union Chapel Missionary Baptist Church and was baptized in 1945 under the pastoralship of Pastor O. D. Boone and served faithfully. After the reduction in force at the arsenal and many were laid off, she decided to move to Dayton, Ohio where many of her relatives lived. She lived and worked in Dayton for many years until the illness of her loving grandmother, Sue Jean Timmons Moore, compelled her to return to Huntsville to be with and take care of her. Laura was a people person, she never met a stranger that she couldn’t say a kind word to. Taking care of people was the enjoyment of her life and she always had a smile. After a long illness and many pains, the Master decided it was time to call Laura home. Sunday morning, November 4, 2007 at 10:30 a.m. in the home of Frank and Betty Brandon, she went to sleep. Laura leaves to cherish fond and loving memories a host of cousins: Franklin R. (Betty) Brandon of Huntsville, AL, Oscar (Vivian) McDonald of Dayton, OH, Shelia Johnson of Huntsville, AL, Theado (Ron) Fletcher of Madison, AL, Betty Jean Russell, Roberta Timmons, Cebell (Leo) Pride, all of Madison, AL and George Clay Jr. of Huntsville, AL; a special friend, Mrs. Everlyn Jackson of Dayton, OH. Funeral services for Ms. Eggleston will be held at 1200 Noon on Friday, November 9, 2007 at Union Chapel Missionary Baptist Church with Rev. Dr. O. Wendell Davis officiating.  Interment will be in Valley View Memory Gardens.

Laurent M. Maurier, 86, of Manchester, died November 4, 2007 at Villa Crest Nursing Home following a brief illness. He was born in Manchester, March 13, 1921 and was the son of Henri and Florida (Hebert) Maurier. He was chairman of Cub Scout Pack 117 in its early years and was a devoted member of the St. Vincent de Paul Society. He participated in the Christian Family Movement and the Octomates Social Group. Mr. Maurier was a member of the League of a Thousand Men of the Precious Blood Monastery. His hobbies included woodworking and repairing televisions and radios out of his home workshop. He cherished keeping his family ties strong through camping. He leaves his wife of 60 years, Antoinette (Hebert) Maurier of Manchester; a daughter, Sr. Pauline Maurier, CSC of Manchester; four sons, Richard Maurier and his wife, Louise, Robert Maurier, Maurice Maurier, all of Manchester, and Roger Maurier and his wife, Irene, of Hooksett; two grandchildren, Adele and Stephen Maurier; a brother, Raymond Maurier of Manchester; two sisters, Dora Page and Theresa Gingras, both of Manchester; many nieces and nephews.He was predeceased by a brother, Armand Maurier, and a sister, Lucille Lawrence.   A Mass of Christian Burial was celebrated Wednesday November 7, 2007 at St. Ann-St. Augustin Church. Charles J. Butterfield, 91, of Windham, NH, died Saturday, November 3, 2007 at his home.  He was born in Boston, MA on August 23, 1916, a son of George and Mary (Moriarty) Butterfield.  Mr. Butterfield graduated from Woodbury High School in Salem, NH in 1935, where he lettered in basketball, football and baseball.  He was a resident of Windham except for the few years that he was employed at Hamilton Standard Aircraft in Hartford, CT.  Mr. Butterfield was the Postmaster in Windham, retiring after 32 years of service.  He served as a special officer on the Windham Police Department as well as a volunteer firefighter.  After retiring from the Postmaster position, Mr. Butterfield helped the town of Windham by heading up the animal control position.  He was a communicant of St. Matthew’s Church in Windham and a member of the Cursillo community at St. Basil Salvatorian Center in Methuen, MA.  Mr. Butterfield was a member of the Knights of Columbus and of the Windham Community Club.  He was an avid sportsman and enjoyed raising raising foxhounds and beagles.  As a national foxhound and beagle judge, his services were sought after as he had earned a reputation for excellent judgment and fairness.  During his lifetime achievement in the “hound world” he was able to breed and campaign two international champions.	Family members include three sons, Dennis C. Butterfield and Gary M. Butterfield both of Windham, and Timothy J. Butterfield DVM of Derry; five grandchildren; and several nieces and nephews.  He was predeceased by his wife of 52 years, Mary V. (Boylan) Butterfield in 2002.	Calling hours were held at the Peabody Funeral Homes and Crematorium, 15 Birch St., Derry, on Wednesday, November 7th from 4 – 8pm.   A Mass of Christian burial was celebrated on Thursday at 10:00am in St. Matthew’s Church, 2 Searles Rd., Windham, with the burial following in the Cemetery on the Plain in Windham.  In lieu of flowers, the family requests donations be made to the CJB & MVB account, C/O Citizens Bank, for an in kind memorial gift to the Windham Conservation Commission, or to the Lazarus House, Attn. Develop Director, PO Box 408, Lawrence, MA  01842.

William J. "Bill" Winn, 65 a longtime resident of Methuen, MA. and a former resident of Lowell, MA. died suddenly Saturday, November 3rd, 2007 morning at Lawrence General Hospital. He was born in Methuen on November 5, 1941, son of the late Peter V. & Margaret (Foley) Winn. Mr. Winn received his early education in Methuen and was a graduate of Essex Aggie. He received his B.S. in Criminal Justice from Northeastern University, and hisMasters from Santa Maria College. Early on, Mr. Winn worked at the Coca Cola Bottling Company of Lowell. He was also a Corrections Officer at MCI Concord, MA. Mr. Winn retired in 1999 from the Lowell District Courthouse, where he had been a probation officer for many years. Since his retirement, he ran his own private detective agencyand was associated with Madison Security Company of Lowell, MA. He was a lifetime member of the Lawrence Elks and held memberships in the East End Club and the Sacred Heart Auto League. He supported the Special Olympics, Toys for Tots, MSPCA, and the Democratic National Committee. It is a sad time like this when the true measure of a man is found. Bill never let a call go by him without answering his phone, whatever the time of day or through the night. Whether it be a problem concerning a relative, a friend, or a friend of a friend he always came up with the guidance help and solution without hesitation. He had the whit of a true Irishman, endless stories and jokes galore. Although at times some what abrupt, family and friends never failed him. Bill was cared for and loved by all around him. God must have needed him to join his flock. He is survived by his wife, Gloria M. (Wilson) Winn of Methuen. His daughter & son-in-law, Debra & John Carnevale of Nashua, NH, a granddaughter Jennifer Carnevale and her husband Adam Johnson of New Hampshire, his grandson Christopher Carnevale of Vernon, CT. His brother and sister-in-law Daniel V. & Laura Winn of Lewiston, ME., his sister Clare Minkle of Salem, NH. He is also survived by Johnny, Michael & Robert Allen of Billerica, MA. as well as many nieces & nephews. A funeral mass will be celebrated on Wednesday, November 7th, 2007 at 10:00AM at Our Lady of Good Council Parish at St. Augustine Chapel, 128 Ames Street, Lawrence, MA. A burial will follow in St. Patrick's Cemetery in Lowell, MA.  Friends may call on Tuesday, November 6th, 2007 from 3:00PM to 7:00PM at the John Breen Memorial Funeral Home, Inc. 122 Amesbury Street, Lawrence, MA. 01841 for more information or to send online condolences please go to: www.breenfuneralhome.com In lieu of Flowers memorials may go to the Lawrence Lodge of Elks, B.P.O.E. #65, 652 Andover Street, Lawrence, MA. 01843.

Gerard F. Brennan, born in South Weymouth on October 13, 1923, passed away at the South Shore Hospital on November 3, 2007. Mr. Brennan is survived by his wife of 60 years, Marjorie Hayden Brennan, a daughter Lee Streeter and her husband Dana of Ossipee, NH; son Shawn Brennan and his partner Nan Crawford of San Francisco, CA; 3 Grandsons: Jeremy, Joshua and Jared Lawton and 5 great grandchildren. His son Robert Brennan preceded him in death. Gerard graduated from Weymouth High School in 1941 and was a retired Foreman for the Weymouth Light and Power Company where he worked for over 40 years. Peter Trova, 94 Dateline: Norton, MA      Peter Trova, 94, of Norton formerly of Pittsfield died on Saturday November 3, 2007 at home. He was the husband of Mary C. (Strona) Trova to whom he was wed to on April 18, 1936.     Born in Wilmerding, Pennsylvania on September 8, 1913 he was a son of the late Alessandro and Margharita (Marcellino) Trova.     At the age of 6 Peter and his family moved back to the family’s native town of Mombercelli, Italy, he lived there for 10 years before his family moved back to America and settled in Pittsfield, MA. He was educated in both Italy and Pittsfield public schools.     Mr. Trova was a well known and respected self-employed carpenter and builder in the Pittsfield area. He came from a long line family of builders and was known for building custom homes.     While living in Pittsfield, he was a communicant of All Souls Church. He and his wife were charter officers of the Italian-American Club in Pittsfield. Since retiring he had taken up the hobby of wood carving and woodworking as hobbies and when younger he enjoyed hunting, fishing and playing bocci.     Peter had made his home in Norton for the past 27 years and was formerly of Pittsfield for over 50 years. He and his wife had also formerly wintered in Port Richey, Florida for 22 years.     In addition to his wife of 71 years he is survived by his children; Saundra V. Perez and her husband Santiago of Norton and Peter R. Trova and his wife Barbara of Pittsfield. He was the brother of Rose Nigrelli of Pittsfield, Angelina Trombley of Pompano Beach, Florida and the late Louis Trova and Emilio Trova. Also surviving are 6 grandsons, 11 great grandchildren and many nieces and nephews.      His funeral Mass, to which relatives and friends are cordially invited to attend, will be celebrated on Wednesday November 7th at 11:00 A.M. in St. Mary’s Church, South Worcester St. Norton. Burial will be held on Thursday November 8th at 11:00 A.M. in St. Joseph’s Cemetery, Pittsfield.     Visitation will be held prior to the funeral Mass on Wednesday morning beginning at 10:00 A.M. at the Norton Memorial Funeral Home 19 Clapp St. (Off Route 140, Taunton Ave.) Norton.     In lieu of flowers, Peter’s family has requested that donations in his memory be made to the Community Visiting Nurse Agency, Inc. 10 Emory St. Attleboro, MA 02703.      For additional information please visit www.nortonmemorial.com

A graveside memorial service will be held at the Sturdevant Cemetery in Halifax on Saturday, November 3, 2007 at 11:00 a.m.  Howard L. Waterman Jr. of Halifax died on March 23, 2007, after a lengthy illness, at the Caritas Good Samaritan Medical Center, Brockton.He was husband of the late Nancy Fuller Waterman. Born on December 15, 1932 at the South Shore Hospital in Weymouth. Howard had numerous interests; Howard was a Mason, a member of the Mayflower Society, Plymouth County Genealogists, the Upland Sportsman’s Club, and the NRA. Joseph A. Baldassarre, MD, a long-time Somerville physician, died Friday evening at the Lahey Clinic. He was 87. Born and raised in Boston’s North End he was the son of the late Marciello and Angelina Baldassarre. Joseph went to college and medical school at Kansas City University. In 1947 he settled in Somerville, married and opened up his practice at 42 Main St. During the Korean War, he served as an Army physician. He returned to his practice at the end of the war and went about caring for the people of Winter Hill and the surrounding neighborhoods. He was affiliated with the Somerville Hospital and the former Central Hospital. Although a general practitioner, he performed surgery and brought 850 babies into the world. Day and night he tended the needs of his patients, whether in his office, at the hospital or on house-calls and never turned anyone away. He was a member of the Vittorio Rocci Post in Beverly, the American Medical Assoc., Somerville Rotary and was an avid bridge player. He was the beloved husband of Rose (Gangi), with whom he shared 60 years of marriage. He was a devoted father to Marcia Baldassarre of Woburn, Joseph Baldassarre and his wife Elaine of Woburn, John Baldassarre and his wife Sharon of Nashua, NH.  He was a loving grandfather to Joseph, Jillian and Michael. He was a loving great-grandfather to Hailey, Heaven, Noelle, Kelsey and Christiano. He was a dear brother to the late Pasquale, Anthony and Alphonse Baldassarre.  His funeral will be from the Dello Russo Funeral Home, 306 Main St., Medford, Wednesday at 9 AM followed by a funeral mass celebrated in St. Clement Church, 71 Warner Street, Medford, at 10 AM. Relatives and friends are respectfully invited to attend. Visiting hours Tuesday 4 - 8. Services will conclude with burial at Oak Grove Cemetery, Medford. In lieu of flowers contributions may be made in Joseph’s name to the Lahey Clinic, 41 Mall Rd. Burlington, MA 01805. Late US Army Veteran, Korean War.

John F. Killory, 85 years, died peacefully at his home after a brief illness.  He was the son of the late John F. and Gertrude V. (Gloney) Killory and was a lifelong resident of Brockton.  He graduated from Brockton High School in 1940 where he played shortstop on their baseball team.  He attended Williston Academy where he won the batting title with a .462 average.  He also played with the Taunton Lumber team, Boston Park League, American Legion team and the Cape Cod League.  He was a member of the St. Patrick’s CYO baseball team in which his friend Rocky Marciano was the catcher and his father, John Sr. was the manager and captured the 1940 State Championship.  He graduated from Boston College in 1947.  In 1941 when BC played against BU he tied a major league record for getting three hits in one inning.  In 1942 when BC played their annual game against the Red Sox at Fenway Park, John got a single off Red Sox pitcher Joe Dobson.  His love of baseball was his lifelong passion.  He enlisted in the Navy in 1942 and was assigned to the Navy V12 Program at Tufts for officers training.  He was commissioned a Lieutenant and assigned to various ships, ultimately to the LST 833 which carried the Marines for the invasion of Okinawa.  After the Japanese surrendered he was assigned to a unit to survey the damage at Okinawa, Japan and the Philippines.  After the war he returned to Boston College and later attended BC graduate school.  He retired from Lechmere Corporate Offices in 1987 and then spent every day with his grandsons when they were young.  He treasured each moment with them.  An avid fan of Notre Dame since childhood, he and his family attended many football games in South Bend, Indiana.   A lover of books, he possessed a vast collection of sport, movie, WWII, political and biographies. He was a collector of many films of the bygone years.  He was a member of the American Legion and a communicant of Our Lady of Lourdes Church.   Besides his wife of 53 years, Adele (Giudici) he is survived by two daughters and their husbands,  Mary and Richard Walker of West Roxbury and Ann and Erick MaczPacay of Brockton and two grandsons, Michael and Kevin Walker. Funeral from the Conley Funeral Home, 138 Belmont Street (Rte 123) Brockton Wednesday at 10am.  Funeral Mass in Our Lady of Lourdes Church, at 11am.  Burial in Calvary Cemetery.  Visiting Hours Tuesday 3-6pm.  Donations in John’s name may be made to Hospice of Greater Brockton, 500 Belmont Street, Brockton, MA 02301.

Corris M. Hofmann, 93Plainville Native Dateline: Boulder, Colorado      Corris M. Hofmann, 93, of Boulder, Colorado died on Friday November 2, 2007 at Frasier Meadows in Boulder.     Born in Plainville, MA on September 18, 1914 she was a daughter of the late Edward and Mabelle (Fuller) Hofmann.     She grew up in Plainville and was educated in Plainville. She was a graduate of the University of Illinois, receiving her BS in Chemistry and went on to receive her Masters & Phd in Chemistry from Brynmawr College.     Ms Hofmann worked in Laboratory Research in the developing and testing of drugs before retiring.     An active attendant of the Methodist churches she was also a member of the American Chemists Society. Corris was extremely creative and enjoyed painting, especially in water colors, she also enjoyed gardening, bowling, golf and traveling.     Ms Hofmann had made her home in Boulder for the past 11 years and had formerly resided for over 10 years in Southbury, Conn.     She is survived by several cousins including Ruth King of Mansfield and her many close family friends.     Graveside funeral services, to which relatives and friends are cordially invited to attend, will be held on Friday November 9th at 1:00 P.M. at the Plainville Cemetery, West Bacon Street, Plainville. Burial will follow     Visiting hours have been omitted. In lieu of flowers donations in Corris’s memory may be made to the Alzheimer’s Association Massachusetts Chapter 311 Arsenal Street Watertown, MA 02472     Arrangements are under the care and direction of the Sherman & Jackson Funeral Home 55 North Main St. Mansfield     For additional information please visit www.shermanjackson.com

Memorial contributions may be made to the Massachusetts General Hospital, c/o the Heart Center, 55 Fruit St., Boston, MA 02114.

Daniel J. Bates, Sr., a Medford businessman and community leader died early Wednesday morning at the Youville Hospital, surrounded by his loving family. He was 81. Mr. Bates was raised in Medford the son of the late Mark J. and the late Catherine (Ahearn) Bates. Dan graduated from Saint Joseph School and Medford Vocational High School. He served in the Navy during World War II. Upon his return from wartime duty he served with the Navy Reserve for another five years while working as a finish carpenter and interior decorator with A. L. Bellans Co. In the late 1950’s he entered the insurance field and worked with Metropolitan Insurance. 31 years ago he opened his own agency in Medford, the Daniel J. Bates Insurance Agency. The father of a large and active family, Dan was himself very active in civic affairs, where he let his business acumen and leadership skills serve the people of his community.  A past member of the VFW and American Legion, Dan was also a board member and past treasurer of the Irish American Club. He was a member of the Saint James Holy Name Society, the Medford Elks Lodge and was a member of the Board of Incorporators at the Lawrence Memorial Hospital. He was a founding member of the Wellington-Glenwood Little League, was a member of the Mystic- Wellington Yacht Club and served in the Coast Guard Auxiliary, teaching children the basics of boat safety. A member of the Medford Chamber of Commerce, Dan served as president and was honored by the community as Citizen of the Year.He was a long-tome and very active member of the Medford Kiwanis club and served as predident. Dan was a Member of the Arlington Retired Men’s Club and the Singing Grandpops.   He was the beloved husband of Catherine (Mulcahy) Bates. He was a devoted father to William H. Professor Roger Richmond Borden, retired, passed away Monday evening, at his home in Worcester, MA while quietly reading the Bible and watching some television after enjoying a train trip to Portland Maine with the Briarwood Community earlier in the day. Prof. Borden taught mechanical engineering at WPI for more than 25 years (as well as managing the engine lab) and previously at Wentworth Institute of Technology in Boston. He was also a retired pastor of the United Methodist Church (serving parishes in Charlton, West Fitchburg and East Templeton, MA) and previously of the United Church of Christ (serving at the Orthodox Congregational Church in Petersham, MA). Not being a person to 'sit still' in his retirement he served as a docent for the Worcester Art Museum, a guide for The Boston Museum of Science, and as a volunteer in the Visitor Center at Olde Sturbridge Village. An avid train buff, and shutter-bug, he was always reading up on or engaged in his hobbies. He was also a member of the Masons through the lodge in Clinton, MA. Mr. Borden was predeceased by his wife of nearly 50 years, Constance Elin (Linberg) Borden, this past April. Anthony S. J. Tomasello, Age 91, died Monday, October 29, 2007 in Brooksville, Florida. He was born in Boston, MA on March 2, 1916, to Samuel J. and Josephine F. Tomasello and retired to Crystal River, FL in 1984 after many vacations to the area. He attended Boston Latin School and was graduated from Harvard College in 1937 with an A.B. degree in Civil Engineering.  He was a certified Professional Engineer.  He entered the family building business and was responsible for the construction of many roads in the Boston area over a period of 37 years.  In 1941, he was graduated from Northeastern University Law School with a J.D. degree and was admitted to the practice of Law in Massachusetts. He was a very active volunteer in the service of children for over thirty years, and was a member of the Child Welfare League of Executives, Mass. Council of Child Care Agencies, was Chairman if the Bos-Line Council for Children and in 1978 was appointed by Gov. Dukakis to serve as Chairman of the Mass. Statewide Advisory Council for Children. In 1973, he gave up his road construction business and took on the position of executive Director of the Italian Home for Children in Jamaica Plain, a position he held until his retirement in 1984.  During his tenure, he oversaw the transformation of the agency from custodial care to one of the finest residential treatment centers for emotionally disturbed children in MA.  He was also the President of the Mt. Washington Bank in South Boston.  In 1991, the Citrus County Commissioners appointed to the Chairman of the Children’s Services Task Force of Citrus County, FL. He was an avid golfer and duplicate bridge player.  He was a member of the Seven Rivers Golf and Country Club in Crystal River, FL, was a former member of the Charles River Country Club in Newton, MA, and also of the Norfolk Golf Club in Westwood, MA.   His first wife, Annadith Tomasello predeceased him.  Survivors include his wife, Patricia Burnes Tomasello; a son Robert G. Tomasello of Middletown, DE; a daughter Nancy Tomasello Furman of Brooksville, FL; two sisters, Anne Strillchuk of Dedham, MA, and Frances Rowen of Wayland, MA; two grandchildren, Joshua Poulin of Jacksonville, FL and Jessica Johnson of Pittsburgh, PA, and one great grandson, Bodhi Johnson. Visiting hours in The Robert J. Lawler & Crosby Funeral Home, 1803 Centre St. West Roxbury on Monday November  5, from 10:00 AM to 12:00 Noon. Relatives and friends are invited to attended. Interment Holyhood Cemetery. Memorial donations may be made to the Italian Home for Children, 1125 Centre Street, Jamaica Plain, MA  02130

Jill Elaine Guisti, 43 of Bellingham died October 29 suddenly in her home.She was the daughter of Charlotte (Samsel) Guisti of Bellingham and the late Ralph P. Guisti.Born Attleboro, February 6, 1964 she had lived in Bellingham for 10 years and previously lived in Norfolk and Lake George, New York.Miss Guisti was a graduate of King Phillip High School and a graduate of the University of Massachusetts in Amherst with a BS in Legal Studies.Miss Guisti was a self employed court reporter.Jill was a supporter of Missions to help the poor and needy.She helped support Life Outreach International in building clean water wells in third world countries and supported orphanages in the Ukraine. She also supported her parent’s ministry to the homeless of Boston.Jill was a member of the New Testament Christian Church in Framingham.Many pages could be filled detailing the ways Jill served others, but her life is best summed up in the scripture James 2:8, “It is good when you truly obey our Lord’s command, ”You must love and help you neighbors just as much as you love and care for yourself.”Jill's love for the Lord overflowed to all those in her life. Her love for God gave her a heart to serve family, friends and those in need. Jesus said, "they will know you by your love for one another."  We were all truly loved by Jill.She is also survived by her sister, Dianne Roberts of Whitinsville and her brother, Ralph Guisti Jr. and his wife, Cathleen Guisti of Amherst, Mass.She is also survived by her nieces and nephews, Taryn Flagg of Auburn, Brett Deneault of Whitinsville, Jacob Deneault of Whitinsville, Ralph Guisti III of Amherst, Rachel Guisti of Amherst, Timothy Guisti of Amherst and Emily Guisti of Amherst.Her funeral service will be private with burial in Norfolk Cemetery.In lieu of flowers, expressions of sympathy may be made in her memory to LIFE, Box 982000, Fort Worth, TX 76182-8000.

LOWELL- Annette Marie (Gennell) Tontar, a lifelong resident of Lowell, died early Monday morning, October 29th, at the Life Care Center of Merrimack Valley, in Billerica, after a long illness.  She celebrated her ninety-first birthday on July 3rd.  She was married for forty-nine years to Silvio Tontar, MD, who died in November of 1991.  Mrs. Tontar was born in Lowell, a daughter of the late Charles and Sophie (Carrier) Gennell.  She was graduated from Presentation of Mary Academy in Hudson, NH, in 1934, and from Rivier College, in 1938.  She was a member of one of the first graduating classes at Rivier and was also one of the oldest living graduates.While teaching at Saint Jeanne d’Arc Elementary School, she pursued graduate studies in French literature at Boston University.  After her marriage in February of 1942, Mrs. Tontar worked in the defense industry while her husband served in the Pacific theater.She was an avid reader and movie enthusiast and enjoyed trips to museums, the mountains and the seashore.  Mrs. Tontar established the Dr. Silvio Tontar Family Scholarship at Merrimack College to assist students pursuing pre-medical studies.Surviving her are a daughter:  Silvia Tontar, of Lowell; a son:  Charles Tontar, and his wife Candace Mitchell, of Newburyport; a beloved grandson:  Darien, of Newburyport; a cousin:  Esther Rousseau, and a sister-in-law:  Elsa Chiapolino, of Italy.At her request there will be no calling hours.  Friends are invited to her Funeral Mass to be offered on Friday, November 2nd, at 11 AM at the IMMACULATE CONCEPTION CHURCH.  Burial in St. Mary Cemetery, Tewksbury.  In lieu of flowers, donations in her memory may be made to the Dr. Silvio Tontar Family Scholarship, c/o Merrimack College; 315 Turnpike Street; North Andover, MA 01845.

Doyle, Edward L. "Ted"  of Hingham October 28, 2007.  Husband of 47 years to the late Catherine Hogan Doyle. Loving father of Katherine Guenther & husband Hector of Pelham Manor, NY, Elizabeth Saltzman & husband David of NYC, Martha Schwartz & husband George of Hingham, Ted Doyle of Newton, and Ann Kane & husband George of Lexington. Brother of the late William, John, and Mary Doyle. Also survived by 13 grandchildren.  A retired insurance executive, Mr. Doyle was until recently an active lay Permanent Deacon of the Archdiocese of Boston, a ministry that he pursued for more than 30 years.  He was a member of the first class of Permanent Roman Catholic Deacons, ordained by the Archdiocese of Boston in 1976.  Mr. Doyle served first at St. Paul Parish in Hingham, and later at St. Mary's Parish in Hull, where he was a fixture in the local religious community for more than two decades. In 1979, Mr. Doyle was chosen by the Archdiocese to assist Pope John Paul II at his historic mass held on the Boston Common, which drew nearly a half-million worshippers.   Mr. Doyle was formerly a professional actor and director for the stage, films, and in television, and later a television executive.  He spent his early career at WNDU-TV, the National Broadcasting Company affiliate at Notre Dame University, where he also taught communications to undergraduates.  He later was assistant program manager at WBZ-TV in Boston, and held executive positions at WHO-TV in Des Moines, IA, and WKGB-TV (now WLVI) in Boston.  His love of musical theater made him well-known in the local community as a volunteer director of amateur productions at Notre Dame Academy in Hingham and elsewhere. Mr. Doyle served in the U.S. Naval Air Force during World War II, received an undergraduate degree from Villanova University, and earned a Masters Degree at Catholic University in Washington DC.  Relatives and friends are respectfully invited to attend the funeral from the Pyne Keohane Funeral Home, 21 Emerald St. (off Central St.), HINGHAM, Saturday at 9 AM. Funeral Mass in Saint Paul's Church, Hingham at 10 AM.  Visiting hours Friday 2-4 & 7-9 PM.  Burial in Saint Paul's Cemetery, Hingham.  In lieu of flowers, donations in memory of Deacon Doyle may be made to the St. Paul Parish Building Fund, 147 North St., Hingham, MA 02043.

Prior to retirement she was an office manager for a medical practice in New Bedford.  A Communicant of St. George Church, Westport, where she served as a lector and as past president of the Ladies Women’s Guild. Mrs. Caron received the Marian Medal Award in 1973, she was honored as Woman of the Year by the Diocesan Council of Catholic Women and also received the Top Hat Award from the St. Vincent DePaul Society, Fall River District.  She was a member of the Children of Mary Sodality of Holy Rosary Church in Taunton.  She enjoyed reading and spending time with her family. Including her husband, she is survived by 2 sons: John P. Caron of Freetown and Michael J. Caron of Dartmouth; a daughter: Christine E. Ferreira of Somerset; a brother: Benjamin Wisz of Northport, FL; a sister: Evelyn Harrop of Cumberland, RI; 2 grandchildren: Jessica Caron and Justin Caron; and several nieces and nephews. She was sister of the late William Wisz, Walter Wisz, Jenney Stanizk and Stella Galipeau. Funeral to which relatives and friends are invited will be Friday, November 2, 2007 from the Potter Funeral Home at 8:30 A.M. followed by a Mass of Christian Burial at St. George Church, Westport at 10:00 A.M. Visitation will be Thursday 2 - 4 & 6 - 8 P.M. Interment will be in St. Joseph’s Cemetery, Taunton. Flowers are acceptable or memorial donations may be made to: Memorial Fund of St. George Church , 12 Highland Ave., Westport, MA 02790.

Clifton A. Jacob of Carver, and formerly of Abington died at the Jordan Hospital on October 28, 2007. He was 85 years old. Mr. Jacob was born, raised, and educated in Abington. After his discharge from the Army he returned to Abington and joined the Abington Fire Department, and was a lieutenant. It was also during this time that he started his own construction company that he ran for over 25 years. Mr. Jacob also worked in real estate after his retirement from construction. Mr. Jacob served on the Abington finance board, and personal board, and was on the Abington American Legion board of trustees. He was a member of the Abington Lions club, and was a charter member of the S. Carver Lions Club. Mr. Jacob also loved to vacation in Vermont. Mr. Jacob is survived by his Wife of 42 years, Merilyn Jacob. Loving Father of Richard A. Jacob of Hanson, Carole Best of NH, Larry Jacob of Duxbury, Don Jacob of Carver, Lyn Curtin of Halifax, Clifton A. Jacob Jr. of Weymouth, and Jon K. Jacob of Abington. He is the Grandfather of 19, and Great Grandfather of 25. He is also survived by several nieces and nephews. Visiting hours will be held on Thursday, November 1st from 4-7:00 PM at the Shepherd Funeral Home, 116 Main St. (Rt. 58) in Carver. A Funeral mass will be held on Friday, November 2nd at Our Lady of the Lourdes church in Carver at 10:00 AM. Family and friends are welcome to gather at the funeral home at 9:00 AM prior to the mass. Burial will be at a later date. In lieu of flowers donations may be made to: Our Lady of the Lourdes Building Fund, PO Box 68, Carver, MA 02330. Visit www.shepherdfuneralhome.com for condolences and directions.

Robin Jane Spencer of Pembroke, Maine, recently of Mystic, Connecticut died Saturday, October 27th at Mystic Manor. Robin was well-loved in the Mystic area as a bright light in the local restaurant scene. She had been the smile at the door at The Fisherman Restaurant in Groton and later a VIP Host and bartender at Foxwoods Resorts until just before she moved to her home in Maine in early 2006. As a child, Robin enjoyed competing in hunter-jumper shows around New England on a series of beloved horses, and diving competitively each summer.  A true bon vivant, Robin loved good conversation, great music, fabulous food, and all of her remarkable friends. She kept a garden every summer and cooking creative and mouth-watering meals for friends and loved ones was her joy, even if she’d just spent the whole day in the kitchen of a restaurant. Robin was a passionate and out spoken person.  She was a generous, thoughtful friend, cousin, and aunt. Robin attended Pine Crest Preparatory School in Fort Lauderdale, Florida and graduated from the Oxford School for Girls in West Hartford in 1970. Edna Smith Bowen Colquitt died peacefully in her sleep on Saturday, October 27, 2007, at her Langdale Place residence in Valdosta.  She was 96.  Funeral services will be held at 11:00 a.m. on Tuesday, October 30, in the Memorial Chapel of Tifton's First Baptist Church.  The service will be conducted by her nephew Dr. John B. Polhill of Kentucky and Dr. Wayne Roe of Tifton with burial to follow in the Bowen family plot in Oak Ridge Cemetery.   Mrs. Colquitt was born on November 9, 1910 in Tifton to the late Enoch Piercel Bowen and Elizabeth Turner Bowen.  She was the youngest and final survivor of three brothers and five sisters.  Mrs. Colquitt graduated from Tifton High School and Bessie Tift College in Forsyth with a degree in speech.  After a summer at Columbia University, she taught High School English in Thomaston, during the early years of the Great Depression.  In Thomaston, she met her future husband, Adrian, whom friends and family knew as “Honey” Colquitt. Mr. and Mrs. Colquitt and their only child, Camille, moved to Tifton in 1948, where Edna became a key community player. She was a longstanding member of the Azalea Garden Club, the Colonial Dames, and the Tallahassee Chapter of the DAR. As a volunteer, she was proudest of her contributions to the First Baptist Church, which she served as a Sunday school teacher for fifty years. Her volunteerism competed with her professionalism when, in 1958, she joined the recreational faculty at ABAC, where she signed on to teach ABAC students every plausible application of a concept which was foreign to many: etiquette.  After teaching, she worked in the public relations department of ABAC until her retirement. Survivors include her daughter, Camille Colquitt Peeples of Valdosta; grandsons Henry William "Bill Peeples, III and his wife, Janine, of Valdosta: John Colquitt Peeples and his wife, Jane, of Valdosta; and great-grandchildren William Benjamin McKinley Peeples, Mary Lawrence Peeples, and Alexandra Bowen Peeples, all of Valdosta and brother-in-law James A. Colquitt and his wife, Betty, of  Marietta.  Mrs. Colquitt also leaves a host of nephews and nieces and their descendants, most of whom knew her well and revere her memory.  Donations may be made to the First Baptist Church in Tifton.

GLADYS (BALTUCCI) MARTIN, age 79, died on Saturday, Oct. 27th, 2007 at the South Shore Hospital in Weymouth.   Born in Boston, she was a lifelong resident of Hingham, graduating from Hingham High School in 1947.  From 1947 to 1969 she performed domestic duties in private homes and nursing homes.  Mrs. Martin then became a social worker with the Massachusetts Welfare Department where she was involved with the Food Stamp Program by interviewing and certifying clients, issuing identification cards, maintaining master files and statistical reports.  She also trained and supervised youth workers on temporary, part time and summer placements. Following the Storm Disaster of 1978, she aided the Scituate Center with certification of applicants for food stamps for the duration of the emergency.She has been an active member of the Dorothy Bradford Chapter #135 Order of the Eastern Star in Hingham since 1980 and served as Worthy Matron from 1990-1991.         She was the wife of the late Leslie I. Martin, and mother of Deborah L. Sirois of Kingston and the late Ronna Lee Sirois.  She was the sister of Donald E. Baltucci of Hingham and the late Barbara Nix.  She also was the step-mother of Deborah A. Clark and Donald G. Martin both of Pembroke, Cheryl L. West of Marshfield and Skip Martin of Kingston.  Also survived by 6 step-grandchildren, 6 step-great grandchildren and several step nieces and nephews.         A Funeral Service will be conducted on Wednesday, Oct. 31st at 11 AM at the Downing Cottage Funeral Chapel, 21 Pond St., Hingham.  Friends may visit at the Downing Chapel on Tuesday from 4-8 PM.  An Eastern Star Service will be conducted by the Dorothy Bradford Chapter at the funeral chapel on Tuesday evening at 7 PM.        In lieu of flowers, donations in her memory may be made to the Dorothy Bradford Chapter #135 Order of the Eastern Star, 12 Edgewood Rd., Scituate, MA 02066.        The interment will be in the Liberty Plain Cemetery, South Hingham.

JEVELI-Edmund S. of Boca Raton, FL, formally of East Boston and Winthrop, Oct 26, 2007. Beloved husband of the late Giacinda (Saggese) Jeveli. Born in East Boston on December 15, 1908. Devoted father of Elaine C. Jeveli and her husband Russell Kraus of Las Cruces, NM. Brother of Teddy Jeveli of Winthrop, and the late Lucia, Evelyn, Armand, and Louis (Lefty). Ed graduated from Tufts in 1934 with a degree in chemistry. He started MIT, but because of the depression, he took part time jobs with the Commonwealth of MA, and at the family restaurant of Jeveli’s in Day Square, East Boston, which was founded by his father in 1924, and is still open today as the oldest family owned restaurant in Boston. Ed worked there in many capacities. Ed then went back to work as an analytical chemist for 5 years for A.S. Campbell Co. He helped develop a plating process leading to pioneering the Watts Bath which led to the Harshaw process. During WWII, he developed a full automatic finishing process for Springfield Armory’s M-1 Garand rifle and a corrosion resistant zinc coating process for 40mm shells for the Pacific Theater. He then went on to work for the Frederick Gumm Co. in the electroplating field for 35 years in western New York and England. Ed married the “girl next door”, Giacinda Saggese, on her birthday, November 29, 1936, in St. John the Evangelist Church in Winthrop. Ed always credited her with his longevity and prosperity. The family would spend wonderful summer vacations fishing the lakes of New Hampshire, Massachusetts and Maine. In 1974, he retired to Boca Raton, raised orchids, and lived near the ocean for 33 years. Ed was real part of what Tom Brokaw called the Greatest Generation. A story that exemplifies Ed was after the war, the company that he worked for was going to lay off personnel. He convinced upper management to cut his pay, and others followed, resulting in no layoffs. I will miss you dad. A Funeral Mass will be held on November 29, 2007, at St John the Evangelist Church at 10am. Relatives and friends invited. Interment will be in Winthrop Cemetery (Belle Isle Section). Funeral arrangements were by the Maurice W. Kirby Funeral Home, WINTHROP. For guestbook, go to www.mauricekirbyfh.com.

For several years, Mrs. Waters helped deliver Christmas baskets and toys to Carney Academy children in New Bedford. She enjoyed spending time with her two grandsons, Pedro and Herbie Ray. A fan of local sports, she had one last special night before her passing, watching the Red Sox win another World Series game with her beloved family at home. She is survived by her husband, Dr. Herbert R. She is also survived by her beloved pet dog Sparki. Her surviving siblings are Antonio, John, Antoinette, and Alice, of Providence, and Theresa, of Philadelphia. She was the sister of the late Joseph Daluz.Funeral from the Saunders-Dwyer Home For Funerals, 495 Park St. New Bedford Tuesday, October 30th at 10 AM.  Funeral Mass at St. Julie Billiart Church at 11 AM.  Burial will follow in the Massachusetts National Cemetery.  Visiting hours Monday, October 29th from 4-8 PM.

Donald F. Cooley, a Needham resident for 55 years died on Friday, October 26th at the Beth Israel Deaconess Hospital in Needham. He was 88 years old. Born in Auburndale, Mr. Cooley was raised in Wellesley and was a graduate of Wellesley High School. He was a US Army veteran of World War II, having served in Germany from 1941 to 1943. Upon being discharged from the Army, he went to work for A.D. Jones Optical in Cambridge. In the late 60’s and early 70’s, A.D. Jones, with Mr. Cooley as Vice President was contracted with NASA to design an instrument to assist the Apollo Space Missions with communications. After leaving A.D. Jones, Mr. Cooley worked for the Meller Company in Providence RI. He retired from there at age 62. Mr. Cooley was an accomplished woodworker. He loved gardening and nature. Mr. Cooley loved being outdoors, he enjoyed salt and freshwater fishing as well as downhill and cross country skiing with his wife. For many years, Mr. and Mrs. Cooley summered on Newfound Lake in New Hampshire. Cooley, Donald F. of Needham, October 26, 2007. The beloved husband for 64 years of Virginia (Fullerton), he was the brother of the late William H. and Arthur N. Cooley. Also survived by several nieces and nephews.  A Funeral Service will be held in the Eaton Funeral Home 1351 Highland Ave Needham on Tuesday, October 30 at 1:00 PM. Relatives and friends are kindly invited to attend. Visiting will be held on Tuesday from 12 noon to 1:00 prior to the service. Interment is private. In lieu of flowers donations in Don’s name may be made to the Needham Fire and Rescue c/o Needham Fire Dept. 88 Chestnut St. Needham MA 02492.

Brayton of Webster; his mother Alberta (Cormier) Travers of Oxford; a brother Albert Plaud of Worcester; a sister Leah Rice of Oxford; 6 grandchildren and several nephews and nieces.  Mr. Plaud was born in Worcester son of Phillip R. Plaud and is predeceased by a son Ernest E. Plaud Jr., two brothers Phillip and Robert Plaud and a sister Louise Barrett. Mr. Plaud worked for the Norton – Saint Gobain Co. in the Abrasives Division for over 25 years retiring in 2002. Thomas S. "Nippy" Noponen of Worcester passed away on October 25, 2007, at his home, surrounded by his loving family. Tom is survived by his wife of 53 years, Frances K. (Cordella) Noponen; daughter, Barbara Demers of Worcester; grandson, James J. Demers and wife Wendy and great-grandson, Joshua J. Demers of Chicopee, MA; sisters, Esther Allen and husband Howie Allen of Worcester, Betty Lampi and husband Rauno Lampi of Arizona; an aunt, Jant Tryon and husband Bill Tryon of Connecticut; along with many nieces and nephews. Tom was born in Worcester, son of Thomas and Bertha Noponen. He is predeceased by his brother, Donald Alden of Florida. Tom graduated from Worcester Boys Trade School where he was a standout athlete. He played on both the football team and was an allstar pitcher on the baseball team. After graduating Tom went on to pitch for the AAA baseball team The Ponies, in Ontario. He rounded out his baseball career after joing the US Army during the Korean War playing at Fort Benning, GA and Fort Hood, TX for two years. During Tom's baseball career he had 4 no hitters credited to his stats. Tom worked 30 plus years as a glazier for Glass Contractors in Worcester until his retirement. His love of sports continued throughout his life as a talented and greatly respected golfer, playing at Wachusett Country Club and later at Green Hill Golf Course. For many years Tom carried a zero handicap and had captured 8 holes in one. In accordance with Tom's wishes he will be cremated. A private memorial service will be held for family members at Greendale Peoples Church. Contributions may be made to The Rev. Ralph E. Marsden Scholarship Fund, C/O Greendale Peoples Church, 25 Francis St., Worcester, MA 01606. The family has selected Nordgren Memorial Chapel, 300 Lincoln St., Worcester, MA 01605 to assist them with arrangements

Marlborough, MARita (Perreault) Sherman, 77, a resident of Marlborough Hills Healthcare Center in Marlborough, died at the Healthcare Center Thursday, October 25, 2007 after a brief illness.She was the wife of the late Richard D. Sherman who died in Nov. 1990. They were married 45 years.She was born in Rochester, NH, the only child of the late Samuel and Gertrude (Shaw) Perreault.She completed her education in the Concord, NH area schools. As an adult she was a longtime resident living in Maynard, Bolton and Marlborough. Rita and her husband Richard enjoyed traveling and cruises. They participated in many area flea markets in Westford, Sturbridge and Grafton. They were active in many local fundraisers, particularly with the Lions Club. They cherished the many lifelong friends whom they met from these activities.She worked as a school bus driver, transporting many students and student athletic teams from the Bolton schools and Nashoba High School to their sports events for many years. She worked for the Maynard Council on Aging and later became the director for the Council on Aging in Stow and Boxborough. She retired in 1993 for the first time and for the past 12 years she was a well known sales associate at Sears in the Solomon Pond Mall in Marlborough, retiring in 2006 due to her illness.She became a resident of Marlborough Hills Healthcare Center in 2007 where she became involved in many of the resident activities, especially enjoying the Tuesday Bazaar and quilting bees activities.She leaves a daughter, Risharda Clark and her husband Michael of Stevens Point, WI; 2 grandsons, Joseph and Jason and a granddaughter Jennifer. She also leaves 2 great grandsons, several nieces and nephews and her beloved cockateel companion of many years, Jiminy, which is in the trusted care of her niece.A mass of Christian Burial will be celebrated Monday, October 29, 2007 at 10:00 a.m. at the Immaculate Conception Church, 19 Prospect Street, Marlborough, MA. Burial will be in South Cemetery, Bolton. Family and friends may attend calling hours Sunday, October 28, from 5:00 to 7:00 p.m. at the Tighe-Hamilton Funeral Home, Inc. (www.tighehamilton.com) 50 Central Street, Hudson, MA 01749. In lieu of flowers, donations may be sent to: UMass Memorial Hospice/Memorial Foundation, PO Box 2795, Worcester, MA 01613-9938

DUDLEY - Henry L. Sroczynski, 74, of 57 New Boston Road, died Thursday, October 25, in Hubbard Regional Hospital due to complications from Kennedy’s Disease. He leaves his wife of 47 years, Pauline M. (Kozlowski) Sroczynski; two daughters, Joyce A., wife of Timothy J. Gergich of Slingerlands, NY, and Elaine E., wife of James A. Norowski of Dudley; five grandchildren, Natalie and Haley Gergich and Benjamin, Aaron and Rebecca Norowski; a nephew and three nieces.   He was born in Webster, the son of Leo A. and Elizabeth R. (Czernicki) Sroczynski. He graduated from Bartlett High School and completed his teacher training at Fitchburgh State College and Westfield State College. He was an Air Force Veteran, serving from 1957 to 1961. Mr. Sroczynski was a carpentry instructor, first at Bartlett Vocational High School in Webster for 14 years and then at Bay Path Technical Vocational High School in Charlton for 21 years, retiring in 1993. In his retirement, he was substitute teacher at Bay Path for ten years. He oversaw the construction of several houses in the area. He also built his own home. Mr. Sroczynski was a communicant of St. Andrew Bobola Church, where he was a collector for many years. He volunteered for the annual parish festival and was chairman for several years. He also called the numbers for the church’s weekly bingo games. He was a member of the Retired Mass. Teacher’s Association and was a former member of the Webster-Dudley American Legion Post #184. He enjoyed wood working, vacationing near the water, and spending time with his children and especially his grandchildren. The funeral will be held Monday, October 29, from the Sitkowski & Malboeuf Funeral Home, 340 School St., Webster, with a Mass at 10:00 AM in St. Andrew Bobola Church, 54 West Main Street. Burial will be in St. Joseph Garden of Peace, Webster. Calling hours are from 4:00 to 8:00 PM Sunday, October 28, at the funeral home. Donations in his name may be made to St. Andrew Bobola Church Elevator Fund, 54 West Main St., PO Box 98, Dudley, MA 01571-0098.

Dale S. Crandall 76, died Thursday October 25th in his Abington home after a period of failing health.  He was the loving husband of Jennie A. He is also survived by 5 grandchildren, 1 great grandchild, and many nieces and nephews. By request of the family all services are private.  Donations may be made in his name to the Old Colony Hospice, One Credit Union Way, Randolph, MA 02368 of to the charity of one’s choice.

William G. Downs III, 84, died Thursday, October 25 at his daughter’s home in Mansfield, Massachusetts. Born in Evansville, Indiana, he completed high school in Greenwich, Connecticut and attended college at the University of Connecticut. He served in the Coast Guard during World War II. He and his wife, Barbara, managed a farm in Cowdersport, Pennsylvania, where he learned to love farming and he later received his Master’s degree in agronomy from Pennsylvania State University. For nearly 30 years, he managed Penn State University’s Southwestern Field Research Laboratory, in Rector, Pennsylvania field testing new varieties of corn, vegetables, and flowers. He organized the annual Chamber of Commerce corn roasts and he was a great supporter of local theater, both on stage and off.  In retirement, he and Barbara moved to Mashpee, MA, where he enjoyed golf, bridge, and tending his home garden.  He was the husband of the late Barbara W. Downs, father of the late William Downs IV, and leaves 4 surviving daughters, Katherine Downs of Washington, DC; Mary Downs of Silver Spring, MD; Martha Downs of Mansfield, MA; and Barbara Tyree of Lebanon, PA. He also leaves a grandson, Luca Medina-Downs of Silver Spring, MD. A memorial service will be held a later date. In lieu of flowers, please make donations in his name to The Arboretum at Penn State, The Pennsylvania State University, 336 Forest Resources Building, University Park PA 16802-3604
